name: Introduction to Life Sciences: Fundamentals of Life
description: Fundamentals of Biochemistry, Genetics, Molecular Biology, and Cell Biology; Structure and Regulation of Genes; Structure and Function of Proteins; How DNA, Proteins, and different units combine together to integrate into cells; How the cells integrate into multicellular systems and organisms; Concepts in population biology: principles of macro- and microevolution, population genetics, and population dynamics; Role of an engineer in Biology

name: Molecular Genetics
description: Structure of nucleic acids, Transcription, Translation, DNA replication, DNA repair and recombination; Gene expression in bacteria and eukaryotes, regulatory sequences, activators, repressors, regulation of transcription factors, elongation and termination of transcription; Post-transcriptional gene control, Processing of pre-mRNA and regulation, transport of mRNA and degradation.

name: Bionanotechnology – Principles and Applications
description: Introduction to Nanotechnology and special focus on Bionanotechnology. Nanotechnology with molecules of life: Proteins-Proteins Structures, Designs, Nanomachines, Origami designs and Applications; Carbohydrates-Glyconanotechnology, Applications in Glycobiology, vaccination, delivery and cell biology; Lipids-Structure, function, designs, membranes and applications in drug delivery formulations and cell biology; Combinatorial Bionanotechnology-Hybrid formulations, structures, functions and applications. DNA Nanotechnology: Structure of DNA- Why DNA is genetic material of life?; Naturally occuring DNA nanomachines; Discovery of DNA nanotechnology - Initial designs and validations; Multi-arm DNA based 1/2/3D nanodevices; DNA Origami; Non-canonical Motifs and applications-Aptamers, i-motif, G-quadruplexes. Applications of DNA Nanotechnology: Cell Engineering; Theranostics Diagnostics (Biosensing) and therapeutic applications; Biomedical Applications-Bioimaging including super-resolution imaging and single-particle tracking; DNA based computing and robotics.

name: Introduction to Biomedical Engineering
description: This course will provide an introductory overview of the multi-disciplinary field of biomedical engineering. We will discuss applications of elementary engineering principles to the analyses of physiological systems for addressing challenges in health care. The course topics will include the following: 1. Biomedical engineers and modern health care systems: Addressing unmet clinical needs, interfacing with clinicians, research, and development 2. Overview of anatomy and physiology: Cellular organization, tissues, major organ systems, communication systems, respiration, digestion, circulation, removal of molecules, homeostasis 3. Biomechanics: Basic mechanics (elasticity, viscosity, deformation and viscoelasticity), mechanics of tissues (e.g., cartilage, ligaments, tendon, muscle), physics and mechanics of locomotion (e.g., walking, running, swimming), design in biomechanics and orthopedics 4. Biomaterials: Materials in medicine, lessons from nature on biomaterial design and selection, tissue-biomaterial interactions, safety testing 5. Tissue engineering: Challenges in organ transplantation, cell culture, regulation of healing processes, replacement of diseased tissues, tissue engineering in control of drug delivery, artificial organs 6. Biosignals: Acquiring and analyzing biosignals, electrocardiogram (ECG), electroencephalography (EEG), electromyogram (EMG), thermography, blood flow analysis 7. Brief overview of imaging: Optical microscopy, X-Rays, computed tomography, magnetic resonance, ultrasound, and nuclear medicine 8. Medical devices: Classes of medical devices, considerations for design and development, introduction to regulatory processes in Indian, U.S., and European markets, case studies from India and abroad 9. Bioethics: Animal and human experimentation, informed consent, euthanasia, ethical issues in feasibility studies, ethical clearance

name: Origins of Species
description: Hierarchical organization of life, the concept of species; Morphological, phenotypic and molecular bases of species; Concept of species in asexually and sexually reproducing organisms; Genomes and species; Population sizes, genetic diversity within species; genetic intermixing of closely related species, positive and negative effects of hybrids between different species; Habitats, ecological niche separation, adaption and selection; Reproductive barriers and roles of sex chromosomes; Reproductive compatibilities and incompatibilities; Classical genetic techniques to study speciation; classical and modern molecular mechanisms of speciation.

name: Methods in Biology
description: Basic laboratory techniques that are an integral part of a Biological Engineering program will be explored in detail via laboratory experiments. This is a laboratory course in which we will conduct experiments in Molecular biology including PCR, restriction enzyme digestion, gel electrophoresis, cloning, ligation, transformation, Protein expression & purification in prokaryotic systems (e.g. affinity chromatography, size exclusion chromatography) and characterization of proteins by SDS- PAGE, Western blotting.

name: Stem Cells: Science and Applications
description: 1. Introduction to Stem Cells: Basic overview of stem cells--what they are, the history of stem cell research, and the potential for stem cell therapies. Stem cells are found throughout the tree of life. 2. How do Stem Cells work: How to induce stem cells to differentiate into particular cell types? New stem cell-assisted technologies such as mitochondrial replacement therapy and generating chimeras for organ transplantation. Background information on the science and ethics of cloning, gene editing methods with special focus on gene editing in stem cells. Combining genome editing and stem cell engineering as link that explores the use of stem cells to understand disease and the use of stem cells as therapeutics. 3. Using Stem Cells to study Diseases: How cultured cells can be used to understand the cause of diseases and to look for drugs that will potentially cure them. We will also explore how stem cells have the potential to help with the study of diseases like hematological disorders, diabetes and Alzheimer's. 4. Using Stem Cells to Treat Diseases: Explore the potential for using stem cells in our own bodies (adult stem cells) to treat hematological disorders, age-related macular degeneration, neuronal-degeneration and like diseases. Some tools for evaluating potential stem cell treatments will be covered in details. 5. The Bioethics of Stem Cell Research and Therapy: Discuss some of the controversies surrounding human embryonic stem cells and the legal, policy, and ethical challenges associated with the use of stem cells in basic and clinical research. Discussion about the future of reproductive technology and stem cell research.

name: Genetic Engineering – Principles and Applications
description: Module a. Introduction to genetic engineering + Fundamentals of molecular and cell biology Brief Introduction to the course and area of genetic engineering, Transcription, Translation, DNA replication and Recombination. Module b. Introduction to Recombinant DNA technology Polymerase Chain Reaction, Fundamental techniques of gene manipulation, Basic principles of gene cloning, Vectors for gene cloning, Purification of DNA from living cells, Isolation of desired Gene, Manipulation of purified DNA. Module c. Introduction of DNA into living cells Transgenics, chimeras, somatic and germ-line gene delivery, site directed mutagenesis, therapeutic reverse mutations, production of recombinant proteins in E.coli and other organisms. Module d: Principles of Gene editing CRISPR-Cas9 technology, genetic manipulation of microbes, plants and animals. Module e: Applications of gene cloning and DNA analysis in Biotechnology Gene cloning and DNA analysis in Medicine, Gene cloning and DNA analysis in Agriculture, Gene Cloning and DNA analysis in Forensic science and Archaeology, Ethical issues and concerns related to Genetically modified organisms.

name: Introduction to Plant Biotechnology
description: Module a. Introduction to Origin of plant biotechnology History and development of plant biotechnology Module b. Introduction to laboratory organization and techniques in tissue culture Laboratory organization methods and criteria; introduction to plant tissue culture; plasticity and totipotency; nutrition medium, sterilization techniques; cytodifferentiation; organogenic differentiation; types of cultures: seed culture, embryo culture, callus culture, organ culture, cell culture, protoplast culture; Micropropogation, generation of virus free plants; plant regeneration (somatic embryogenesis); cell suspension and secondary metabolites; invitro production of haploids (anther culture, microspore culture Module c. Special techniques in plant biotechnology Protoplast isolation; somatic hybridization; embryo rescue; somaclonal variation; germplasm storage and cryopreservation Module d: Transgenic technology for crop improvement Recombinant DNA technology; strategies for cloning genes from plants; gene transfer in plants; transgenics in crop improvement; current state of transgenic crops and GM crop regulation Module e. Beyond genetically modified crops Genome editing; genetic mapping; insertional mutagenesis and tilling; functional genomics.

name: Systems Biology
description: Introduction to mathematical modeling of biological systems- Networks in biological systems- Reconstruction of networks from experimental data, Network motifs and their role in autoregulation, feed forward loops. Specific biological networks and their modeling- Transcription networks- Activators, Repressors, Input functions and Dynamics; Metabolic networks- Rate laws, Flux Analysis, Signal transduction networks- Static models and dynamic models. Robustness in biological circuits- Principle of robustness with examples of robustness in biological systems, detailed analysis of bacterial chemotaxis as robust biochemical system, Stochasticity in biological processes- introduction to stochastic modeling. Design of biological systems- Metabolic Engineering, Case Studies- synthetic biology, Emerging topics in systems biology- precision medicine, biomarker discovery using systems biology

name: Introduction to Cell Biology
description: Module a. Origin of Cell Biology History and development of Cell Biology; Invention of microscopes; Discovery of cells; Cell theory; Membrane and bulk phase theories; Steady-state membrane pump concept Module b. Cell structure and organization Cell as a unit of life, prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, difference between plant and animal cells, bio-membranes, Structure and functions of various cell organelles i.e., nucleus, ribosome, ER, golgi, lysosomes, peroxisome, exosomes, vacuoles, chloroplasts, cytoskeleton; Organization of tissues: Cell-cell and cell matrix interactions, cell adhesion molecules, composition of extracellular matrix, cellular junctions and role; Cellular movement: types of movement, cilia and flagella, muscle contraction, role of cytoskeletal proteins in movement Module c. How cells obtain energy from food Glycolysis, Citric acid cycle, Energy generation in mitochondria and chloroplasts, oxidative phosphorylation, electron transport, photosynthesis Module d: Cell cycle, Control of Cell Numbers and Cell Size G1, G2, S and M phase of the cell cycle, Cell cycle analysis and its applications, Programed cell death apoptosis vs necrosis, Mitogens stimulating cell division, growth factors Module e: Transport systems in cells Osmosis, active and passive transport, protein transporters, ion channels, protein sorting, vesicular transport, secretory pathways, endocytic pathways, exocytosis, micropinocytosis, role of cytoskeletal proteins in transport, molecular motors Module f: Cell signaling Principles of cell signaling, Concept or receptor-ligand, Signalling molecules, Receptor types: ion-channel-coupled receptor, G-protein Receptor, Enzyme coupled receptors Module g: Techniques used in cell biology and relevance of cell biology Histology, staining, microscopy, TEM and SEM, live cell imaging, Fluorescent dyes and genetically encoded fluorescent tags, cell culture, FACS; Understanding Cancer, Stem cells and application, Tissue engineering, Infectious diseases, manipulating cells for therapeutic purposes

name: Applied Biological Engineering
description: Advanced themes that are an integral part of a modern Biological Engineering graduate program will be explored in detail. A representative list of topics is given below: Nucleic acid in disease diagnosis: Detection of TB and Malaria by PCR, diagnosis of HIV by qPCR, monitoring flu evolution and hereditary genetic tests. Antibiotic resistance: Prevalence and development of resistance in microbes. New approaches to identification and management of resistance. Recombinant DNA technologies: Cloning vectors, mutagenesis methods, manipulation of gene expression in prokaryotes, introduction to eukaryotic gene expression systems. DNA forensics: Sample collection, DNA extraction methods, DNA database searches, role of bioinformatics, low level and degraded DNA detection. Waste water treatment and microbes: Aerobic and anaerobic oxidation, role of activated sludge, microbial ecology of a water treatment plant, genetically engineered microbes. Biotechnology and clean energy: Renewable sources of energy, biofuel production by microbes, hydrogen producing biological systems and challenges. Protein Engineering: Protein folding and recognition for protein design principles, Structure- Based Combinatorial Protein Engineering (SCOPE), proteomics. Cancer Diagnostics: Diagnostics Imaging; X-rays, CAT-scan (computerized axial tomography), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), ultrasound. Tumor Markers Microarrays in Cancer Diagnostics, Mass Spectrometry in Cancer Diagnostics.

name: Biophysical Techniques
description: Molecular structure determination: Part A: X-ray crystallography, the single most important technique used in determining the 3-D structure of macromolecules: Structural elucidation of proteins, Nucleic acids (DNA). Crystallographic theory, exercises on crystallization, data processing and model building (Docking, related software). Part B: Biophysical techniques are covered that supplement the 3-D characterization of biological macromolecules: NMR (Amino Acids, Mononucleotides, Proteins and Nucleic Acids) and mass spectroscopy (Fragmentation studies: A tool for structural elucidation of proteins).

name: Cellular signaling
description: Importance of cellular communication illustrated using some classic pathways (GPCR, Ras- MAPK etc) as examples; How signal transduction cascades are affected by disease states (various cancers, cardiovascular problems etc); Drug discovery (in the context of signaling pathways) wherein knowledge of the players of a specific signaling pathway has helped design drugs ( Eg: Gleevac)

name: Neurophysiological Basis of Movement
description: Muscle Physiology, Motor units and electromyography, Voluntary control of a single muscle, Voluntary control of single joint movements, Cortical and subcortical control, Ascending and Descending Pathways, Control and coordination of multijoint movements, Constraints on motor control: neurological impairments, Motor learning and recovery of function, Rehabilitation approaches

name: Molecular and Cellular Biotechnology
description: Topics covered in the course will cover fundamentals of molecular & cell biology including DNA replication, DNA recombination and repair, transcription, translation, gene regulation, post-transcriptional and post-translational processes, cellular transport as well as the basics of recombinant DNA technologies, PCR, cloning, mutagenesis, Sanger sequencing, heterologous expression systems.

name: Molecular Basis of Neurodegenerative Diseases
description: Overview of neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) including Alzheimer’s Disease (AD), Parkinson’s Disease (PD), Huntington’s Disease (HD), Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) and prion disease; Pathological events in human neurodegeneration; Genetic variation in inherited and sporadic disease; Role of pathogenic proteins and their aggregation, Inter and intracellular transmission of toxic species including Prion mechanism; Other prevalent disease mechanism hypotheses: Abnormal post- translational modifications, ubiquitin proteasome system (UPS) impairment, autophagy dysfunction, ,oxidative stress and inflammation, microbial infection; Development of therapeutic approaches including genome editing methods: latest trends and future perspective

name: Molecular Oncology
description: Nature of a cancer cell, similarities and differences from a normal cell; Molecular biology of cell growth, cycling and proliferation in oncogenic transformation (oncogenes and TSGs); Experimental and spontaneous cell transformation; Genetic and Epigenetic bases of cancer; Cancer initiating/stem cells; Genomic and epigenomic integrity vis-a-vis tumorigenesis; Stress and cancer cell survival (senescence, apoptosis and other forms of cell death in tumor environment); Cell immortality and tumorigenesis; Cellular and animal models of cancer.

name: Biological Physics
description: Biology by numbers: Size and geometry of cells and organelles, concentration of cellular building blocks, Energy currencies; Cellular processes at rest: Mechanical and Chemical Equilibrium of biomolecules, Entropy considerations, Random walks and the structure of Macromolecules, Electrostatics of intra and extracellular salty solutions, Biological membranes; Dynamics of cellular processes: A statistical view of biological dynamics, Water and diffusion, Rate equations, dynamics of molecular motors, Light and Electricity in biological systems; Organization of Biological Networks; Biological Patterns in space and time.

name: Biochemistry
description: Biomolecules: Chemical bonding, structure and function of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids, water and its properties. Synthesis, separation and purification: biochemistry of cell membrane, isomerism, subcellular fractions and their identification, protein separation and purification from different host system, disorders of carbohydrate and lipid metabolism, peptide synthesis, protein synthesis and post-translation modifications and it importance, protein engineering, protein lithography and its applications. Enzymes and their kinetics: enzymes, enzyme kinetics and enzyme regulation, enzyme engineering and its applications. Metabolism disorders: Inborn errors of metabolism, digestive diseases, disorders of liver and kidney, principles of thermodynamics, bioenergetics and oxidative phosphorylation. Blood clotting, electrolytes and acid-base balance, biological oxidation and muscle contraction. Nucleic acids: Structure and function of nucleotides, primary, secondary and tertiary structure of nucleic acids, DNA and RNA forms and conformations and their biological significance, denaturation and renaturation of DNA and RNA, in vivo and in vitro synthesis of DNA and RNA and their applications.

name: Biostatistics
description: The course is aimed at providing an understanding of statistical techniques used to analyze biological and clinical data. The following topics will be covered: 1. Making sense of and comparing biological datasets (Probability distributions, description of a population, measures of central tendency and dispersion, hypothesis testing, analysis of variance, nonparametric methods, correlation, and regression) 2. Rates and standardization (Rates, Methods for standardization of rates, cross- sectional vs longitudinal studies, person-time observations, Inference on person- time data) 3. Life tables (Computation of life tables, applications of life tables, years of potential life lost) 4. Survival Analysis (life table method, product-limit method, log-rank test) 5. Clinical Trials (randomization, sample size calculation for clinical trials, statistical power, inference on proportions) 6. Genetic Epidemiology (Twin studies, association studies, genome-wide association studies, Manhattan plots, rare variant studies) 7. Risk (risk prediction, additive value of biomarkers, net reclassification index, integrated discrimination improvement) 8. Scale construction and measurement (reliability, validity, responsiveness) 9. Measures of performance of diagnostic tests in clinical practice (Receiver operating characteristic curves, sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, positive and negative likelihood ratios) 10. Common pitfalls and ethics in biostatistics reporting 11. Ethics in biological research (dealing with biological/human samples, research integrity, informed consent, safety, privacy and data monitoring, rigor, reproducibility)

name: Bioinformatics and Computational Biology
description: Biological Data- origin (experimental, such as genome sequencing, macromolecular structure determination; and theoretical, such as structure prediction, mathematical modeling), type, representation, analysis and storage. Biological sequence analysis- databases- querying and retrieval, alignment- pairwise and multiple, Hidden Markov models, Phylogenetic trees- Bayesian and Maximum likelihood methods, RNA sequence and structure. Genome sequencing, Introduction to proteomics, transcriptomics and metagenomics Going 3D- Protein structure, function and evolution, databases (query and retrieval), alignment, protein structure prediction (secondary and tertiary)- conventional and recent deep learning methods, coevolution, protein folding, Introduction to in-silico drug discovery. Introduction to systems biology- biological networks- protein interaction networks, regulatory networks. Basics of biological computing- Python, Biopython and R- specific only to biological data analysis.

name: Human Physiology
description: environment, cellular homeostasis, Transmembrane transport, active and passive.

name: Novel Drug Delivery Technologies: Fundamental Principles and Engineering
description: Overview of drug delivery: Current landscape of therapeutics and medicinal products. Challenges in the development of new therapeutics. Processes of conventional drug delivery, dosage forms of drugs, delivery routes, biological barriers (Skin, systemic, and local) and toxicity. Novel Drug Delivery Technologies: Comprehensive overview of novel drug delivery technologies. Evolution of drug delivery technologies. Mechanisms of drug release (Drug release kinetics: Diffusion, degradation, bioerosion, swelling). Types of novel drug delivery technologies (Controlled drug delivery technologies, targeted drug delivery technologies (Passive and active, bio-responsive drug delivery technologies (pH, enzyme, and light), progress (Intra-articular drug delivery systems, local depots, and local drug therapies for bacterial infections), and promises. Wrap-up: Translational and clinical aspects of drug delivery technologies. Present and future advancements of drug delivery technologies. Development process of novel drug delivery technologies from concept to market. Regulatory, intellectual property consideration, industry and start-up opportunities.

name: Molecular Biotechnology
description: Molecules and processes in Biotechnology: DNA and replication, RNA and transcription, proteins and translation, DNA Recombination and repair, gene regulation, post-transcriptional and post-translational processes; Polymerase Chain Reaction, Nucleic acid sequencing, Genome-scale libraries (cDNA, RNAi, CRISPR-sgRNA etc). Case studies highlighting the applications of biotechnology in medicine, agriculture, forensic science, Archaeology and Evolutionary Biology; Genetic Engineering: Molecular cloning, generation of recombinant DNA, DNA transfer into living cells, Protein expression systems, purification of recombinant proteins, transgenics; Genetic manipulation: mutagenesis methods, targeted genome engineering, genome-scale screens. Lab module: Lab sections will be designed to sync with the lectures. Some core components include PCR, electrophoresis, restriction digestion, cloning by ligation, bacterial transformation and recombinant protein expression

name: Cellular Communication
description: Membrane Transport: Active Transport, pumps, ion channels; Intracellular Transport: Transport of molecules between nucleus and cytosol, into mitochondria and chloroplasts, Peroxisomes, Endoplasmic Reticulum, Protein sorting; Vesicular Traffic: Transport from ER through Golgi, from Trans Golgi Network to Lysosomes, Endocytosis, Exocytosis; Cellular communication: signal transduction cascades(GPCR, Ras-MAPK, nuclear receptors etc.) important for development, differentiation, immune and hormonal response, cell proliferation and disease (cancer, cardiovascular disorders etc)

name: Physical Biology of the Cell
description: Biology by the numbers, Temporal scales of Biological Phenomenon, Mechanical and Chemical Equilibrium in cells, Entropy considerations for Biological processes, Two state systems, Random walk view of biomacromolecules, Electrostatics of intra and extracellular salty solutions, Architecture of cells and cytoskeleton, A statistical view of biological dynamics, Diffusion, Molecular crowding in cells, Rate equations, dynamics of molecular motors, Light and Electricity in biological systems, patterns in living systems.

name: Peptide Technologies
description: Physiochemical properties of amino acids and peptides; Chemistry of amide bond formation; Solid Phase Peptide Synthesis: Resins, Protecting groups, Coupling reagents, Deprotection mixtures, Cleavage cocktails; Assembly of large peptide sequences: Fragment assembly, Native Chemical Ligations and Expressed Protein Ligation; Analytical Methods for Peptides: UV-Vis, CD, FTIR, Liquid Chromatography and Mass Spectrometry.

name: Applied Stem Cells Technologies
description: Topics include: Introduction to Stem Cells: Definition, History, and Classification; Embryonic Stem Cells: Derivation, Properties, and Applications; Adult Stem Cells: Types (Hematopoietic, Mesenchymal, Neural), Niches, and Uses; Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs): Reprogramming and Applications; Stem Cell Culture and Differentiation Techniques; Gene Editing Technologies in Stem Cells; Preclinical Models of Stem Cell Therapies; Stem Cells in Regenerative Medicine (e.g., cardiac, neural, musculoskeletal applications); Stem Cells in Oncology and Immunotherapy; Stem Cells in Autoimmune and Genetic Disorders; Clinical Trials: Design, Phases, and Case Studies; Regulatory Pathways and Ethical Considerations (FDA, EMA, ISSCR Guidelines), Future directions

name: Biocomputing
description: Review of linux; Shell scripting; Notebooks; Version control Accessing biological databases using APIs (NCBI, Uniprot, PDB etc.) Biopython; Biological Sequence analysis; Protein Structural modeling using Colab fold. Python and R studio for Biological data analysis and plotting; Bioconductor; Case studies- Next generation sequencing analysis; Differential expression analysis;

name: Protein Biologics: From Concept to Clinic
description: Foundations of Protein Biologics: Introduction to Biologics and Biopharmaceuticals, Protein Structure, Function, and Dynamics, Immunogenicity of protein biologics, Recombinant DNA Technology for Protein Production; Design and Discovery of Protein Biologics: Classes of Protein Biologics, Target Identification and Validation, Protein Engineering and Rational Design; Upstream and Downstream Processing: Cell Culture and Fermentation, Protein Purification, Protein Characterization and Quality Control; Pre-clinical and Clinical Development: Formulation Development, Pre-clinical Studies, Clinical Trials, Regulatory Affairs and Quality by Design (QbD), Emerging Trends & Case Studies

name: AI Applications in Life Sciences
description: Biological & Biomedical Data Landscape, Introduction to AI and its role in modern biology; AI in Genomics, Proteomics and Precision Medicine; AI in Drug Discovery & Chemoinformatics, AI in Protein and Biotherapeutic Design; AI in Diagnostics and Clinical Research; AI in Smart Agriculture; Ethics and Regulation, Emerging Trends and Case Studies of AI applied to Life Sciences

name: Metabolic Engineering
description: Overview of cellular metabolism and quantitative concepts- thermodynamics of metabolic reactions, stoichiometry etc., Regulation of metabolic pathways; Reconstruction of Metabolic Networks, Metabolic Flux Analysis- Introduction and applications, Metabolic Control Analysis; Omics tools for Metabolic Engineering, Computational tools and software; Metabolic pathway engineering-metabolic balancing, optimization strategies, yield optimization, case studies- artemisinin, ethanol production; Machine learning and AI in metabolic engineering

name: Methods in Structural Biology
description: Introduction to Biomolecular X-ray Crystallography; Overview of NMR, Cryo-EM as integral part of macromolecular structure solution approach; Principles and challenges of biomolecular crystallography, Planning and executing a biomolecular structures elucidation; Biomolecular structures and functions, Methods of structure determination: Molecular Replacement (MR), Single wavelength Anomalous Diffraction (SAD), Multiple-wavelength Anomalous Diffraction (MAD), Software program usage in structure analysis, electron charge density interpretation and model building, analysis and description of the structure, Ramachandran plot and pro-check; Hands-on practice in using Macromolecular crystallography software

name: Transport Phenomenon in Biological Systems
description: Review of fundamentals of transport phenomena in biological systems: Mass conservation in microscopic & macroscopic systems, equation of continuity, shell balance approach, Momentum flux, fluid flow types, rheology in biological fluids, shear stress and shear rate, Newton’s law of viscosity, non-Newtonian fluids, boundary layers, properties of biological fluids, Steady state diffusion, unsteady state diffusion, pseudo steady state approximation, Thermal energy flux and equation of energy, temperature profile in a tissue, unsteady-state heat conduction; Transvascular Transport: Pathways for transendothelial transport: Continuous, fenestrated, and discontinuous capillaries, Rates of transvascular transport: Osmotic pressure, rate of fluid flow, and solute transport; Transport of gases between blood and tissues, Oxygen-hemoglobin equilibria and binding kinetics, dynamics of oxygenation of blood in lungs, Krogh cylinder model of oxygen transport in tissues, Nitric Oxide production and transport in tissues, Discrete capillary models and continuum porous approximations; Drug transport in solid tumors, Drug delivery in cancer, routes of drug administration, Quantitative analysis of transvascular transport, interstitial fluid transport, and interstitial transport of solutes; Mass and Heat Transport for Cell and Organ Preservation: Temperature dependence of diffusion and permeability, concentration changes during freezing

name: Undergraduate Science Laboratory
description: Experiments in the Science Laboratory: The experiments encompass concepts from various fields of chemistry, such as organic synthesis, environmental chemistry, forensic sciences, polymer science, renewable energy, and electrochemistry. Physics experiments cover broadly the concepts related to various UG physics courses, like Mechanics, Electromagnetic theory, Optics, Electronics and Atomic physics. Chemistry:Synthesis of Aspirin, a popular OTC drug, Removal of organic pollutant from water using UV light, Synthesis of a commercial polymer, Detection of pollutant gasses, Fingerprint detection, Recycling of solid waste, Organic photocatalysis, Water splitting experiment; Physics:Fraunhofer diffraction, Spectrometer, Operational amplifier, LCR circuit, Study of emission lines of hydrogen, Hall effect, Normal modes of a coupled pendulum, Magnetic susceptibility measurements.

name: Nanoscale Science
description: Surfaces at the Nanoscale – Particle shape and the surface (exterior surface area, interior surface area); Surface and volume (specific surface area, spherical cluster approximation); Structural magic numbers; Particle orientation (effect of size on surface polarization, quasi-static limit, effect of orientation of nanoscale objects); Surface energy (fraction of surface atoms, energy of surface atoms; lowering of surface energy); Chemical interactions at nanoscale; Surface reactivity and catalysis; Surface stabilization (electrical double layer, surfactants and microemulsions, Ostwald ripening, sintering); Synthesis and Assembly at the Nanoscale – Gibb’s energy at nanoscale; Bottom-up approaches – gaseous phase (chemical vapor deposition, atomic layer deposition, epitaxy, ion implantation, combustion, thermal decomposition), liquid phase (molecular self- assembly, supramolecular chemistry, sol gel synthesis, electrolytic and electroless deposition), and template synthesis(microporous template materials, mesoporous template materials); Top- down approaches – mechanical , thermal, high energy, chemical, and lithographic; Self-assembly for synthesizing hierarchical nanomaterials (assembling nanosized components); Societal Implications of Using Nanomaterials – Overview of how nanomaterials are being applied (select examples from photonics, plasmonics, energy harvesting and storage, environmental remediation, devices); Ethical implications; Legal implications; Environmental implications (nanotoxicology, risk assessment); Public perception; Future trends.

name: Sustainability and Environment
description: Special topics inf the form of case studies will be discussed related to: Introduction to sustainability: humanity and environment, the evolution of environmental policy, climate and global change, climate processes: external and internal controls, modern climate change, climate projections, biosphere, soil and sustainability, biodiversity and ecosystem functions, physical resources: water, pollution, minerals, environmental and resource economics, modern environmental management, systems of waste management, sustainable energy systems, sustainable infrastructure, embodied energy, life cycle, sustainable materials and construction, problem solving and tools of sustainability

name: Geospatial Engineering
description: Introduction, coordinate systems and datum Projection systems, Georeferencing; Spatial data models and data structures; Attribute data input and management; Data editing, exploration, and analysis; Spatial Interpolation; Digital terrain analysis using digital elevation model (DEM) data; Watershed analysis; Network analysis; Concept of Geodatabase; Google Earth Engine; SURVEYING: Introduction to Surveying, Types of land surveys; Instruments, Topographic maps and its interpretation, Measurements and Errors; Types of Errors; Precision and Accuracy. Drone Surveys; Concepts of GPS; GPS receivers; GPS positioning; GPS accuracy and error sources, Applications of GPS, Applications of Auto Level, Digital Level, Manual and Robotic Total Stations

name: Soil Mechanics
description: Phase relations; Soil classification, index properties, grain-size distribution; Effective stress principle; Flow through porous media, Darcy’s law, permeability, different heads, 2-D Seepage and flow nets; Compaction characteristics; Compressibility and Consolidation characteristics, 1-D compression response, Terzaghi’s theory of consolidation, secondary consolidation; Settlement of foundations, immediate and time-dependent settlement, allowable settlement; Shear-strength of Soil, Mohr- coulomb failure criteria, direct shear and UC tests; in situ test – SPT, CPT; Earth-pressure theory, Coulomb and Rankine approaches; Bearing capacity, failure modes, generalized bearing capacity equation, net- and gross bearing capacity, allowable bearing pressure.

name: Structural Analysis
description: Degrees of indeterminacy (flexibility & stiffness); Trusses (including types of trusses), beams and frames: determinate and indeterminate structures, cables and arches; moment area theorem; conjugate beam method; principle of virtual work; energy method; Castigliano’s theorems; unit-load and unit-displacement theorems; reciprocal theorems; Betti's and Maxwell's theorem; method of consistent deformations; slope-deflection method; displacement based methods; influence lines; Muller-Breslau's principle; moment distribution method; column analogy method; Introduction to matrix method. Introduction to using structural analysis software for the analysis of simple structures/structural components.

name: Geospatial Engineering
description: Introduction to Surveying, Types of land surveys; Instruments, Topograhpic maps and its interpretation, Measurements and Errors; Units; Types of Errors; Precision and Accuracy; Error Propagation. Concepts of GPS; GPS receivers; GPS positioning mode- point positioning & relative positioning (DGPS & RTK GPS); GPS accuracy and error sources, Applications of GPS. GIS: Introduction, Coordinate systems and datum Projection systems; Spatial data models and data structures; Attribute data input and management; Data editing, exploration and analysis; Digital terrain analysis using DEM data, Path analysis, network applications and watershed analysis.

name: Concrete Design
description: Introduction: Properties of concrete and reinforcing steel, design philosophies, limit state, ultimate load method, working stress method; Loads and load combinations; Elements of Masonry design; Limit state method: Design of Beams: Singly reinforced, doubly reinforced, rectangular, T and L beams; Design of Slabs: One way, two way, waffle slabs; Design of Columns: Subjected to concentric and eccentric axial loading; Design of footings: Individual and combined footing; elements of foundation design; Standard and ductile detailing.

name: Steel Design
description: Design of tension members; Design of beams; Design of compression members; Analysis of eccentrically loaded columns; Design of beam-columns; Design of connections (riveted, bolted and welded); Single and built-up sections.

name: Civil Engineering Materials Lab
description: Background on stones, bricks, tiles, cement, steel, concrete, paints and polymers with relevant discussions of IS code provisions; concrete mix design; durability of concrete. Standard consistency, initial and final setting time of cement sample using Vicat’s apparatus; Soundness of given sample of cement and lime by (Le‐Chatelier test, autoclave test); Compressive strength of cement sample; Fineness of cement using (dry blank sieving, Blaine’s air permeability method) + Specific gravity and water absorption of coarse aggregate; Fineness modulus and particle size distribution, shape test and abrasion test of coarse, fine, and all in aggregates; Consistency & workability of freshly mixed concrete (slump & compaction test); Cube strength and cylinder strength of concrete of given proportion and given water cement ratio; Tensile strength of steel; Compressive strength and water absorption of burnt clay bricks and stone samples. Bitumen tests.

name: Masonry Design
description: Basic structural behavior and design of low-rise bearing wall buildings; Basic material properties; Strength design of unreinforced masonry elements; Allowable stress design of unreinforced masonry elements; Introduction to reinforced masonry; Introduction to confined masonry.

name: Water Resource Engineering
description: Hydraulic processes: control volume approach, continuity, energy, momentum, velocity distribution, Hydrologic processes: introduction to hydrology, precipitation, evaporation, and infiltration, Surface runoff: drainage basins and storm hydrograph, hydrologic losses, unit hydrograph, Streamflow routing: hydrologic reservoir routing, hydrologic river routing, hydraulic routing, Introduction to pipe flow: classification of flow, head losses, forces in pipe flow, Introduction to open channel flow: steady uniform flow, specific energy and specific forces, steady and gradually varied flow, rapidly varied flow, discharge measurement, Introduction to groundwater flow: groundwater concepts, saturated flow, steady state one dimensional flow, steady state well hydraulics, transient well hydraulics, and boundary effects

name: Field Survey Project
description: Survey camp of 7-10 days: Total station survey, Geological survey, Creating survey map of the area including information about geological features at the site. Identification of different rock types and landforms in the area.

name: Hydrology and Hydraulics
description: Fundamentals: control volume approach, continuity, energy, momentum, velocity distribution; Hydrologic processes: introduction to hydrology, precipitation, evaporation, and infiltration; Surface runoff: drainage basins and storm hydrograph, hydrologic losses, unit hydrograph; Streamflow routing: hydrologic reservoir routing, hydrologic river routing, hydraulic routing; Extreme Value Statistics: Generalized Extreme Value Theory for return levels; Open channel flow: steady uniform flow, specific energy and specific forces, steady and gradually varied flow, rapidly varied flow, discharge measurement; Groundwater flow: groundwater concepts, saturated flow, steady state one dimensional flow, steady state well hydraulics, transient well hydraulics, and boundary effects

name: Design of Reinforced Concrete Structures
description: Introduction to Reinforced Concrete Structures and Design Philosophy: Working stress (WS) and limit state (LS) design philosophy; Factor of safety vs partial safety factor; and Characteristic strength vs characteristic load; Design of Typical Structural Components: Beams (WS and LS methods; Singly vs doubly reinforced; and Flexure and shear actions); Slabs (One-way and Two-way; Deflection check; and Yield line theory); Columns (Columns under axial load and bi-axial moment; P-M-M interaction; and Slender columns and secondary effects); Foundation (Isolated footing with axial and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Combined footing); Staircase (Dog-legged; and Three flights); and Shear walls (per IS 13920); Introduction to Seismic Design: Seismic load as per IS 1893; Capacity Design per IS 13920; and Design of beam-column joints; Introduction to Design of Flat-Slab and Raft Foundation (Structural Design): Equivalent frame method; and Analysis using FE software; Introduction to Prestressed Concrete Design: Philosophy; Losses in prestressing; and Design of prestressed concrete beam; Testing of Cement: Standard consistency, Initial and final setting time and Soundness. Fineness (Dry blank sieving, Blain's Air permeability) and Specific gravity; Testing of Aggregate: Bulking of sand, Water absorption, Bulk density of course and fine aggregate; Sieve analysis and fineness modulus of course and fine aggregate; Abrasion resistance, Impact toughness, flakiness and elongation test; Testing of Fresh and Hardened Concrete: Air content, Slump cone test, Compaction factor test and Flow table test; Compressive strength of concrete cube and cylinder and mortar cube; Concrete mix-design; Non-destructive Testing of Concrete: Ultrasonic pulse velocity test and rebound hammer tests. Active Learning Components: Architectural planning, design and detailing of a low-rise RCC building in a group-project; Survey of a construction site and preparing a brief report describing the observations on i) formworks, ii) construction sequence, iii) reinforcement detailing etc. This report will map onto the background knowledge imparted during the lecture classes; Laboratory testing of concrete in the fresh and hardened states and the mix-design of concrete for a targeted strength

name: Design of Steel Structures
description: Review of Design Philosophy: Working stress (WS), Ultimate load (UL), Load resistant factor design (LRFD), and Limit state (LS); characteristics load vs strength; Load combinations; and Partial safety factors; Elementary Plastic Analysis: Moment-curvature relationship; Plastic hinges; and Collapse load and Mechanism; Design of Pure Tension and Pure Compression Members: Bolted vs welded members; Theory of elastic buckling with and without imperfection; Long and short column effects; and P-Delta effect; Design of Beams and Plate Girders: Kirchhoff’s plate bending theory; Plate buckling; Torsional and Lateral-torsional buckling; Design of laterally supported and unsupported beams; and Rolled steel vs built-up sections ; Design of Beam-Columns: Combined effect of axial and flexure (P-M interaction), Design of columns subjected to axial force and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Rolled steel vs built-up sections; Design of Beam-Column Joints: Strong vs weaker axis connections; Moment-shear connection, Torsion-shear connection; and Bolted vs welded connections; Design of Column-Base Plate Connections: Bolted vs welded connections; Axial force and uniaxial / biaxial moment; and Uplift and Anchor bolts. Active Learning Components: Design and detailing of one of the followings in a group project. Design of an Industrial Shed, Design of a Moment-Resisting Frame Building, Design of a Gantry-Girder system

name: Environmental Science and Engineering
description: Review of fundamental concepts: absorption, adsorption, reaction kinetics, dispersion, diffusion, electrochemistry, and biochemistry; fundamental of combustion and pollutant formation. Types of environmental pollution: air, water, soil, and noise pollution along with solid and hazardous waste such as nuclear, chemical, and bio; causes/sources of pollution, impacts, and extent; standards and regulations for each type of pollution; in case of water pollution, treatment technologies (source to end-user and end-user to discharge); in case of air pollution, transformation, transport, and the fate of air pollutants. indoor vs outdoor air pollution, and mitigation technologies and strategies. Environmental Management: Introduction to environmental impact assessment, environmental audit, environmental monitoring Hands-on component: Laboratory tours, instrument orientations, basic tests such as water characterization (alkalinity, pH, TDS, etc.), basic air quality instrumentation and measurements, and modeling of pollutant transport. Filed visit and course project: A field visit to a water or waste treatment facility, thermal power plant, or any other relevant site. A hands-on group project involving but not limited to data collection, data analysis, or sample analysis using the in-campus resources accessible to undergraduate students.

name: Geotechnical Engineering
description: Geotechnical investigations, reconnaissance and investigation plan, drilling, sampling, field-tests, groundwater level, laboratory tests, etc.; Stress-strain-strength behavior of soils, Triaxial tests – UU, CU and CD tests, stress paths, Skempton’s pore-pressure parameters, sample disturbance; Stability of slopes, limit equilibrium methods, ordinary methods of slices and simplified Bishop method, factors of safety; Seepage and stability of earth embankments, Types of foundations - shallow/deep, isolated, combined, mat, etc., contact pressure distributions, soil - foundation interactions, basics of structural design; Design of shallow foundation, bearing capacity, stress distribution in soils, total and differential settlement, plate-load test, structural forces and design; Retaining structures, gravity, cantilever, counterfort, reinforced earth, etc., design and checks for stability; Design of deep foundations, piles, pile groups, well foundations, shaft and base resistances, downdrag, pile load tests. Introduction to Geosynthetics and reinforced earth structures.

name: Civil Engineering Materials
description: 1. Introduction: selection of materials, engineering properties, nature of materials. 2.Iron and Steel: production process, microstructure, phase change diagrams, properties and test methods, corrosion. 3.Cement: production process; types of cements; composition; hydration reactions; chemical and physical properties. 4.Aggregates: types of aggregates; experimental methods to determine physical properties – water absorption, density. 5.Chemical Admixtures: Types of admixtures; working mechanisms; applications. 6.Concrete: Mixture proportioning, types of concrete, fresh properties, hardened properties, durability. 7.Bituminous materials: production process, asphalt grades and properties, asphalt concrete 8.Wood and wood products: properties, shrinkage and seasoning, treatment and durability 9.Masonry: masonry units; masonry construction: types of bonds and joints; mortar, grout, and plaster; properties. 10.Plastics and polymers: types, properties, and applications 11.Composites 12.Sustainability

name: Comprehensive Project - 1
description: A big construction project will be considered and will be sub-divided into several components. Teams of students will act as separate ‘consulting agencies’ and will carry out complete planning, analysis, design and construction planning for these components. The teams will be required to merge their designs of individual components of the entire project towards the end and present a consolidated plan, design and construction plan of the entire project.

name: Geotechnical Engineering
description: Geotechnical investigations, reconnaissance and investigation plan, drilling, sampling, field-tests, groundwater level, laboratory tests, etc.; Stress-strain-strength behavior of soils, Triaxial tests – UU, CU and CD tests, stress paths, Skempton’s pore-pressure parameters, sample disturbance; Stability of slopes, limit equilibrium methods, ordinary methods of slices and simplified Bishop method, factors of safety; Seepage and stability of earth embankments, Types of foundations - shallow/deep, isolated, combined, mat, etc., contact pressure distributions, soil - foundation interactions, basics of structural design; Design of shallow foundation, bearing capacity, stress distribution in soils, total and differential settlement, plate- load test, structural forces and design; Retaining structures, gravity, cantilever, counterfort, reinforced earth, etc., design and checks for stability; Design of deep foundations, piles, pile groups, well foundations, shaft and base resistances, downdrag, pile load tests; Special topics – Geosynthetics, reinforced earth structures, ground improvement techniques, machine foundations.

name: Construction Technology & Management
description: Cost estimating and bidding: material estimates, labor and equipment costs, cost control, purchasing, tender bidding; Project scheduling: bar charts, PERT, CPM, network diagrams; Project management: quality assurance, crisis management, claims management, safety; Construction machinery and methods; Construction accounting and budgeting; Construction law: building codes, local laws, approvals, environmental impact; arbitration; Construction blueprint and plan reading, environmental considerations; Client relations; Introduction to use of project management software

name: Transportation Engineering
description: History of highway planning in India, India’s highway network, highway economics; Transportation infrastructure: geometric components of highways and airport pavements; Geometric design of highways: cross-section design, sight distances, horizontal and vertical alignments, drainage elements; Pavement design: characterization of subgrade, aggregates, bitumen, bituminous mixes, pavement quality concrete, design of flexible and rigid pavements using IRC codes and ICAO Annex 14; Traffic engineering: Macroscopic and microscopic models of traffic flow, introduction to the Indian Highway Capacity Manual (IndoHCM) for estimating highway capacity, traffic signal design.

name: Advanced Geotechnical Engineering
description: Soil Composition, Structure and Classification; Shear Strength of soils: Failure analysis, UC, DS and UU tests; Characterization of ground: Designing an Investigation plan, In-situ test such as SPT, DCPT, CPT, etc, Sampling techniques; Bearing capacity: Failure modes, Generalized equation, Codal provisions, General correlations and interpretations from in-situ tests. Compressibility behavior of soils: Compaction & Consolidation, Settlement of foundations: stress in soils, immediate, consolidation and creep settlements, methods based on in situ tests; Dynamic properties of soils, Geophysical investigation, general correlations; Special topics: Ground Improvement Techniques, Geosynthetics, Liquefaction, Expansive soils, Soft soils, Solid waste & Landfill.

name: Analysis and Design of Foundation Systems
description: Stress-strain behavior of soils, CU and CD tests, p-q space, stress path; Constitutive models, Design of shallow foundations, Isolated and combined footings, Rafts; Design of deep foundations, piles, piled rafts, well foundations; Foundation optimization, Soil dynamics, Machine foundations. Geotechnical Investigation and design project: field and laboratory tests, interpretations, reporting, design parameters, analysis and design of foundation system.

name: Constitutive Models in Soil Mechanics
description: Role of constitutive modeling; Importance of laboratory testing with relation to constitutive modeling; Elasticity: linear, quasilinear, anisotropic; Plasticity basics: yield criteria, flow rule, plastic potential, hardening/softening; Rate Independent Plasticity: Mohr-Coulomb, Non-linear failure criteria, Drucker-Prager, and cap models; Critical state soil mechanics: critical state concept, cam-clay models and simulation; Stress-dilatancy theory; Work hardening plasticity theory: formulation and implementation; Applications of elasto-plastic models; Special Topics: hypoelasticity-plasticity, disturbed state concept.

name: Remote Sensing of Land and Water Resources
description: An overview of remote sensing, Electromagnetic radiation principles, Remote sensing data collection, Geometric correction, Image enhancement, Image interpretation, Image classification, Band transformation, Thermal infrared remote sensing, Change detection, Feature extraction, Monitoring of land and water resources, Accuracy assessment, Remote sensing of soil, vegetation, water, and urban areas, Object oriented classification, and Spectral Indices.

name: Advanced Structural Analysis
description: Review of Elementary Structural Analysis, Static indeterminacy, Indeterminate system, Truss analysis, Energy Method, Slope-Deflection Method, Analysis of Portal Frames; Matrix Method of Structural Analysis, Structural analysis using stiffness matrix and flexibility matrix, and Direct stiffness method; Conceptual Transition from Direct Stiffness Method to Finite Element Method, Truss and frame elements, Shape functions, Properties of stiffness matrix, Discretization; Introduction to Nonlinear Analysis, Introduction of geometric and material nonlinearities, P-delta effect, Moment-Curvature relation, Moment- Rotation relation, P-M-M interaction; Special topics: Nonlinear Analysis of Structures, 2D Frame analysis with geometric nonlinearity, 2D Frame analysis with material nonlinearity, 2D Frame analysis with both geometric and material nonlinearities.

name: Advanced Engineering Hydrology
description: Hydroclimatology, water balance, understanding hydrologic change, statistical representation of hydrologic data, flood frequency analysis, understanding frequency of droughts, hydrologic design, hydrologic time series analysis, parametric and non-parametric trends, spectral analysis, wavelet analysis, uncertainty analysis, hydrologic modeling, and hydrologic forecasting

name: Analysis and Design of Masonry Buildings
description: Masonry materials and components; general design considerations for masonry structures per Indian and international standards; design of masonry beams; design of masonry loadbearing walls for concentric and eccentric axial loads, including slenderness effects; seismic performance of masonry buildings; an overview of the seismic design provisions contained in Indian standards; seismic design of masonry shear walls for in-plane and out-of-plane seismic loading; seismic design of unreinforced, reinforced, and confined masonry buildings; seismic evaluation and retrofit of masonry buildings.

name: Structural Design for Fire
description: Review of performance-based design concepts, LRFD cold design of RCC and steel structures; codified models for fire, standard and natural fire; material behavior of steel and concrete constituents in fire; estimation of time-temperature curves for structural members in different fire and exposure/ventilation scenarios; design considerations for individual members exposed to fire: structural members in tension, compression, bending; provisions in Eurocodes and IS 456, 800, 1641, 1642 and 1643 to ensure fire safety; fire proofing materials used to enhance fire resistance of structural members; behavior of structural systems under fire; preventive/protective systems for structures.

name: Structural Dynamics
description: Single Degree Freedom System (equation of motion; free and force vibrations; seismic excitation; time history analysis; response spectrum; approximate methods), Multiple Degrees Freedom System (eigenvalue problem; shear buildings; mode superposition method; modal combination rules; time history analysis), One story system (lateral-torsional coupling; non-orthogonal lateral coupling; directional combination rule) and introduction to continuum systems (flexural beam, its natural properties, response due to seismic excitation)

name: River Engineering
description: Open channel flow; Computations in gradually varied flows and rapidly varied flows;Mathematical description of flow and sediment transport;1-D/2-D/3-D computations in river flows; Dam break flow on rigid and mobile beds; Meandering of rivers; River training works including flood protection due to river overflow,levee, river bed lining,bridge pier protection due to river water flow;Design of bridges, dams, spillways; Irrigation control structures; Indian standards related to river engineering; Flow measurements in rivers; Physical modeling of river flows;Protection of river water from pollution;Introduction to various Indian agencies (CWC, NIH, CWPRS etc.); Field trip to a river;Project work .

name: Advanced Hydraulic Engineering
description: Open Channel Flow: Uniform flow, Critical flow, Gradually varied flow (GVF), Computations in GVF, Sediment transport, Design of canals, Hydraulic jump, Flow past sharp- and broad-crested weirs, Design of spillways, Flood routing, Dam-break flow, Hydraulic design of bridges Pipe Flow: Head losses in pipes, Pipe network analysis, Transients in pipes, Detection of leak and partial blockage Flow measurements and laboratory scale modeling

name: Earthquake Engineering
description: Earthquakes: structure of earth, movement of plates, types of faults, P wave, S wave, surface waves, characterization of earthquakes and earthquake-induced ground motion; response spectra for individual ground motion records, site-specific response spectra, design spectra; single-degree-of-freedom systems; multi-degree-of-freedom systems; analysis, design and detailing of RC frames based on state-of-the-art and various codes IS 456, IS 1893 & IS 13920; Special topics: selection and scaling of ground motions, characterization of seismic hazard, seismic analysis and design of bridges, retaining walls, liquid-storage tanks, dams etc., design of non-structural components, passive structural control

name: Slopes and Retaining Structures
description: Stability of slopes, stability analysis, seismic analysis, probabilistic analysis, design of earth embankments and dams; Earth pressure theories; Earth retaining structures: rigid and flexible, Braced excavation; Reinforced earth structures; Buried structures, Case studies.

name: Applied Hydraulic Transients
description: Transients in pipe flows: Causes of transients; Governing equations; Method of characteristics, Transients caused by centrifugal pumps, Hydraulic transients in long oil pipelines, Resonance in pressurized piping system, Methods to control transients, surge tanks. Transients in open channel flows: Causes of transients, Governing equations, Method of characteristics, Explicit and Implicit Finite Difference methods, Sediment routing, Coordinate transformation and two-Dimensional flow simulations, VOF method for surface tracking.

name: Geosynthetics
description: Historical background, Types, Manufacturing Methods, Functions, Typical Applications; Geosynthetic Testing, Physical, Mechanical, Construction survivability and durability testing of Geotextiles and Geogrids; Principles of Soil Reinforcement, Types of Reinforcement, Testing, Allowable loads for design (creep etc.), Elements of Design + NCMA Method, Fill Material, Detailed Design of Reinforced Soil Walls (BS 8006), Reinforced Slopes (BS 8006), Facia and Construction Methodology, Specifications, Bearing capacity Improvement, Embankments on soft soils, Basal Reinforcement – Design, Geocell – Design, Construction and Specification, IRC/MORTH Guidelines, Case Histories; Filteration and Drainage, Principles, Typical applications, Dams, French Drains, Testing, Design Principles, Specifications; Pavements and Airports; Influence of cyclic loading, Design for soft soils (unpaved) and new construction – Granular layers, GS in Bituminous Pavements – new construction and rehabilitation, Construction and specifications, Rural Road applications, Railway Tracks, Indian experiences; Natural fibre Materials, Jute and Coir Geotextiles - Products, Processes; Environmental Control, Erosion Control, Mechanics of Erosion, Methods of Control, Products, Design for various conditions, Silt Control, Land slides – Occurrence and methods of mitigation, Liners for Pounds and Canals; Engineered Landfills, Municipal Solid Waste, Hazardous Waste Landfills, Site location –MOEF Guidelines, Covers and liners, GCLs, Material aspects and, Design, construction and maintenance, the Indian Scenario

name: Irrigation Engineering and Hydraulic Structures
description: Water requirement of crops, Duty, Delta, Canal irrigation, Tank irrigation, Sprinkler irrigation, Drip irrigation, Canal flow analysis; Ground water flow equations, Well hydraulics, Well irrigation;Design of canals and canal head works, Design of sluice gates, River training works, Structures for silt control, Gravity dams, Earthen dams, Spillway.

name: Advanced Concrete Design
description: Review of Design Philosophy: Characteristic load and strength, WSM / ULM / LRFD / LSM, Limit States: ULS / SLS, Probabilistic framework; Essential Structural Components: Estimation of wind and earthquake load; Design of beam and column; Design of beam-column-joint with ductile detailing; Design of Shear wall--Solid with boundary elements--Degree of coupling--Shear lag-- Flanged and Shear wall core; Strut-and-Tie Model based Design: Conceptual design; Application to the design of--Deep Beam--Shear Wall--Corbel--Beam- column joint--Secondary beam and beam-to-beam connection--Diaphragm-to- beam connection and flanged action; Yield Line Theory in Regular Slab; Design of Flat plate and Flat Slab; Design of Foundation Systems: Isolated and Strip; Combined and Raft; Pile; Overall Structural Design (One of the three modules in a project based mode)— Module-1: Design of Liquid Retaining Structures--Water tank at ground--Elevated water tank with staging-- -Module-2: Design of Mid-to-High rise Buildings---Understanding through different structural configurations--Shear wall core--Tube system and Tube-in- Tube system---Module-3: Design of Bridges---Highway or Railway---Loading, load combination and different limit states--Bridge deck---Piers--- Foundation—pre-stressed concrete bridges. It is proposed to cover one module in special topics depending on the instructor.

name: Infrastructure Systems: Planning and Management
description: 1. Introduction and System modeling: System Components, Planning and Management, Case study: modeling of water resource systems, Supply and Demand Curves for Infrastructure systems. 2. Introduction to optimization: Modeling Constraints, Linear Programming Solution Strategies: Graphical and simplex methods, Dynamic Programming and stochastic programming, Python for Water Resource Management: Introduction to GEKKO, and PULP. 3. Engineering cost-benefit analysis: Premise of cost-benefit analysis; Time value of Money; Comparison of alternatives including uncertainty analysis; Decision trees (Deterministic and probabilistic); Life cycle cost analysis 4. Risk assessment: Probabilistic Risk and Reliability Assessment; Risk Aversion; Non-stationary risk analysis; Fragility curves for Infrastructure systems 5. Network modeling for Infrastructure systems: Critical Infrastructure Fundamentals; Network Analysis; Robustness and tipping point analysis; Network of Network modeling; Recovery Modeling of networked systems, 6. Future of Critical Infrastructure Systems: Integrated risk and resilience management policy, resilient design: best practices and opportunities.

name: Air Pollution Control Engineering
description: Overview of air pollution: Types of air pollutants (classified into two categories): (1) Particulate matter (PM) and (2) gaseous pollutants (volatile organic compounds, sulfur oxides, nitrogen oxides, ozone, CO2); common sources; health and environmental impacts of air pollutants; the global and national context of air pollution: evolution of policies and regulations and the current status

name: Pavement Materials and Design
description: Introduction: types of pavements, materials used in pavements and their characterization; Aggregates: Aggregate gradation, Bailey method, MoRTH specifications; Bitumen and bituminous mixes: bitumen grades (PEN, VG, PG) and associated tests, design of bituminous mixes using Superpave, design of mixes with RAP; Cement and concrete mixes: mix design of Pavement Quality Concrete (PQC), role of fibers; Design of flexible pavements: layered elastic theory, mechanistic-empirical flexible pavement design for fatigue and rutting using IRC and AASHTO methods; Design of rigid pavements: mechanistic-empirical rigid pavement design for fatigue and faulting using IRC and AASHTO methods; Additional topics: Design of composite pavements, pavement evaluation and rehabilitation, and other topics of interest, time permitting.

name: Traffic and Roadway Engineering
description: Introduction: Classification of roadways, vehicle characteristics, traffic stream characteristics, car following models; Traffic design: highway capacity analysis using the Indian Highway Capacity Manual (Indo-HCM), level of service, traffic control and calming devices, traffic signal design; Roadway elements: cross- section, alignments, sight distances; Alignment design: stationing, curve, superelevation, and transition design for horizontal alignment, sag and crest vertical alignment design, K value, clearance requirements for overpasses and underpasses, slip roads/ramps; Drainage: design of transverse and longitudinal drainage elements; Earthworks: cut and fill quantification, borrow and fill, cost estimation.

name: Advanced Concrete Technology
description: Cement: Production process; types of cements; composition; hydration reactions; chemical and physical properties; characterization techniques – XRF, XRD, particle size analysis; Aggregates: Types of aggregates; experimental methods to determine physical properties – water absorption, density, abrasion and impact resistance, particle size distribution; Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs): Types of SCMs; chemical and physical properties; hydraulic and pozzolanic reactions; SCM reactivity tests – strength activity index, lime reactivity, R3 test; Chemical Admixtures: Types of admixtures; working mechanisms; applications; Concrete Mixture Proportioning – high performance concrete; performance-based mixture proportioning; Fresh Properties: Rheology; advanced tests to determine fresh properties of special concretes such as self-compacting concrete; pumping of concrete – high-rise buildings, 3D concrete printing; Hardened Properties: Mechanical properties – compression, tensile, flexural strength tests; creep; shrinkage – chemical, plastic, and drying; Transport Properties: Pore structure; diffusion; permeability ; Concrete Durability: Corrosion; carbonation; sulfate attack; alkali-aggregate reactions; freezing and thawing damage. The deterioration mechanisms and advanced tests to evaluate them.

name: AI for Civil Engineering
description: Mathematical foundations of machine learning: linear algebra, probability, optimization, and vector calculus; supervised and unsupervised learning methods: regression, classification, clustering, and dimensionality reduction; neural networks and deep learning: feedforward, convolutional, recurrent, and attention-based architectures; transfer learning: fine-tuning for small and heterogeneous datasets; integration of physics and domain knowledge: physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and hybrid loss formulations; operator learning: DeepONet, Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs), Graph Neural Operators (GNOs), and transformer-based operator networks for spatio-temporal modeling; fully differentiable architectures: differentiable physics simulators and end-to-end learnable pipelines for governing processes; gray-box and hybrid modeling frameworks: combining mechanistic equations with learnable residuals; uncertainty quantification and explainable AI: SHAP, LIME, and feature-attribution methods for model interpretability; applications in hydrology and water resources: rainfall–runoff modeling, flood forecasting, groundwater flow and recharge estimation; environmental systems: air quality prediction, pollutant dispersion, and coupled hydro-climatic modeling; transportation systems: traffic flow and travel-time prediction using graph and transformer networks, pavement performance prediction models; applications in geotechnical engineering: slope stability and landslide prediction using convolutional networks, liquefaction potential prediction models, soil classification models; structural and material systems: constitutive relations, crack detection, and damage localization through physics-guided neural networks, surrogate models for design; integration with urban digital twins and resilience analytics: linking data-driven insights with decision-making for sustainable and climate-adaptive infrastructure; ethical, interpretable, and trustworthy deployment: responsible use of AI/ML in civil and environmental domains

name: Hydrodynamics of Sediment Transport
description: Sediment properties: Physical properties of sediment, sediment sizes, sediment mixture, suspended sediment mixture, terminal fall velocity of sediment in fluid; Turbulent flow characteristics: Introduction to turbulence, decomposition and averaging procedure, Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) equations, Reynolds shear stress in a zero-pressure gradient flow, the law of the wall, the log-wake law and the velocity defect law, secondary currents and dip phenomenon; Sediment threshold: Definition of sediment threshold, threshold velocity concept, Yang’s threshold velocity model, lift force concept, threshold bed shear stress concept, Shields diagram, probabilistic concept of sediment entrainment, turbulence-induced concept of sediment entrainment, stable channel design, straight trapezoidal channels, stable-ideal section of a threshold channel; Bedload transport: Definition of bedload transport, bed shear stress concept of bedload transport, du Boys’ approach, du Boys-type equations, discharge concept of bedload transport, velocity concept of bedload transport, bedform concept of bedload transport, probabilistic concept of bedload transport, Einstein’s approach, Engelund and Fredsøe’s approach, deterministic concept of bedload transport, Bagnold’s approach, Yalin’s approach, saltation, saltation characteristics, particle trajectory and characteristic parameters, streambed armoring and water-worked bed; Suspended-load transport: Diffusion concept of suspended-load transport, generalized advection–diffusion equation for suspended sediment motion, governing equation for sediment concentration distribution, various approaches to determine sediment concentration distribution, reference level and reference concentration, computation of suspended load by diffusion approach, energy concept of suspended-load transport, wash load, turbidity current; Total-load transport: Indirect approach, direct approach, total-load transport of nonuniform sediment; River processes: Bed forms, bars, prediction of bedforms, mathematical developments on sand waves, bed features in gravel-bed streams, resistance to flow due to bedforms, meandering rivers, braided rivers; Scour: Scour within channel contractions, scour downstream of structures, scour below horizontal pipelines, scour at bridge piers, scour at bridge abutments, scour countermeasures

name: Computation and Cognition
description: Mechanistic/algorithmic approaches and issues addressed by these approaches: parallel versus serial processing, flow of information, timing effects. Rational/probabilistic approaches and issues addressed by these approaches: adaptation to the environment, behaviour under uncertainty, learning, timing effects. General issues: top-down versus bottom-up processing, online processing, integration of multiple sources of information. Methodology and issues in the development and evaluation of cognitive models: Which psychological data are relevant? What predictions are made by a model? How could these be tested? Modelling techniques: in the assignments, students will experiment with both symbolic (rule based) and subsymbolic (probabilistic) cognitive models. Connectionist models | Bayesian models |Dynamic system models |Logic/symbol-based models | Classical cognitive architectures/production models | Unified Theories of Cognition, Cognitive Architectures and Frameworks. COGENT as a modelling framework SOAR and ACT-R as cognitive architectures | Models of specific cognitive functions

name: Foundations of Cognitive Science
description: The course aims to give an overview of different component disciplines of cognitive science and how they come together to study brain and cognition. The topics will include: Philosophy: Theories of mind (Indian & Western), Consciousness, Ontological and epistemological approaches to Mind Artificial Intelligence: cybernetics, Information theories, Turing machine, knowledge representation, neural networks Linguistics: Ancient India, structuralism, formalism, generative grammar, Chomsky’s arguments against behaviorism, syntax and semantics Anthropology: Evolutionary and alternative theories, diffusion, functional school, culture and language, personality research, structural, symbolic anthropology Psychology: associationism, psychophysics, psychometrics, cognitivism, metacognition, constructivism, evolutionary psychology Neuroscience: neuron, reflex arc, brain structure and function, disorders (deficit), stimulation, Hebbian theory, experimental methods (electrophysiology, imaging), language & lateralization

name: Fundamentals of Cognitive Psychology
description: basic processes in vision; object and face recognition; attention and performance; learning; memory and forgetting; reading and speech perception; language comprehension and production; judgment and decision making & cognition and emotion

name: Research Methods in Cognitive Science
description: Research methods: Null Hypothesis formulation & testing, dependent & independent variables, qualitative, quantitative and categorical data, measurement scales, descriptive and inferential statistics (measures of central tendency, significance testing, ANOVA, repeated measures, posthoc testing, correlations, chi-square tests); Experimental design:single subject, between- group and within-group designs, randomization, factorial, parametric, subtractive, conjunction type designs; Research Tools: Review of basic mathematics, experimental psychology software (psychtoolbox, eprimeetc), Statistics using PSPP/Matlab/R; Research ethics and scientific writing with reference to APA guidelines.

name: Fundamental Neuroscience
description: Foundational principles of neuroscience Cellular basis of nervous system function Neural circuits and systems Sensory systems The structure and function of the motor system Cognitive neuroscience and higher order brain function Attention, language, memory and executive functions Neuroanatomy and damage to the nervous system.

name: Experimental Techniques in Cognitive Science
description: Introduction to experimental design – Hypothesis; Independent and dependent variables; principles of Reaction Time studies. Applying statistical techniques – lab based module primarily intended to create a practical awareness of statistical testing. This will include many example data datasets and deciding what an effective test is and how to do it. Data interpretation – what does significant statistical results mean. How does these numbers inform about mental processes. Writing – Including effective writing techniques; how to report statistics. Hands on experience in different behavioural and imaging methodology including eyetracking, EEG etc.

name: Evolutionary Neuropsychology
description: Paleoneuropsychology is the study the evolution of the structures and functions of the human brain. It is an emerging multidisciplinary science that spans the fields of anthropology, archaeology, cognitive neuroscience, and psychology. This course is designed as introduction to the specific structures and functions of the human brain with particular attention to empirical brain research advances since 2000. It is also designed as an introduction to the evolution of the human brain (size, shape, neurons, chemical neurotransmitters, etc.) and the evolution of human cognitive processes such as memory, language, dreaming, and creativity.

name: Behavioral Economics
description: Introduction: Historical development of the field, interdisciplinary perspectives linking psychology, economics and neuroscience, Overview of the field with real life examples. Modes of thought, Heuristics and biases: Modes of thought (intuitive and deliberative), heuristics (anchoring and availability, representativeness), Biases (Framing effects, mental accounting), taming intuitive heuristics, self-regulation in the brain, The positive side of heuristic decision making. Choice under certainty - concept of preference in rational choice theory, revealed preference, decision making under certainty. Choice under uncertainty – The concept of value and utility under uncertainty, psychological weighting of probabilities, deciding about prospects, gains and losses, Neural representations of subjective value and choices in the brain. Thinking over time - Discounted utility, hyperbolic discounting, affective forecasting biases, retrospective experiential biases for value computation. Applying behavioral economics - Marketing examples from prospect theory, choice architecture, Real examples of nudges for better health, wealth and happiness.

name: Introduction to Cognitive Linguistics
