description: Basics of Cognitive Linguistics: This module introduces theoretical assumptions and commitments of Cognitive Linguistics, a functional approach to language use contrasted by formal approaches. In that sense, a fundamental question is asked: “What does it mean to know a language?” Also, two main aspects of Cognitive Linguistics—Language and Cognition—are interpreted; Linguistic Relativity: Beginning with the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, this module taps into uniformity and diversity across languages in regard to the associated processing and behavior. The main discussion is on cross-linguistic variations in conceptualizations of time, space, and color; Categorization: Projection of how mental lexicon and concepts operate in the human mind. To understand the hierarchy of concepts, the association of lexical items as well as lexicon-internal polysemy are explored. Theories including the prototype theory are discussed and compared. It leads to the semantic basis of grammatical category; Mental Spaces: Cases of metaphor, metonymy, meronomy, and blends are analyzed with cross-linguistic points of view. The discussion components include the Conceptual Metaphor Theory and the Mental Space Theory; Cognitive Approaches to Grammar: This module discusses the structural consideration of human language processing. It covers idioms, Construction grammar, and Cognitive grammar.

name: Motor Learning and Memory
description: Motor systems in the brain – motor and premotor cortices, parietal and frontal cortices, basal ganglia and cerebellum; Approaches to studying motor learning – defining and measuring motor learning, designing experiments on learning, alternative methods for measuring learning, issues about “amount” of learning, understanding differences between learning and changes in performance; Learning of motor sequences – implicit versus explicit learning, chunking, role of sleep, neural basis, disruption in different brain disorders; Adaptation learning – adaptation to novel dynamical and visual perturbations, sensory signals responsible for adaptation, generalization and transfer of learning, elementary computational models, neural basis; Learning under unstable and unpredictable conditions – motor noise, impedance control, feedforward and feedback components of impedance control; Motor skill learning – use dependent learning, “model-free” reinforcement learning, neural basis; Vocal learning in songbirds – role of variability and use of exploration, critical period, neural circuits, application to human speech learning; Application to neurorehabilitation and robotics – motor learning principles in rehab, robot-assisted rehab, error augmentation strategies, model of motor recovery after stroke, concurrent force and impedance adaptation in robots, robotic implementation.

name: Classics in Brain Science
description: Half semester course examining, at an advanced level, classic research that has shaped current understanding of brain function. Following topics will be covered: 1. Generation and transmission of neuronal signals ( 2. Intercellular communication 3. Vision 4. Proprioception (Processing of somatosensory information and organization of somatosensory cortex. Classic experiments by Vallbo, Matthews and Willis) 5. Spinal Reflexes (Classic experiments on reflex circuit organization by Sherrington, which won him the Nobel prize; work by Matthews, Eccles and Lundberg on reflex physiology). 6. Control of voluntary movement (organization of motor cortex, descending projections from motor cortical areas, movement planning and control all examined via studies of Evarts, Georgopoulos, Strick and Kalaska) 7. Learning and Memory (Cortical basis of learning and memory, two learning/memory systems, retention and recall of memories as explained by the definitive work of Squire, Poldrack and Nobel prize winner Kandel) Modern techniques used in research in the above areas will be introduced alongside the discussion on each topic.

name: Perception and Attention
description: Information processing view and ecological approach to perception; The visual anatomy and physiology; Psychophysical methods; Perception of color, motion and depth; Theories of attention – FIT, CODE, TVA etc; Mechanisms of selection; locus of control; Inhibitory processes in selection etc; Experimental paradigms (cueing, visual search, and phenomena like in attentional blindness and attentional blink)

name: Phenomenology, Embodiment, and Consciousness
description: This course introduces Phenomenology, the various forms that it takes (Transcendental, Genitive, Existential, Feminist, Post-, Neuro), its emphasis on description (particularly first- person experience), and the role of the body in consciousness. Phenomenology as a method is oriented around what is called the ‘époché’ or bracketing (phenomenological reduction), wherein the subjective concerns of an individual are placed ‘in brackets’ in order to move toward an accurate description of the things themselves. We will discuss whether or not such a bracketing is possible, the role of Phenomenology in description and pay particularly close attention to concepts such as sedimentation, constitution, institution, intentionality, incorporation, extension, habit, bodily motility, spatiality, intersubjectivity, and body/mind dualism as they are presented through the work of founding figure of Phenomenology, Edmund Husserl, the Existential Phenomenology variation and focus on the body disclosed in the work of Maurice Merleau-Ponty, and the potential for intertwining Phenomenology and Cognitive Science in the more recent disclosure of Neurophenomenology.

name: Learning and Memory
description: Psychological theories of learning, behavioral and cognitive approach to learning. Classical conditioning, reinforcement learning and Motor learning. Types of Associations, Biological constraints on classical conditioning. Procedures of shaping behavior, Role of reinforcer. Theories of memory, Storage, Encoding and retrieval, Types of Memory, techniques of testing memory. Localization of memory, Mechanisms of Memory, memory disorders Neurobiology of learning and memory, functional network of brain areas involved in learning and memory

name: Philosophy of Mind
description: Cognitive Science and philosophy of mind: Epistemic and metaphysical issues of mind in Cognitive science Western theories of mind: Dualism, behaviorism, materialism, eliminativism functionalism, physicalism, phenomenology, representational theory of mind, modularity of mind and identity theory Neurobiological approaches to mind: Patricia Churchland arguments, the binding problem, the problem of Mary's Knowledge, Connectionism Computational approaches to mind: Searle's Chinese room argument, intentionality, the problem of intelligence and the representational nature of mind Indian theories of mind: Heterodox: Jainism and Buddhism; Orthodox: Samkhya, Nyaya and Vedanata

name: Emotion and Cognition
description: Historically, cognition and emotion were considered two independent processes that did not interact much with each other. Moreover, the study of emotions was considered subjective and therefore unscientific in a scientific world dominated by behaviourism. However, recent developments in cognitive neuro-sciences have enabled the study of emotions in a scientific manner and have increasingly shown evidence to its interconnectedness to cognition. What are the implications of such inter-connectedness? How do our feelings affect our thoughts, memory, decision making etc. and vice-versa? Topics will include: nature and measurement of emotions, categorical and dimensional approaches to affect, Affect cognition relations in specific cognitive processes like perception, attention, memory and judgement.

name: Neuroscience of Decision making
description: Anatomy and connectivity of reward circuit, neurophysiological studies in animals, dopamine and reinforcement learning, Neural basis of perceptual decision making, reinforcement learning in the brain; Human Brain imaging studies of decision making, Value based decision making, contextual influences, model-based analysis; Neural bases of inter- temporal choice, Emotional modulation in decision making ; Brain imaging studies of Game theory and social decision making

name: Neuroscience and Cognitive Neuroscience
description: Peripheral and central nervous system, neurons, action potential, synapses, synaptic transmission, neurotransmitters, brain structure, sulci, gyri, lobes, somatosensory homunculus, motor pathways, autonomous nervous system, hypothalamus, slee; Cognitive functions of the brain, experimental techniques (electrophysiology, ERP, imaging, lesion, TMS), Cognitive Neuroscience of Vision, Attention, Action, Memory, Speech and Language, Executive functions, Emotions

name: Neural Plasticity
description: The emergence of plasticity: experience-dependent plasticity in cats (monocular deprivation), Hubel & Weisel’s experiments, the concept of ‘critical period’, developmental plasticity and correlation with cognitive development Adult plasticity: plasticity following peripheral deafferentation in adults, Merzenich & Kaas’ experiments, limits and phases of adult plasticity, phantom limb phenomenon, peripheral vs CNS plasticity (stroke), motor plasticity and performance - expertise and time course of plasticity. Cross-modal plasticity: somatosensory plasticity following blindness, Molyneux question, plasticity in the newly sighted, visual cortex of deaf, rewiring of cortex in animal models (Pallas and Sur experiments) Neural mechanisms of plasticity: axonal sprouting, dendritic spine dynamics, synaptic plasticity ( LTD, LTP), Hebbian and homeostatic plasticity, the molecular and genetic basis of plasticity mechanisms Plasticity for cognitive enhancement: mechanisms for reintroducing critical period plasticity in animals, FastForWord - Merzenich and Paula Talal, brain training apps, stereopsis training for adult plasticity

name: Analysis and Modelling of Locomotion
description: Introduction to the basics of anatomy and physiology of locomotion, evaluations methods in clinics, and gait disorder; Spatio-temporal gait analysis. Walking phase detection, measurement of stride length, stride velocity, cadence, and other spatio-temporal parameters. Gait symmetry, gait variability, and gait coordination measurement. Clinical gait analysis. Practical examples to clinics, dual-task paradigm, and modelling relevant analogies of equivalents of locomotion; Kinematics approach for gait analysis, kinematics measurement, and motion capture. Stereo-photogrammetry, ultrasound, and magnetic motion capture. Accelerometers, gyroscopes, magnetometers and inertial-based motion capture systems, Lab vs. daily life monitoring, anatomical and functional calibration, joint kinematics, and application in orthopedics; Kinetics of locomotion. Forces and moment measurements- Forces transducers and force plates, pressure measuring systems and pressure insoles, combining kinetics with kinematics, energy, and power of body segment inverse dynamics, muscular activity. Application to knee adduction moment measurement; Neural basis of locomotion. Spinal circuitry underlying locomotion, the role of sensory information, modulation through descending systems, and cortical circuitry contributing to locomotion. Implication for the design of gait neuroprosthesis and exoskeletons; Models of the mechanics of biped locomotion. Inverted pendulum models. Spring-loaded inverse pendulum models. Links to robotics such as passive and dynamic walkers.

name: Human Brain Mapping
description: Principles of Brain Mapping: Localization of mental functions including perception, memory, attention and executive processes in the human brain and the study of their neural mechanisms; Mapping methods: Fundamentals and principles of mapping methods like functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI), and Electroencephalography (EEG). fMRI – Basics of MR physics, image acquisition, acquisition protocols, basics of T1w/T2w/T2* imaging, understanding BOLD data and sources of noise, design of experiments suitable for BOLD imaging, analysis of BOLD task and rest data, visualization of BOLD images, creation of individualized and averaged brain atlases; EEG – basics of EEG, experiment design, properties of data, sources of noise, Event Related Potentials, signal processing of brain state, microstates analysis, principles behind concurrent EEG-fMRI. Brain connectivity: Understanding how brain function and connectivity are interrelated, brain connectome, diffusion weight imaging (DWI), fMRI connectivity, changes with various disorders, connectivity based machine learning models, graph-based network properties.

name: General Chemistry
description: Coordination Chemistry: Werner’s work, EAN Rule, V. B. Theory, Crystal field theory, Ligand Field Theory, Jahn–Teller distortion, Square planar and Tetrahedral complexes, Chelates, Nomenclature and Isomerism of coordination compounds. Structure and bonding in rare gas compounds; HSAB theory; Chemical and biological buffers. Bioinorganic chemistry: Occurrence and availability & biological functions of inorganic elements in organisms, Uptake, transport and storage of Oxygen, Hemoglobin and Myoglobin, Photodynamic therapy. Organic Chemistry and Stereochemistry: Resonance and Inductive effects; Aromaticity and Huckel’s rule; Optical activity, Polarimeter, Chirality, Enantiomerism, Configuration, R-S Nomenclature, Reactions of stereoisomers, Resolution, Conformations of Alkanes and cycloalkanes, Introduction to UV-visible and IR spectroscopy and their applications.

name: Chemistry Laboratory
description: A mix set of experiments are taken from all three branches of chemistry (i.e. physical, inorganic and organic). The laboratory course will incorporate the experiments illustrating the basic principles of complexometry titrations, conductometry, chemical kinetics, colorimetry, polarimetry, thin layer chromatography, green synthesis, oscillatory reactions, simple salt- mixture analysis, UV-visible and IR spectroscopy.

name: Fundamentals and Applications of Spectroscopy
description: Fundamentals quantum chemistry: Schrodinger’s equation, basic description of wave functions, solution of particle in 1D box, Basic theoretical discussion on light-matter interaction from quantum mechanical approach, spectroscopic transitions and selection rules, Spectral broadening, rotational, vibrational (FTIR), electronic, Raman, and NMR spectroscopy; Various applications of spectroscopy: chemical bond length calculations using rotational-vibrational spectroscopy, identification of different materials using FTIR and NMR spectroscopy, temperature measurement with Raman and electronic spectroscopy, chemical sensing with electronic spectroscopy, applications of FTIR in food technology; Optical imaging of materials, applications in biology and soft materials.

name: Organic Chemistry in Everyday Life
description: Energy from hydrocarbon: Fossil fuels, anti-knocking agents, Research Octane Number (RON), and isooctane, biodiesel; Fats, oils and detergents: Natural fats, edible and industrial oils of vegetable origin, common fatty acids, hydrogenation of unsaturated oils, soaps, synthetic detergents; Synthetic polymers: polymerization, methods of polymerization, natural and synthetic rubbers; Synthetic dyes: Color and constitution, classification of dyes, synthesis of methyl orange, congo red, malachite green, crystal violet, phenolphthalein, fluorescein, alizarin and Indigo; Medicine: Drugs for the chronic (anticancer, antidiabetic drugs) and acute diseases (analgesics, antibiotics, etc.), synthesis and characterization of drugs, chiral drugs, purification methods such as crystallization, distillation, chromatography. Hands-on experiments: Synthesis, characterization and analysis of dyes such as fluorescein dye; Synthesis and characterization of drugs such as aspirin; Polymer synthesis; Functional group interconversions; Analysis of racemic drugs vs chiral drugs using polarimeter.

name: Forensic Chemistry
description: Chemicals underlying evidence; chemistry of controlled substances; methods of extraction, purification and sample purification for further analysis; methods for detection and identification of trace substances; basis of analytical assays; DNA based and biomolecular forensics; statistical treatment of forensic evidence; legal norms for chemical forensic evidence; ethics of biological forensic analysis.

name: Electrochemical Science and Engineering
description: Introduction to Basic principles- Electrochemical cells, characteristics of electrochemical equations, Faraday’s law and efficiency, potentials and Ohm’s law; Cell potential and thermodynamics: Electrochemical reactions, Standard potentials, activity correction, equilibrium electrochemistry, Pourbaix diagram, liquid junction potential; Electrochemical kinetics: Double layer capacitance, use of Butler-Volmer (BV) kinetic expression and its simplified forms, Fitting of BV, Ion transport in electrochemical systems; Electrode structure and configuration: Ideally polarized electrode, Micro electrodes, porous electrodes, gas liquid interface in porous electrodes; Electrochemical techniques: cyclic voltammetry, static techniques, electrodeposition; Battery fundamentals: Battery science fundamentals, cell chemistry, various types of batteries and their chemistry, Materials aspect in the battery; Corrosion: Fundamentals and thermodynamics of corrosion, types of corrosion, corrosion protections; In class demonstration: Analytical techniques, double layer charging, capacitor charging and discharging cycle etc.

name: Food Chemistry
description: 1. Description of Core Constituents of Foods: Understanding of biomolecular composition of raw fruits, vegetables and meats. Alteration in chemical constituents during standard food preparation (cooking/processing) approaches. Changes in bonding, composition and activity of water during food preparation. 2. Food Flavor, Color and Aroma: Molecular basis of flavor, aroma and color of foods; changes during standard food processing methods. 3. Biochemical transformations underlying food processing; implications on flavor, color and aroma. Production of food products with corresponding enzymes such as: high-fructose corn syrup (a-amylase, glucose isomerase), Cheese and dairy products (chymosin, b-galactosidase), Bread making/baking (groups of enzymes), Malt brewing (a-amylase, protease), fruit juices (pectic enzyme). Immobilized enzymes for analytical processes, starch to fructose conversion. Pasteurization of milk. 4. Additives, Contaminants and Adulterants: Molecular basis of additional ingredients that are intentional, incidental and/or hazardous additives. Introduction to chemical basis of preservative action. Established chemical measures of food safety. 5. Analytical Methods and Approaches: for study of composition, color, texture, flavor and aroma. Detection and quantification of hazardous substances. Introduction to global regulatory framework of food safety. Detection of chemical adulterants and detect antibiotic residues in foods (IR spectrometer), Colorimetric detection of pesticides and food additives (UV-vis spectrometer), pesticide residue and veterinary drugs in foods (LC-Mass analysis). 6. Hands-on Experiments: Adulteration in edible oil, added food colors/dyes and sugars in the food items and soft drinks can be tested /detected by simple demonstration based experiments. Students would be able to test the samples of food items/soft drink by simple instruments such as: polarimeter, chromameter and UV-visible spectrometer. The details are provided in Annexure II.

name: Fundamentals of analytical chemistry
description: Tools of Analytical Chemistry; Chemicals, Apparatus, and Unit Operations of Analytical Chemistry; Using Spreadsheets & Calculations Used in Analytical Chemistry; Errors in Chemical Analyses, Statistical Data Treatment and Evaluation, Sampling, Standardization, and Calibration; Chemical Equilibria, Aqueous Solutions and Chemical Equilibria. Effect of Electrolytes on Chemical Equilibria, Classical Methods of Analysis, Gravimetry, Titrimetry, Neutralization, Complexation; Electrochemical Methods, Oxidation-Reduction Titrations, Potentiometry, Electrogravimetry and Coulometry, Voltammetry; Spectrochemical Methods, Optical Spectrometry, Molecular Absorption Spectrometry, Molecular Fluorescence Spectroscopy, Atomic Spectroscopy; Kinetics and Separations, Kinetic Methods of Analysis, Introduction to Analytical Separations, Gas Chromatography, HPLC; Practical Aspects of Chemical Analysis

name: Physical Organic Chemistry
description: Structure and Bonding, Dipole moment, polarisability, Hyperconjugation; Tautomerism, Aromaticity in benzene and nonbenzenoid compounds; Acidity and Basicity,Bronsted/Lewis acids and bases, hard/soft acids and bases, nucleophilicity and basicity; pKa of organic compounds, structural effects of acidity and basicity; Addition, Substitution and Elimination Reactions,Nucleophilic addition to the carbonyl group, electrophilic and radical addition to alkenes, electrophilic and nucleophilic aromatic substitutions, S N 1 , S N 2 and S N i reactions, eliminations E1, E2 and E1cB reactions; Reactive Intermediates,Introduction to structure, formation, stability and reactions of carbocations, carbanaions, free radicals, radical anions, radical cations, arynes, carbenes and nitrenes,methods to determine the reaction intermediates; effect of structure on reactivity; Protecting groups, Introduction to the importance of protecting groups in organic synthesis.

name: Quantum Chemistry
description: Review of mathematical methods. Vectors, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues and ordinary differential equations; Solution of the Schrodinger equation for exactly solvable problems like particle in a box, particle in a ring, harmonic oscillator, one dimensional potential step and wells. Angular momentum; Hydrogen atom; Variation Method and Perturbation Theory; Valence bond and molecular orbital theories; Hückel approximation

name: Reactions and Mechanisms in Organic Chemistry
description: Substitution, Elimination and Addition Reactions: Nucleophilic aliphatic substitutions, Elimination reactions, Structural and solvation effect on reactivity, and Electrophilic, nucleophilic and free radical addition reactions. Electrophilic, nucleophilic, and Transition metal-catalyzed aromatic substitution reactions, and Aromatic radical substitution. Oxidation and Reduction: oxidation of alcohols, ketones and aldehydes (transition metal oxidants, peroxides and peracids etc.), allylic oxidation, oxidation of C-C double bonds (epoxides formation, ozone, KMnO 4 , OsO 4 etc.). Catalytic hydrogenation of functional groups, Group III hydride donor reagents (LAH, NABH4, DIBALH, B2H6), Groupd IV hydride donors (Silicon hydride and hydride transfer from Carbon). Functional Group Interconversion: Conversion of alcohols to alkylating agents, installation and removal of common protecting groups, Interconversion of carboxylic acid groups. Stereochemistry: Stereoisomers, Optical isomerism and Chirality, Resolution, Stereoselective/Setreospecific/Enantionselective reactions.

name: Advance Organic Chemistry
description: Rearrangement reactions: Electrophilic (Beckman, Hofmann, Lossen, Curtius, Wolff, Schimidt, Baeyer- Villiger, Pinacol-pinacolone, Wagner-Meerwein etc.), Nucleophilic (benzilic acid, Favorski), radical rearrangement (Wittig, aza-Wittig). Pericyclic reactions: Cycloaddition reactions, stereochemical aspect and applications of Diels-Alder reaction, 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition reactions, [2+2] cycloaddition reactions, electrocyclic reactions, overview of sigmatropic reactions, [1,3]-, [1,5]-, [1,7]- Sigmatropic shifts of hydrogen and alkyl groups, Sigmatropic rearrangements (Cope, aza-Cope, Oxy-Cope, Claisen, aza-Claisen, Eschenmoser-Claisen, vinyl- cyclopropane-cyclopentene). Photochemistry: General principles, cis-trans-Isomerazation, photoisomerization of 1,3-butadiene, photochemistry of carbonyl compounds, photochemistry of aromatic compounds, cycloaddition reactions, photochemical reactions of 1,4-butdienes.

name: Bioinorganic Chemistry
description: Biogeochemical cycles: Energy transduction between biotic and abiotic world (Emphasis on the carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen cycles); Transport and storage of metals in biology: Transferrin and Ferritin will be discussed in details by using iron as a model system; Metals in medicine: Development and current usage of various metal based anti-cancer agents and MRI contrast agents; Hydrolytic chemistry by metalloenzymes: Role of acid-base chemistry in biology. Examples of Carbonic anhydrase, Urease, and Acotinase will be discussed in details; Electron transport in biology: The basic concepts of electron transport through proteins. Roles of [Fe-S] clusters and Copper cofactors. Fundamentals of Photosynthesis, and Respiration; Detailed study of complex metalloenzymes: [NiFe]- and [FeFe]-Hydrogenase, and Nitrogenase.

name: Advanced Chemistry Laboratory
description: Column chromatography; Gas Chromatography (GC); High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC); Ultraviolet and visible absorption spectroscopy; Infrared spectroscopy; Fluorescence and phosphorescence; Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR); Mass Spectrometry; Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-Ray Powder Diffraction Analysis (XRD).

name: Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory
description: The Inorganic Chemistry Lab will have the following list of experiments: Synthesis of transition metal complexes: Objective is to learn synthetic skills to prepare coordination complexes of 3d metals [e.g. Ni(II) and Co(II)] from their metal salts. The experiment will also demonstrate the use of few topics “spectrochemical series” and “chelate effect” covered in theory course. Polarimetry: Experiment involves breakdown of disaccharide sugar (sucrose) into its components (glucose and fructose) and kinetic study of this process with the help of Polarimeter instrument. Optical rotation of organic substances (sugars) can be measured and in-turn the rate constant. Gravimetric analysis: Purpose is to learn basic principles of Gravimetry, an analytical method used for the quantitative estimation of analytes (metal present in the solution) by treating it with the reagents to give sparingly soluble products which can be precipitated and weighed. Complexometric titration: Basic principles of titrimetric method will be demonstrated and hardness of water can be calculated by the solutions of of Zn(II), Mg(II) salts and titrating them with EDTA (ethylenediamine tetraaceticacid), a chelating reagent. Recycling of aluminium: This “Green experiment” is used to demonstrate, how aluminium foils can be recycled by converting it to potash alum in the chemical laboratory. Estimation of phosphoric acid: Phosphoric acid is used as acidifier in “Cola drinks” and its amount can be determined by interesting chemical method using molybdenum complex and “Absorption spectrophotometer”. Back Titration: Direct titration of the aluminium ions in the solution is challenging, due to the slow formation of the metal complex with the chelating reagents and non-availability of suitable metal indicator. Thus, back titration of solution containing mixture of ions (Zn 2+ and Al 3+ ) with EDTA can be used effectively to determine the aluminium present in the solution. UV-visible Studies: Experiment includes measurement of optical density (OD) of the given set of metal complexes in solution, which can be correlated with their Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE in Octahedral geometry). Fluorescence Studies: This involves solution based emission studies of organic dyes prepared in the “Organic Chemistry Lab” and calculate the emission quantum yield with the help of a fluorescence standard. IR Spectral analysis of compounds: Experiment includes hands-on training of IR spectrometer and learn basic interpretation of IR spectra of metal complexes and selected organic compounds; which is a simple analytical tool to find out the functional groups present in the sample.

name: Organic Chemistry Laboratory
description: Synthesis of dibenzyledeneacetone- Experimental demonstration of aldol condensation for the carbon-carbon bond forming reaction. Synthesis of aspirin from salicylic acid- Acetylation of phenol using acetic anhydride. Methyl salicylate from aspirin- Esterification reaction of the synthesized salicylic acid using methanol and conc. sulfuric acid. Syntheis of Fluorescein-, Preparation of a classic fluorescent dye using phthalic anhydride and resorcinol. Reduction of ketone- NaBH 4 reduction of benzophenone /acetophenone in methanol. Synthesis of Chalcone- A solid state chemical reaction using the mechanical grinding method Diels-Alder reaction- One of the important pericyclic reaction, [4+2] cycloaddition of anthracene and maleic anhydride. Solvent extraction- Separation of an Acidic, a Basic and a Neutral Substance using solvent extraction will be performed. Interpretation of IR spectra of organic molecules- IR spectrum of the synthesized organic molecules will be recorded and the interpretation of the spectrum will be carried out to identify the organic molecules. Interpretation of NMR spectra of organic molecules- NMR spectrum of the synthesized organic molecules will be recorded and the interpretation of the spectrum will be carried out to identify the organic molecules.

name: Analytical and Computational Chemistry Laboratory
description: Analytical Chemistry Spectrophotometric Determination of Iron: The experiment will teach Complexometry, UV absorbance measurements of standards and unknowns. Identification of amino-acids using pH titrations: Estimation of Solubility Products of Silver Salts through potentiometric methods. Determination of Formation constants of Iron(III)-Glycine using spectrophotometry. Chromatographic separations: Students will learn the following techniques; Thin Layer, Paper chromatography, High Pressure Liquid Chromatography and Gas Chromatographic separation methods. UV and Fluorescence spectroscopy: Solvatochromic studies of dyes in various solvents. Theoretical Chemistry Density functional theory calculations: Identification of transitions states. Molecular Dynamics: Introduction to force fields and molecular dynamics simulation methodology. Molecular Docking: Identification of lead compounds.

name: Physical Chemistry Laboratory
description: Acetic acid adsorption over charcoal-Testing the physisorption models. Thermodynamics of charge transfer complexes- Estimating parameters such as enthalpy and entropy from an equilibrium reaction. Elevation in boiling point to calculate the boiling point elevation constant- Verifying this physical phenomenon by using a number of solute and solvent. Calculating the distribution constant and/or equilibrium constant of I 2 in aqueous medium- An equilibrium reaction between potassium iodide and iodine will be used to estimate the parameters. Electron transfer studies in Fe 3+ /Fe 2+ system using electrochemical techniques- Several techniques such as cyclic voltammetry will be used to understand the electron transfer process. Recording and interpretation of powder XRD pattern of an oxide materials- Specific oxide sample will be used to record the x-ray diffraction and its interpretation will be taught. Estimating the density of a crystalline powder using x-ray diffraction method- Powder x-ray diffraction pattern will be recorded and the lattice parameter estimation will reveal the density. Sol-gel synthesis of SiO 2 aerogels- Sol-gel synthesis will be utilized to make few amorphous and crystalline material and characterize Temperature dependent adsorption of CO 2 over solid materials- A detector based technique will be used to quantify the adsorption of gases such as CO 2 on adsorbent materials. Biodiesel synthesis using soybean oil and its characterization using TGA- Base catalyzed reaction will be used to synthesize the biodiesel. Due to specific combustion behavior, biodiesel can be easily characterized by TGA. Thermal decomposition behavior of carbonates, nitrates and hydroxides using TGA- Thermal behavior of the mentioned materials will be used to understand their decomposition behavior by TGA technique.

name: Group Theory & Spectroscopy
description: Symmetry and group theory: Introduction to group theory, properties of a group, Symmetry elements and symmetry operations, Symmetry point groups; Matrix representation of groups, properties of matrix representations, reducible and irreducible representations, character tables, Orthogonality relationships in group theory; Application of group theory in spectroscopy: Basic description of light-matter interactions using time-dependent perturbation theory approach, transition probability and selection rules; Molecular vibrations, Simple harmonic oscillator model and quantum mechanical solution, application of group theory to molecular motions, IR and Raman spectroscopy; Electronic spectroscopy, Molecular orbitals, selection rules, Emission of light: spontaneous and stimulated emission. Various applications in lasers and in imaging.

name: Organometallic Chemistry
description: Introduction to organometallic complexes: 18-electron "rule" and its exceptions; oxidation states, coordination number and geometry. -bonded & -bonded ligands: Metal alkyls, aryls and hydrides, alkene, alkyne and allyl complexes, cyclopentadienyl complexes. Fischer and Schrock carbenes, Carbonyls and Phosphine complexes Organometallic reagents: examples of Grubbs catalysts and Pd-catalysts and their applications in organic synthesis, olefin metathesis, CH activation. Role of metals in biology: metals in medicines, introduction to organometallic complexes and their role in biology /medicine.

name: Transition Metal Chemistry
description: Theories of Bonding in Coordination Chemistry (VBT, CFT), Metal d-orbital splitting in different geometries (using CFT) Jahn-Teller distortion with examples in different geometries. The Isomerism with examples in coordination complexes. Electronic Spectroscopy: Term symbols, Laporte and Spin transition rules. Orgel diagrams: Absorption spectra of transition metal complexes (3d series) Tanabe-Sugano Diagrams, Lanthanides and Actinides, Molecular magnetism Calculation of magnetic moments (μs, μJ): (in d-block, Lanthanides and Actinides elements), Effects of the structural geometry on magnetic moments.

name: AI in Chemistry
description: Fundamentals of machine learning for chemistry: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning with examples from molecular and reaction modeling; Chemical data representation and feature engineering: SMILES and molecular graphs, atomic and bond features, molecular descriptors and fingerprints using RDKit; Chemical informatics tools and databases: overview of key datasets (QM9, ZINC, PubChem) and open-source packages (scikit-learn, Chemprop, DeepChem); Machine learning for chemical property prediction: regression and classification models for thermochemical, electronic, and solubility data; model validation and benchmarking; Deep learning for molecular modeling: neural networks and graph neural networks for structure–property relationships; introduction to ML potentials; Generative and inverse design approaches in chemistry: overview of variational autoencoders, GANs, and diffusion models for molecular design

name: Chemistry of Natural Products
description: This is an advanced course once the students are familiar with basic synthetic chemistry principles. Reactions of heterocyclic compounds (which form the basic skeleton of natural products) includes imidazoles, oxazoles, thiazoles, pyridazines, pyrimidines and pyrazines, terpenes, alkaloids, antibiotics, carbohydrates, vitamins, nucleic acids and proteins. This course will also offer biosynthetic pathway of some important natural products.

name: Electrochemistry
description: Introductory principles in electrochemistry, Conductance, Transference number, ionic conductivity and Redox reactions, Electric double layer, different models, capacitance, Electron transfer and kinetics; Nernst Equation, Butler-Volmer equation, Tafel equation, Instrumental methods in electrochemistry, cyclic voltammetry and steady state techniques; Special Topics; electrocatalysis, batteries, and fuel cells.

name: Applied Chemical Biology
description: Inspiring properties of biological systems, Bio-inspired analogs and mimics, Classes of synthetic modifications and artificial self-assembling systems: nucleic acids (PNA, LNA), beta- peptides, artificial lipids and carbohydrates, Bio-inspired materials as sensors, Conjugation of biological molecules with micro- and nanomaterials, diagnostics & therapeutics inspired by biomacromolecules, bio-inspired molecules in imaging, Case studies of commercially viable bio-inspired entities.

name: Interpretative Organic Spectroscopy
description: UV–Visible Spectroscopy: Electromagnetic spectrum, basic principles, concept of chromophore, electronic transitions in organic compounds, Structure elucidation by UV-Vis and Woodward – Fisher rules; IR – Spectroscopy: Basic principles, IR as a tool for functional groups determination in organic compounds; NMR Spectroscopy: Basic principles. Introduction to NMR techniques – CW and FT NMR techniques, 1H NMR Spectral parameters – intensity, chemical shift, multiplicity, coupling constant; Analysis of first and second order spectra. Multinuclear NMR (with specific emphasis on 13C NMR): Proton coupled, off–resonance decoupled 13C NMR spectra. Structure determination by NMR: Assignment of chemical shifts, additively effect, characteristic chemical shifts of common organic compounds and functional groups, DEPT spectra. Introduction to multinuclear NMR of common hetero atoms present in organic compounds (N, F, O, P, D). 2 D NMR techniques Homo- & Hetero-COSY and NOESY spectra; Mass Spectroscopy: Basic principles, techniques of ion production and ion and daughter ions, molecular ion and isotope abundance, Meta-stable ions, Fragmentation pathways: Mc-Lafferty Rearrangement and fragmentation pattern of common chemical classes. Application of MASS for organic structure elucidation: determination of molecular formula and MASS spectra-structure correlation.

name: Selected topics in Physical Chemistry
description: Solid state Chemistry- Introduction to crystal structure, Bonding and electronic properties of solids, Defects and non-stoichiometry, Brag’s law and application, Descriptive solid state chemistry, Introduction to phase diagram, electrical and magnetic properties, symmetry elements and point groups. Molecular spectroscopy- Theories of spectroscopic; vibrational and rotational spectroscopy, photo electron spectroscopy, magnetic resonance spectroscopy. Surface chemistry- Heterogeneous catalysis, adsorption isotherms. Introduction to electrochemistry- Redox reactions, Electrode processes, Electric double layer, Electron transfer and kinetics; Nernst Equation, Butler-Volmer equation, Tafel equation, Instrumental methods in electrochemistry; cyclic voltammetry and steady state techniques; Electrode reactions with coupled chemical reactions, Special Topics; electrocatalysis, batteries, and fuel cells.

name: Asymmetric Synthesis and Catalysis
description: Introduction to asymmetric synthesis; chiral auxiliaries and chiral pool approach; Chiral resolution processes such as Kinetic Resolution (KR); Parallel Kinetic Resolution (PKR); and Dynamic Kinetic Resolution (DKR); Asymmetric catalysis like Biocatalysis; Metal catalysis and Organocatalysis. Application to the asymmetric synthesis of bioactive drug molecules.

name: Metabolism & Biosynthesis
description: Chemistry of Digestion and Absorption Processes: Digestion and absorption of carbohydrates, lipids and proteins; Solute transport; Solubility of substrates and products; Molecular chaperones for absorption Metabolic Pathways and Control: Location of carbohydrate, lipid and protein metabolism pathways with respect to one another; common metabolic sources and fates; compartmentalization of metabolic pathways; regulation of carbohydrate, lipid and protein metabolism; synthesis, storage and utilization of energy sources; thermodynamic relationships among pathways Biosynthesis: Synthesis of biomolecules (carbohydrates, lipids and proteins) from common building blocks; transport and regulation of synthetic precursors; common intermediates in synthesis of other biomolecules; synthesis of nucleic acid components Clinical Relevance of Metabolism & Biosynthesis: Health implications of abnormal metabolism and biosynthesis; energy metabolism and diet; selected diseases of metabolism; drug development for countering abnormal metabolism and biosynthesis.

name: Fluorescence Spectroscopy for chemists and biologists
description: Introduction to fluorescence, Absorption and emission processes, fluorescence markers and their characteristics, Environmental effects, molecular sensors and other photo-induced non-fluorescent states of fluorophores, polarization and rotational measurements of molecules, resonance energy transfer (FRET) and molecular distance measurements with fluorescence, ultra-sensitive fluorescence spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including single molecule spectroscopy and methods based on fluctuation analysis, applications of fluorescence spectroscopy in biology, medicine and drug development.

name: Medicinal Chemistry for life
description: This course would include Pharmacology, Molecular Pharmacology, Microbiology, Biochemistry, Physiology, Medicine and Pharmacy. Classification of Drugs, Mechanism of drug action at enzymes, Principles of drug discovery, Synthesis of important (historical) drugs and their biological applications.

name: Catalytic Chemistry
description: Introduction Basic modes of catalytic action, Classification and key concepts; Practical applications and Economic impact; Auto catalysis; Bio catalysis; Electro catalysis and Photo catalysis, Materials Perspective Desired characteristics, nature of the active site, metal support interaction, lattice oxygen and defect; Adsorption, adsorption mechanisms and models; Oxides, noble metals, and other materials as catalysts; Synthesis and Characterization of catalysts; Analytical techniques; Supports, Dopes, Alloys, Core-shell, Nanoparticles. Screening of catalysts Scientific and Engineering considerations Kinetics of Catalytic Reactions Strategies and designs for laboratory studies; Development of basic forms of rate equations. Deactivation and poisoning Mechanisms, models and design strategies

name: Solid State Chemistry and Applications
description: Structure and chemistry of solids: Diffraction techniques and analysis of diffraction data; Crystal defects, nonstoichimetry and solid solutions; Structure of solid electrolytes, conducting polymers and surfaces; Solid state transformations and reactions. Electronic and magnetic Properties: Band theory of metals, insulators and semiconductors, k space and Brillouin zones. Photodiodes, Avalanche photodiodes, piezoelectric effect; Magnetic moment, Curie law, Curie-Weiss law, Mechanism of magnetic ordering, Exchange Interaction, Domain theory, Hysteresis, Anisotropy, Ferromagnetism, Ferrimagnetism, Antiferromagnetism. Optical properties of solid: Polarizability, origin of refractive index, optical properties of dielectrics and metals, Surface plasmon resonance. Selected examples of materials, structures, properties and applications with respect to structure/property relations.

name: Single Molecule Spectroscopy
description: Review of interaction of matter and light; Transition dipole moment; Frank Codon principle; Photophysics of molecules; Introduction to microscopy, diffraction limit, numerical aperture. Various microscopy methods: confocal, wide-field, and wide-field TIRF; Excitation and detection of single molecules in solution and immobilized on surface. Systematic study of signals, noise, and signal-to-noise. Fluorescence correlation spectroscopy; Fluorescence resonance energy transfer; Detection of single molecules in a solid matrix a low temperature. Zero-phonon line, natural linewidth; saturation, spectral diffusion, Zeemn effect; Introduction to super-resolution imaging. PALM, STORM, and STED method for super-resolution localization; Application of single molecule imaging in biology, chemistry, soft matter; Application as single photon source in quantum optics.

name: Introduction to Molecular Dynamics
description: Provides an introduction to molecular modelling. Course covers the following topics. Empirical force field models: Molecular mechanics; Molecular Dynamics simulation method; Energy minimization methods for exploring the energy surface; Density Functional Theory. The course will introduce students to bio-molecular simulation programs CHARMM and NAMD. The Gaussian 09 package will be introduced to perform DFT calculations. Tutorial sessions will be included into the curriculum. The course requires a project to be completed for credits.

name: Chemical Crystallography
description: Introduction to chemical crystallography; Database approach for structure identification; Single crystal X-ray Diffraction Techniques; Small molecule crystallization; Crystal symmetry and lattice space groups; Data collection strategy and data integration; Data reduction, space group determination and structure identification; Oak Ridge Thermal Ellipsoid Plot (ORTEP) and Mercury GUI software; Crystallographic information file (CIF); Check CIF report for publication; Mercury software applications; Polymorphism and cocrystals in small molecules;

name: Statistical Thermodynamics and its Applications in Chemistry
description: Module I: Statistical Viewpoint Thermodynamics postulates; conditions of equilibrium; reversible processes and maximum work postulate; Maxwell relation; review of probability theory; phase space; microstates and configurations; Boltzmann distribution law; significant configurations; physical interpretation of the distribution law; variation of W with E; approach to thermal equilibrium; the free particle and the ideal gas; concept of entropy Module II: The Partition Function Definition of partition function; chemical equilibrium and partition function; formulation of partition function – multiple degrees of freedom, degeneracy, indistinguishable and multiple units; thermodynamic functions from partition function – energy, entropy, factorability and separability; Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions and their classical limit Module III: Evaluations of Partition Functions Translational partition function – ideal monoatomic gas, Sackur-Tetrode equation; multitude of translational states, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution; internal degrees of freedom – rotation, vibration, electronic excitation, potential energy function for a diatomic molecule; rigid-rotor harmonic-oscillator approximation; polyatomic gases; monoatomic crystals; molecular symmetry and relationship with partition function Module IV: Statistical Mechanical Ensembles Ensemble concept – basic postulates, types; microcanonical ensemble; canonical ensemble; grand canonical ensemble; analytical descriptions of the ensembles; equivalence of ensembles; fluctuations of thermodynamic properties Module V: Applications Intermolecular potential, Virial coefficients; heat capacities – Einstein solid, diatomic gases; the equilibrium state – dissociation of iodine, determinants of equilibrium; transition state theory – potential energy surface; distribution functions in classical monoatomic liquids

name: Organic Electronics
description: Molecular Crystals; Amorphous Molecular/Polymer Films; Electronic States of a Molecule; Atomic Orbitals to Molecular Orbitals; Orbitals to States; Transitions between States; Potential Energy Curve; Radiative Transitions: Absorption and Emission; Electronic Factor; Vibrational Factor; Spin Factor; Franck-Condon Factor and Energy Gap Law; Electronic Coupling; Accepting Modes, Promoting Modes, and the Isotope Rule; Basic Photophysical Parameters: Lifetimes and Quantum Yields; Molecular Excitations – Effects Due to Polarization, Statistical Averaging, Environment, and Couplings; Dimers and Excimers; Electronic Interaction in the Ground and Excited States; Förster and Dexter Energy Transfer; Creation of Charged Molecules by Injection, Absorption, and Doping; Charged Molecules in Disordered Films and Molecular Crystals; Electrical and Optical Gap; Amorphous Solids; Comparison between Inorganic and Organic Semiconductors; Charge Carriers and Excitons; Frenkel and Wannier Exciton; Injection and Extraction of Charge Carriers; Charge Transport in Organic Solids; Band Transport; Hopping Transport; Polaronic Transport; Disorder Controlled Transport; Trapping Effect; Impact of Charge Carrier Density and Morphology on Transport; Charge Carrier Recombination; Generation and Dissociation of Excitations; Diffusion of Excitons; Basics of Organic Light-Emitting Diodes; Device Structures; Fluorescent and Phosphorescent Emitters; Organic Solar Cells Based; Electron-Donors and Electron-Acceptor Materials; Bulk-Heterojunction Structure; Role of Morphology of the Active Layer; Operational Principle of Organic Field-Effect Transistors; Improving Charge Transport; Polarity; Ambipolar Transport.

name: Electronic Structure Theory
description: Overview: Quantum theory and origin of electronic structure; many-electron problem; Born-Oppenheimer approximation; periodic solids and bands; uniform electron gas and simple metals. Basis sets, linear algebra, and secular equation; Slater and Gaussian type orbitals; Wave-function based approaches: Hartree, Hartree-Fock, self-consistent field method; correlation corrections; configuration interaction theory; coupled cluster methods; many-body perturbation theory; Density functional theory: DFT foundations; Thomas-Fermi-Dirac approximations; Hohenberg- Kohn Theorems; intricacies of DFT; Kohn Sham variational equations; time dependent DFT; local spin density approximation; GGA; LDA; solving Kohn-Sham equations; Numerical implementations: basis sets; atomic pseudopotentials; Brillouin zone sampling; iterative diagonalization methods; software packages; Ground state properties of materials: Hellmann-Feynman theorem; stress tensor; geometry optimizations; electronic band structures; Fermi surfaces; Electronic excitations: time-dependent density functional theory; Bethe-Salpeter equation; optical spectroscopy; Hands-on component: Predicting properties of matter from electronic structure.

name: Advanced Main Group Chemistry
description: Periodic trends in main group chemistry; Alkali and alkaline earth metals: metal hydride, alkyl and aryl complexes, and their application in synthesis and catalysis; Boranes and carboranes, Low-oxidation state boron and aluminum compounds; Carbene (NHC and CAAC) and their heavier congeners; Multiple bonding in heavier main group elements; Structural anomaly in heavier main group elements; Noble gas and their molecular compounds; Frustrated Lewis Pairs (FLP) chemistry; Main group compounds in catalysis, solar cells, LED and as transparent conducting materials; Past, present and future of main group compounds in industry.

name: Bio-inorganic Chemistry
description: Biogeochemical cycles: Energy transduction between the biotic and abiotic world (Emphasis on the carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen cycles); Transport and storage of metals in biology: Transferrin and Ferritin will be discussed in detail by using iron as a model system; Metals in medicine: Development and current usage of various metal-based anti-cancer agents and MRI contrast agents; Hydrolytic chemistry by metalloenzymes: Role of acid-base chemistry in biology. Examples of Carbonic anhydrase, Urease, and Aconitase will be discussed in detail; Electron transport in biology: The basic concepts of electron transport through proteins. Roles of [Fe-S] clusters and Copper cofactors. Fundamentals of Photosynthesis, and Respiration; Detailed study of complex metalloenzymes: [NiFe]- and [FeFe]-Hydrogenase, and Nitrogenase; Small molecule activation and conversion by metalloenzymes: Diatomic and monoatomic oxygen incorporation into substrates: cytochromes, methane monooxygenase, dioxygenases, and spectroscopic characterization of their reaction intermediates. Nitrate reductase, Tungsten containing formate dehydrogenase; Artificial metalloenzymes in catalysis. (Newly added topics are in italics)

name: Chemistry of Energy Materials
description: 1. Introduction to Chemistry in the Energy Landscape (3 lectures) Global energy demand, Renewable vs Non-renewable energy, Role of chemistry in energy conversion and storage Materials of interest: Layered structures (MX₂, Ruddlesden-Popper, Dion–Jacobson (DJ), Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), Homologous Series), Tetrahedral frameworks (Zeolites, MOFs, Phosphates), Pyrochlore & Garnet, Intermetallic, Topological Materials, and others 2. Synthesis Methods for Energy Materials (3 lectures) Solid-state reaction, Sol-gel, Single-source precursors, Hydrothermal, Microwave, and Vapor-phase synthesis, Intercalation/deintercalation, Thin film deposition (CVD, ALD, PLD), High-pressure synthesis and Crystal growth, Nanomaterials 3. Selective Topics on Energy Conversion and Storage Technologies (18 lectures) (Working principles, state of art materials and their structure-property correlations) • Thermoelectric materials for waste heat recovery (4) • Hydrogen Energy (production, storage, catalysts) (4) • CO₂ storage and conversion technologies (3) • Topological materials for sustainable energy applications (2) • Solid-state batteries (Li-ion, Na-ion) (3) • Supercapacitors and next-gen energy storage devices (2)

name: Non-linear Spectroscopy and its Applications
description: • Time-dependent quantum mechanics (8 lectures): Schrödinger equation, time evolution Hamiltonian and operator, light-matter interaction, two-level system, time-dependent perturbation theory, Fermi’s Golden rule, Schrödinger and Heisenberg representations, Interaction picture. • Condensed-phase dynamics (6 lectures): mixed states and density matrix, fluctuations, time-correlation function, linear response theory, line broadening, spectral diffusions. • Applications and techniques (8 lectures): energy and charge transfer, quantum relaxation processes, linear spectroscopy, pump-probe spectroscopy, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC), fluorescence up-conversion, two-dimensional spectroscopy (2D NMR, 2D infrared spectroscopy, 2D electronic spectroscopy and 2D electronic-vibrational spectroscopy). • Hand-on learning (4 classes): UV-Vis, fluorescence, FTIR spectroscopy, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC).a

name: Chemical Process Calculations
description: History of Chemical Engineering; Applications of Chemical Engineering; Types of calculations performed in Chemical Engineering, Fundamentals of calculations: Units, data, errors and reporting; Types of processes; Material balances on non-reactive processes, processes involving purge, recycle and bypass streams, multiphase processes; Material balances on reactive processes; Combined material and energy balances; Computer applications in chemical process calculations; Case studies for industrial processes and non-industrial processes

name: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Maxwell Relations and Fluid Properties Estimation; Pure Component Phase Equilibria, thermodynamic properties; ideal gas mixtures, imperfect gases; liquid state and solution theories. Single Phase Mixtures and Solutions; Ideal Solutions; Partial molar quantities; Gibbs- Duhem Equation; Phase-Rule; Phase equilibrium criteria, non-ideal solutions, residual and Excess properties; Fugacity and Activity Coefficient models; vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) at low to moderate pressures; Raoult’s Law, Henry’s law High-Pressure VLE, Chemical Reaction Equilibrium

name: Process Fluid Mechanics
description: Review of Basics of Fluid Flow: Fluid Statics, pressure measurement; Basic equations of fluid flow, viscosity, newtonian and non-newtonian fluids, laminar and turbulent flows, Boundary layer theories; Bernoulli theorem and applications; Flow of incompressible fluids, friction factor, piping systems; Flow of compressible fluids, adiabatic and isothermal flows, sedimentation and flotation, centrifugal separation, packed beds and fluidized beds; Transportation and metering of fluids, pump types, pump curves, blowers and compressors, direct flow measurement (pitot tube, rotameter, orifice meter etc., indirect methods and commercial flow meters; Mixing and Agitation, power consumption, impeller types and flow patterns, mixing times.

name: Heat Transfer
description: Basic Concepts of Heat Transfer: Modes and laws of heat transfer, Conduction, heat transfer through extended surfaces, concept of resistance, Convection, boundary layer, heat transfer coefficient, overall heat transfer coefficient, LMTD; forced convection; natural convection; boiling and condensation; Radiation; Heat Exchangers: Classifications and applications of heat exchangers, fouling factor, basic concepts of heat exchanger design, Kern method, NTU methods, design considerations for heat exchangers

name: Chemical Reaction Engineering - I
description: Motivation; fundamentals of batch and flow reactors; conversion and reactor sizing; reactors in series; Isothermal reactor design; Collection and analysis of rate data; Reactor design with multiple reactions; Steady state non isothermal reactor design: energy balance equation to flow and batch reactor systems, design of adiabatic and isothermal reactors, equilibrium conversions, multiple steady states; Introduction to Residence Time Distribution (RTD)

name: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Maxwell Relations and Fluid Properties Estimation; Pure Component Phase Equilibria, thermodynamic properties; ideal gas mixtures, imperfect gases; liquid state and solution theories. Single Phase Mixtures and Solutions; Ideal Solutions; Partial molar quantities; Gibbs- Duhem Equation; Phase-Rule; Phase equilibrium criteria, non-ideal solutions, residual and Excess properties; Fugacity and Activity Coefficient models; vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) at low to moderate pressures; Raoult’s Law, Henry’s law High-Pressure VLE, Triangular phase diagrams. Chemical Reaction Equilibrium: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous reactions; Multi- reaction Equilibria; Combined Phase and Reactions Equilibria.

name: Fluid Mechanics Lab
description: Measurement of viscosity by efflux time; Type of flows (Reynolds Number apparatus), Verification of Bernoulli Theorem; friction in circular pipes; Flow measuring devices: orifice meter, venturimeter, rotameter; Equivalent length of pipe fittings; characteristics of centrifugal pump; Vapor pressure of liquid; infinite dilution activity coefficient; Vapor-liquid equilibrium; Calorimeter; calibration of thermocouple.

name: Chemical Reaction Engineering - II
description: Unsteady state ideal reactors (isothermal and non-isothermal), Non-ideal Reactors and Reactor Safety: Dispersion Models, The Tank in Series model, Convection and laminar flow, Segregation and RTD, Reactor Stability & Reactor Safety. Gas-Solid Reactions: Shrinking core and volume reaction models to treat External diffusion, Internal Diffusion & Reaction control reactions. Catalysis: Catalytic rate equation and catalytic reactor design, catalyst deactivation, Various fluid-particle reactors and its designs. Gas-Liquid Reactions: Rate equation for mass transfer and reaction limited reactions, Factors important to design Gas-liquid reactors and various gas-liquid contactors and its design aspects. Solid- Solid Reactions: Kinetic models and reactor design for solid processing units. Special Reactors: Bioreactions and Bioreactors, - Enzyme Fermentation and Microbial Fermentation, photoreaction and photoreactors, Vacuum and aerosol systems for material synthesis.

name: Separation Processes - I
description: Diffusion; Interphase mass transfer: theories of interphase mass transfer, local and overall mass transfer coefficients, correlations; analogy between momentum, heat and mass transfer; Gas absorption: Equipment for gas absorption/stripping, design of tray and packed tower design; Distillation: batch distillation, continuous fractionation-tray and packed towers, Tray hydrodynamics and efficiencies, special distillation techniques; Simultaneous heat and mass transfer: drying, humidification operations, design of cooling towers

name: Process Dynamics and Control
description: First principles model development; Process dynamics for first, second and higher order systems: linearization, transfer function models, effect of poles, zeros and time delays on system response; Empirical models from data; introduction to feedback control: objectives, PID control; analysis of closed loop systems: stability, root locus, frequency response using Bode and Nyquist plots; control design techniques: design criteria, time and frequency domain techniques, controller tuning; Introduction to advanced control strategies

name: Separation Processes - II
description: Extraction: Liquid-Liquid extraction-Calculations with and without reflux for immiscible and partially miscible system; Solid-liquid extraction; Adsorption and Ion Exchange: Types and nature of adsorption, Adsorption equilibria, stage-wise and continuous-contact adsorption operations, ion- exchange equilibria, ion exchange cycle and operation of ion exchangers; Crystallization: Crystal geometry, supersaturation, nucleation, crystal growth, crystallization equipment; Membrane Separation Processes: Gas separation processes, reverse osmosis processes; ultrafiltration, pervaporation, dialysis and electrodialysis. Supercritical fluid separation processes

name: Process Synthesis, Design, and Simulation
description: Heuristic methods for process flowsheet synthesis and design, Douglas hierarchical design procedure, material and energy balance for flowsheet development-unit models for linear mass balances, setting temperature and pressure levels from the mass balance, energy balances, energy integration and heat exchanger network using pinch point analysis, process diagrams (PFD, P&ID), Preliminary sizing and mechanical design of process equipment, cost estimation, engineering economics and profitability analysis. Simulation using software: Vapor Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) calculations, simulation of unit operations such as flash vaporization, reactors, distillation column, and simulation of process flowsheet.

name: Separation Processes
description: Gas absorption: Equipments for gas absorption/stripping, design of tray and packed tower design; Distillation: batch distillation, continuous fractionation-tray and packed towers, Tray hydrodynamics and efficiencies, special distillation techniques; Extraction: Liquid-Liquid extraction-Calculations with and without reflux for immiscible and partially miscible system; Solid-liquid extraction; Simultaneous heat and mass transfer: drying, humidification operations, design of cooling towers; Adsorption and Ion Exchange: Types and nature of adsorption, Adsorption equilibria, stage-wise and continuous-contact adsorption operations, ion-exchange equilibria, ion exchange cycle and operation of ion exchangers; Crystallization: Crystal geometry, supersaturation, nucleation, crystal growth, crystallization equipment;Membrane Separation Processes: Gas separation processes, reverse osmosis processes; ultrafiltration, pervaporation, dialysis and electrodialysis.

name: Chemical reaction engineering
description: Motivation; fundamentals of batch and flow reactors; general mole balance equation; rate laws and stoichiometry; conversion and reactor sizing; reactors in series; algorithm for data analysis from batch reactor and flow reactor data; experimental planning for data analysis; Isothermal reactor design: mole balance in terms of conversion, mole balance in terms of concentration and molar flow rates, effect of pressure drop in conversion, micro reactors, membrane reactors; Multiple reactions: selectivity and yields in series, parallel and complex reactions. selection and ordering of reactors for parallel, series and complex reactions; Steady state non isothermal reactor design: energy balance equation to flow and batch reactor systems, design of adiabatic and isothermal reactors, equilibrium conversions, multiple steady states; Catalytic reactors: catalytic reaction mechanisms, rate law for catalytic reactions, external diffusion, internal diffusion, design of packed bed reactor; Residence Time Distribution (RTD): fundamentals of non-ideal reactors, measurement and characterization of RTD, RTD for ideal reactor, non ideal reactor modeling using RTD

name: Introduction to Polymer Science and Engineering
description: Nature of macromolecules and basic concepts of Polymer Science and Engineering, Molecular forces and chemical bonding, Chain configuration and conformations, Molecular weight and molecular weight distribution; Polymerization, Condensation polymerization, Addition polymerization; Mechanical properties of amorphous polymers, Viscous flow, Rubberlike elasticity, Viscoelasticity, Glass transition; Macromolecules in solutions, Equilibrium thermodynamics, Extension to polymer blends and gels, Kinetics of phase separation, Dynamical systems involving polymer gels; Historical perspective and classification of polymer processing operations, Manufacturing processes, Orientation and structure development during processing, Models for processing techniques.

name: Transport Phenomena
description: Vectors and Tensors, Viscosity, Kinematics: Motion, streamlines, pathlines and streaklines, Molecular mechanism for transport processes, Chapman Enskog Theory. Shell balance; Governing equations of fluid mechanics: Equation of continuity, Momentum balance, Stress tensor, constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Thermal conductivity, Governing heat transfer equations by various modes: Energy balance, Temperature distribution, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions, Macroscopic balances. Concentrations and mass fluxes: Governing equations of mass transfer: Species mass balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Case studies and applications of momentum balance, energy balance and mass balance in various situations in the process.

name: Integrated Chemical Engineering Lab-I
description: Vapor pressure of liquid, Infinite dilution activity coefficient, Vapor-liquid equilibrium, Friction in circular pipes, Flow measuring devices: orifice meter, venturimeter, rotameter, Equivalent length of pipe fittings, Characteristics of centrifugal pump, Pressure drop in packed bed, Estimation of Thermal Conductivity, Pin Fin apparatus, Laminar flow heat transfer, Turbulent flow heat transfer, Heat transfer by natural convection, Heat transfer in agitated vessel, Plate Heat exchanger, Shell and Tube heat exchanger, heat transfer in fluidized bed, absorptivity of different colored substances.

name: Integrated Chemical Engineering Lab-II
description: Reaction in Batch reactor, Reaction in CSTR and CSTRs in series, Residence time distribution (RTD) in LFR, RTD in CSTR, Gas-phase diffusivity using Stephen tube, Solid-liquid mass-transfer coefficient, Differential distillation, Sieve plate distillation column, Forced convection batch dryer, Second-order dynamics of critically damped system-interacting tanks, second-order dynamics of critically overdamped system-non-interacting tanks, PID controller.

name: Chemical Engineering Practice in Industry
description: The Chemical Engineering Practice course aims to bridge the gap between academic learning and industrial application, focusing on the practical aspects of Chemical Engineering concepts. The course will consist of lectures delivered by invited instructors who are experienced professionals from the Chemical Industry. The lectures will cover case studies and current industry standards on: • Synthesis and production of organic and inorganic chemicals • Optimization of separation processes in industrial settings • Challenges in scaling up reactions and optimizing reaction conditions for yield/selectivity • Safety as well as design of industrial reactors • Process optimization and troubleshooting using control systems • Application of Chemical Engineering concepts in food industry • Production of specialty chemicals, catalysts, high-value products (essential oils, perfumes, etc.) • Production methods, processing techniques, and quality control in polymer industry • Process development, scaling up, technology transfer, and compliance with regulatory standards in pharmaceutical industry • Case studies on application of Chemical Engineering beyond the Chemical Industry (for example, semiconductors, biomedical devices, automobiles, aerospace, defense, etc.) and related technical challenges.

name: Heat Transfer and Thermodynamics Lab
description: Thermal conductivity of metal rod; Laminar flow heat transfer; Turbulent flow heat transfer; Heat transfer by natural convection; Finn tube heat exchanger; Plate Heat exchanger, Heat transfer in agitated vessel; heat transfer in fluidized bed; Gas-phase diffusivity by using Stephen tube; Solid-liquid mass-transfer coefficient.

name: Mass Transfer and Reaction Engineering Lab
description: Differential distillation; Sieve plate distillation column; packed bed gas absorption; Liquid- liquid extraction; Cooling tower; Forced convection batch dryer; Residence time distribution (RTD) in Laminar flow reactor (LFR); Residence time distribution (RTD) in Continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR); Batch reactor; Single CSTR and CSTRs in series; CSTR and LFR in series.

name: Introduction to Process Safety
description: Introduction to Process Safety: What is process safety? Difference between process safety and occupational safety, Importance in the chemical industry, Accident and loss statistics; learning from history, Brief on inherently safer design. Safety Regulations and Management Basics: OSHA Process Safety Management (overview), Key national and international standards (OSHA, ISO, OISD), Role of safety audits and compliance. Basics of Reactive Chemicals & Hazard Identification: Hazardous and reactive chemicals, reactor safety, Hazard surveys and simple hazard analysis, Introduction to HAZOP (basic concepts only), Real-world examples of hazard identification. Basics of risk analysis Fires and Explosions: Fire triangle and ignition sources, Flammability concepts (limits, diagrams), Types of explosions (VCE, BLEVE, dust explosions – basics only), Simple prevention measures (inerting, ventilation, explosion-proof equipment). Toxicology and Industrial Hygiene: Basic toxicological concepts: dose-response, TLVs, PPE basics and industrial hygiene principles, Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS). Accident Investigation & Case Studies: Simple accident investigation process, Root cause thinking, Brief review of major industrial accidents (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City, Flixborough) and key lessons.

name: Process Control
description: First Principles model development; Process dynamics for first, second and higher order systems: linearisation, transfer function models, effect of poles, zeros and time delays on system response; Empirical models from data; control system instrumentation; introduction to feedback control: objectives, PID control; analysis of closed loop systems: stability, root locus, frequency response using Bode and Nyquist plots; control design techniques: design criteria, time and frequency domain techniques, model based design, tuning; advanced control strategies: cascade and feed forward, introduction to multivariable control; controller implementation through discretisation.

name: Non-Linear Analysis
description: Introduction to nonlinear dynamics of physical systems with varying degrees of complexity; survey of a variety of concepts associated with flow, fractals & attractors, bifurcation phenomena, mappings, nonlinear oscillations, nonlinear modeling and forecasting, chaotic behavior, and strange attractors; Specific examples of nonlinear behavior from Engineering and Sciences.

name: Process Analysis and Simulation
description: Introduction to design, Balances in large chemical processes, Flowsheeting & Process Simulation Concepts, Process simulation programs (Aspen plus), Preliminary sizing and mechanical design of process equipment, Cost estimation, Engineering economics and profitability analysis

name: Process Synthesis and Design
description: Process Synthesis and Design, Heuristic methods for process design –(Douglas hierarchical design procedure, Onion diagram),Separation system synthesis, Energy integration and heat exchanger network,Process Optimization, Process diagrams –( PFD, P&ID), Safety analysis, Environmental and sustainability considerations, Capstone design project.

name: Biochemical Engineering
description: Introduction to Biochemical Engineering Who is a Biochemical Engineer – What do Biologists and Engineers have in common, Success Stories from the World of Biochemical Engineering (Penicillin to COVID vaccine) An Engineer’s Perspective on Biology – Microbial Diversity, Cell Structure and Construction, Cell Nutrients Overview of Enzymes – What are Enzymes and how do they function, Metabolic Pathways – Glucose metabolism, Respiration, Nitrogen Fixation Cell Kinetics – Cell growth in batch and continuous cultures, Cell growth phases, Factors affecting cell growth, Chemostats and its deviations Engineering Principles for Biochemical Engineering Operating Consideration for Bioreactors – Choosing the cultivation method, Chemostat with recycle, multistage chemostat, Fed-batch operation, Perfusion systems Immobilized Cultures - Cell immobilization, diffusional limitations Bioreactors – Mechanically stirred tanks, Bubble columns, Packed bed reactors, Airlift reactors, Microreactors, Scale-up, Scale-down Unit Operations in Biochemical Engineering Separation Processes in Biochemical Engineering - Filtration, Centrifugation, Coagulation, Flocculation Cell disruption – Ultrasonication, Osmotic shock, Freezing, Enzymatic digestion, Chemical solubilization Purification and Quantitation of Biochemical Products - Precipitation, Chromatographic, Electrophoretic, Aqueous Two-Phase Systems Product Storage – Stabilization and storage of proteins, Concentration, Ultrafiltration, Lyophilization, Dialysis Overview of Unconventional Approaches and Applications of Biochemical Engineering Circumventing Traditional Biochemical Processing - Extracellular protein production; Cell-free protein synthesis Applications of Biochemical Engineering – Medical Devices (artificial kidney), Gene therapy, Biological wastewater treatment, Biomaterials, Biofuels, Biosensors, Hybrid Materials Biochemical Engineering and Society - Bioethics, Regulating the use of Genetically Modified Organisms, Safety and Concerns Future – Where do we go from here? Moving towards a circular economy and a sustainable future involving synthetic meat

name: Formulation Science and Engineering
description: The course is focused on liquid, semisolid (paste-like) formulations where one or more components are dispersed in a solvent. The course will be taught in the form of modules, each module focusing on a formulation with a specific application. Module-I: Introduction to formulations design. Examples of formulations. Module-II: Suspensions: Formulations of solid/liquid dispersions. Understanding of inter-particle interaction potential, stability, and potential stabilizer. Discussion on properties of dilute, semi-dilute, and concentrated suspensions. Module-III: Emulsions: Formulations of liquid/liquid dispersions. Synthesis and thermodynamics of emulsification. Introduction to nanoemulsions, microemulsions, Pickering, and multiple emulsions. Stabilization and destabilization of emulsions. Discussion on properties of dilute, semi-dilute, and concentrated emulsions. Module-IV: Foams: Formulations of air/liquid dispersants. Surface force theory. Surface viscosity and elasticity. Foam films, foam drainage, foam collapse Module-V: Evaluation techniques for formulations after dilution Module-VI: Evaluating formulations without dilution. Discussion on toxicity evaluation of formulations

name: Quality Control in the Chemical and Pharmaceutical Industry
description: Introduction: Quality Assurance, Quality Control, Quality Management Systems, Cost of Quality, Role of Chemical Engineer; Regulatory Landscape and Compliance: Regulatory Agencies, Guidelines, Good Manufacturing Practices, Documentation; Quality by Design and Risk Management: Quality Target Product Profile, Critical Quality Attributes, Critical Material Attributes, Critical Process Parameters, Failure Modes and Effect Analysis, Ishikawa Diagrams; Process Analytical Technology: in-line, on-line, and at-line monitoring, Real Time Release Testing, sensors, sampling system design, integration of PAT in control loops, quality and regulatory implications of batch and continuous manufacturing; Validation and Qualification: Equipment Qualification, Process Validation, Cleaning Validation; Statistical Quality Control: Probability distributions and associated parameters for manufacturing systems, Statistical Process Control; Application of Artificial Intelligence in the Quality Management Systems.

name: Process Dynamics and Control Lab
description: First order dynamics of bulb thermometer; Second-order dynamics of under-damped system- pendulum; second-order dynamics of critically damped system-interacting tanks; second-order dynamics of critically overdamped system-non-interacting tanks; On-off controller (liquid-level control); PID controller (Temperature control); Adsorption; Reverse osmosis; High performance liquid chromatography; Liquid chromatography; UV-vis spectrophotometer.

name: Advance Transport Phenomena
description: Vector and tensor algebra, Calculus. Kinematics: Motion, streamlines, pathlines and streaklines.Governing equations of fluid mechanics: Equation of continuity, Momentum balance, Stress tensor, constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Unidirectional flows: steady and unsteady; Two-dimensional flows: Stream function. Limiting cases: Creeping flow, Inviscid flow, Boundary layer theory, Turbulent flow: Transition to turbulence, Turbulence models. Macroscopic balances. Governing equations of heat transfer: Energy balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions, Conduction, Forced convection, Natural convection. Radiation, Macroscopic balances. Concentrations, velocities and mass fluxes: Governing equations of mass transfer: Species mass balance, Constitutive equations, Boundary conditions. Complete solutions, Diffusion in gases, Diffusion and reaction, Forced convection, Simultaneous heat and mass transfer.

name: Advanced Thermodynamics
