description: Classical Thermodynamics: Introduction, Review of Basic Postulates, Conditions of Equilibrium, Legendre Transformation and Maxwell’s relations, Stability of Thermodynamic systems, First Order phase transitions and Critical Phenomenon (second order phase transitions) Phase Rule, Single component phase diagrams, Introduction to Multicomponent Multiphase equilibrium mixtures, partial molar properties, fugacity and activity coefficients, Ideal and Non-ideal solutions, VLE/SLE/LLE/VLLE triangular plots, Chemical Equilibrium and Combined phase and reaction equilibria. Statistical Thermodynamics: Mathematics-probability distribution, stirling approximations etc Canonical Ensemble, Microcanonical Ensemble, Grand Canonical Ensemble: Expressions for partition functions and their relation to thermodynamics variables Boltzmann, Monoatomic, Diatomic and Polyatomic Gases: Calculation of Partition functions and thermodynamics properties, Introduction to Classical Statistical Mechanics: Calculation of partition function, phase space, Liouville equation Crystals (Solids): Partition functions, the Einstein and Debye theory Intermolecular forces and potential energy functions, Imperfect Monoatomic Gases: Virial Equation of State, Second Virial Coefficient, Law of Corresponding states, Perturbation theory: Distribution functions.

name: Advanced Reaction Engineering
description: Review of fundamentals in chemical reaction engineering, Numerical solutions of reaction engineering problems, membrane reactors, micro-reactors, reactive distillation, unsteady state non-isothermal batch-semibatch-CSTR-PFR reactor design, chemical reaction with mass transfer, fluidized bed reactor, multiphase reactors, design of non-ideal reactor using residence time distribution data.

name: Colloidal Domain:Where Science meets Engineering
description: Forces in Colloidal Systems, Electrostatic Interactions in Colloidal Systems, Colloidal stability, Colloidal Sols, Surface Chemistry, Colloidal properties and characterization techniques of systems such as polymers, emulsions, amphiphiles.

name: Chemical Microscopy
description: Fundamentals of optics (reflection, refraction, dispersion, objectives, aberrations, illumination, image formation, resolving power, and image contrast);Polarized light microscopy (In-situ imaging, measuring anisotropy, melting point determination, contaminant identification, phase transformations);Phase contrast and interference contrast microscopy; Confocal microscopy coupled with fluorescence spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy for chemical mapping; Advanced techniques in fluorescence microscopy (Fluorescence Resonance Energy transfer, Fluorescence lifetime imaging, fluorescence recovery after photo bleaching, fluorescence correlation spectroscopy); Scanning probe microscopy (Near field optical microscopy, chemical force microscopy, scanning thermal and tunneling microscopy); Electron microscopy (field emission, combination with x-ray and auger electron spectroscopy ;Ultraviolet and Infrared microscopy for chemical imaging; Overview of microscopic practices

name: Particulate Solids: Processing & Surface Engineering
description: Dry synthesis of particles, Particle and bulk powder characterization: Particle size, shape, hardness, surface properties, nano-scale surface roughness, Surface energetics, Powder flow and packing properties, Dispersion properties of fine particles, Mechanical properties of powder bed; Powder processing: Storage and flow of powders through hoppers – hopper designing, Separation of particulate system from gas – gas cyclone, Pneumatic transport of powder materials, Mixing and segregation of particulate solids, Flow through packed bed of particulate solids – fluidization, Reactor with suspended solid particulates – the bubbling fluidized bed (BFB), the K-L model for BFB, Circulating fluidized bed, Heat transfer in fluidized bed, Granulation, Powder sintering; Inter-particle forces and surface engineering of particulate solids; Particle engineering through surface coating, plasma processing, size reduction, design and formation of composite particles for advanced applications in pharmaceuticals, food, propellant, aerosols, cements, fertilizers, cosmetics and fine chemicals etc.; Hazards of fine particle handling: Explosion hazards of fine powders, Health effects of fine particles.

name: Liquid State Theory
description: Recap of statistical mechanics: Microstates and macrostates, canonical ensemble, isothermal-isobaric ensemble, grand canonical ensemble, introduction to the types of simulations. Functionals: Introduction to functionals and their differentiation, functional derivatives of the grand potential. Distribution functions: Introduction to Distribution functions, direct correlation functions, density response functions, Classical density functional theory. Diagrammatic methods: Diagrammatic expansions of direct correlation functions, Introduction to virial expansion of the equation of state. Integral equation theory: Yvon-Born-Green hierarchy, Ornstein-Zernike equation, hypernetted chain approximation, Percus-Yevick approximation, mean spherical approximation. Time-dependent correlations: Properties of time correlation functions, Langevin equation, linear response theory, time-dependent fluctuations, estimation of the macroscopic transport coefficients. Molecular liquids: Molecular pair distribution function, Site-site distribution functions, Reference Interaction Site Model (RISM) equations. Inhomogeneous liquids: Free energy functionals for liquids at interfaces, application to liquid-vapor interface, calculation of surface tension, density functional theory of wetting.

name: Fundamentals of Aerosol Science
description: The proposed course encompasses aerosols' properties, transport, and transformation that one would need a firm hold on to mitigate undesired aerosols and engineer desired aerosols or nanoparticles via the aerosol pathway.

name: Catalyst Design for Heterogeneous Reactions
description: 1. Introduction to heterogeneous catalysts: prominent industrial thermal catalysts; unconventional catalyst synthesis techniques – vacuum synthesis, autoclave methods, thermal methods 2. Fundamentals of heterogeneous catalysis: turnover-rate; tools (mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, titration) and methods of catalytic measurements; transient and steady-state measurements; mass transfer limitations and catalyst loss 3. Basics of chemical bonding and reaction barriers • Free energy/ scaling relationship 4. Understanding elementary catalytic steps: pressure dependence; kinetic isotope measurements; steady-state isotopic transient kinetic analysis 5. Catalyst design: • Can the ligand effects and ensemble effects be decoupled? • Ex situ tools • Composition, importance of particle and crystallite sizes, morphology, acidity/basicity (NH3 adsorption), heats of adsorption (TPD, isothermal calorimetry, differential scanning calorimetry) • Surface science studies of crystalline catalysts; Measure of reaction barriers • In situ tools • Importance and challenges, current status; time-resolved measurements and effective spectroscopic cell design • Spectroscopic technique selection rules; X-ray techniques, electron beam techniques, light techniques (IR, Raman, UV-vis) and other techniques • Operando measurements • Set up and limitations, structure-function relationships, compatibility with in situ spectroscopic techniques • Case-studies of in situ and operando measurements – strength and pitfalls

name: Pharmaceutical Crystallization
description: Basics of crystallization: Solubility, solid-liquid equilibrium, supersaturation, metastability, nucleation, crystal growth, crystal structure, crystal habit, population balance, control of particle size and morphology, application of crystallization in pharmaceuticals and foods

name: Advanced Separation Processes
description: Fundamentals of Separation Processes: An overview of mass transfer fundamentals such as driving forces, and mass transfer coefficients, overview of basic separation processes (gas absorption, distillation, extraction etc.) and their limitations; Membrane Separation Processes: Membrane types, materials, synthesis, and characterization. Transport mechanisms (e.g., solution-diffusion model) and membrane modules, Pressure-driven processes such as microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), and reverse osmosis (RO), Concentration polarization and membrane fouling, Other membrane techniques like dialysis, electrodialysis, gas separation, and pervaporation; Adsorption and Chromatography: Adsorption equilibria (e.g., Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms), Adsorbents and ion exchangers, Chromatographic techniques, retention theory, and design, Advanced methods like pressure swing adsorption (PSA), temperature swing adsorption (TSA), and simulated moving bed (SMB) chromatography; Supercritical Fluid Processes (SFE): Properties of supercritical fluids (typically supercritical CO2) and phase equilibria, Working principle, advantages over conventional solvents, and industrial applications (e.g., extraction of essential oils from seeds, spices and flowers); Advanced and Hybrid Distillation Techniques: Multicomponent distillation, Enhanced distillation methods, including azeotropic and extractive distillation. Reactive and catalytic distillation, combining reaction and separation in a single unit for process intensification, Molecular distillation and short-path distillation for processing heat-sensitive materials. Pressure swing distillation; Advanced Crystallization: Basics of crystallization, principles of melt crystallization and fractional crystallization, Techniques like zone melting and freeze crystallization, Supercritical Fluid Crystallization processes

name: Applications of AI in ChE
description: Foundations of AI and Chemical Engineering (Overview and AIs transformative role in chemical engineering, Understanding AI paradigms: machine learning, deep learning, and hybrid approaches, Practical applications including process control, optimization, and materials discovery); Data Handling and Preparation (Nature of chemical engineering data: process measurements, molecular data, and time series, Techniques for data preprocessing—cleaning, normalization, and feature extraction, Exploratory data analysis and visualization tools to reveal underlying patterns); Classical Machine Learning Methods (Key supervised learning algorithms: linear and nonlinear regression, classification (Support Vector Machines (SVM), decision trees, ensembles), Model selection strategies, validation techniques, and performance measurement, Applications: Prediction of thermophysical properties of solutions); Unsupervised Learning and Dimensionality Reduction (Clustering techniques—k-means, hierarchical, density-based clustering of applications with noise (DBSCAN)—and their uses in process monitoring, Dimensionality reduction methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and t-distributed stochastic neighbour embedding (t-SNE) for handling complex chemical data, Applications in anomaly detection and development of soft sensors for chemical reactors); Deep Learning in Chemical Engineering (Introduction to neural network architectures including Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Tasks such as reaction prediction, fault detection, and image analysis in chemical processes, Addressing overfitting, generalization, and interpreting deep learning models); Integrated Modelling, Physics-Informed Machine Learning, and Optimization (Hybrid Modelling: Combining mechanistic first-principles models with data-driven AI to create robust, efficient process simulators and surrogate models, Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML): Embedding physical laws, constraints, governing differential equations directly into machine learning models, physics-based feature engineering, custom loss functions incorporating PDE residuals, neural network architectures constrained by process physics, Applications focus on modelling complex multi-physics phenomena in chemical reactors, including momentum, heat, and mass transfer, nonlinear chemical kinetics, Optimization and Control: Implementing AI for real-time process optimization, predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and adaptive control strategies. Use of model predictive control (MPC) frameworks and reinforcement learning for scheduling and decision-making.

name: Statistical Mechanics of Liquids*
description: Recap of statistical mechanics: Microstates and macrostates, canonical ensemble, isothermal-isobaric ensemble, grand canonical ensemble, introduction to the types of simulations; Functionals: Introduction to functionals and their differentiation, functional derivatives of the grand potential; Distribution functions: Introduction to Distribution functions, direct correlation functions, density response functions, Classical density functional theory; Diagrammatic methods: Diagrammatic expansions of direct correlation functions, Introduction to virial expansion of the equation of state; Integral equation theory: Yvon-Born-Green hierarchy, Ornstein-Zernike equation, hypernetted chain approximation, Percus-Yevick approximation, mean spherical approximation; Time-dependent correlations: Properties of time correlation functions, Langevin equation, linear response theory, time-dependent fluctuations, estimation of the macroscopic transport coefficients; Experimental estimation of distribution functions; Applications of machine learning.

name: Theory of Computation
description: Finite Automata and Regular Languages: Deterministic and Nondeterministic finite automata and their equivalence, Pumping lemma, the Myhill-Nerode Theorem; Pushdown Automata (PDA) and Context free languages (CFL): Context free grammars (CFG), normal forms, variants of pushdown automata, equivalence of PDA and CFG, pumping lemma for CFLs; Turing Machine (TM) and Computability: Variants of TM and their equivalence, Decidability and Undecidability, Halting problem, reductions, Rice’s theorem; Complexity Theory: Time Complexity, P, NP, many-one reductions, NP-completeness, Cook-Levin theorem, Space Complexity.

name: Software Tools and Techniques for CSE
description: • Introduction to version controlling: local, centralized, distributed. • Implementation of Agile development methods: Continuous integration/delivery/deployment. e.g., Git workflow, actions etc. Source code differencing algorithms in Git: myers, minimal, patience, and histogram. • Makefiles and build tools: Dependency rules, macros, suffix rules etc. build tools e.g., Gradle, Apache Maven etc. • Rapid application development strategy: introduction to event-driven programming and its structure, object based programming using windows form controls and event handling. • Data and connectivity mechanisms: Data binding in windows forms, connecting controls to data sources, displaying, and updating data. • Concept of Debugging/profiling: Visual studio debugger, GNU debugger, Linux perf – stat, trace, sampling and off-cpu profiling, hotspot UI frontend.

name: Software Tools and Techniques for AI (STT-AI)
description: 1. Data collection and labeling: a. collecting data: tools for logging/instrumenting sources (e.g. website) to collect usage data; b. validating data c. labeling data: tools such as label studio to annotate datasets, methods such as Cohen Kappa inter-annotator agreement, snowball sampling, active learning for cost-effective data annotation, d. data augmentation 2. Model and code reproducibility and versioning: a. architecture and hyperparameter search: tools such as weights and biases for architecture and hyperparameter search and various AutoML tools such as AutoGluon b. model checkpointing: tools such as MLFlow, Tensorboard/Weights, and Biases for model/weights checkpointing c. data versioning; 3. Self-contained environments: tools such as Docker for managing released packages; cloud platforms such as GCP/AWS/Azure; 4. Run-time profiling and optimization (e.g., memory footprint, measuring GPU utilization and optimizing it). 5. Continuous Integration/Co: A/B tests; Distribution, Covariate shift— data version management and retraining to account for shifts; Redeployment 6. Deployment on constrained devices: tools such as TF-lite, topics like quantization, etc.

name: Operating Systems
description: Operating System basics: OS Evolution, History and Design Principles. Processes and Threads: Kernel/User Level Operations; Thread/Process Synchronization. System Calls and APIs; Interrupt Handling; CPU Scheduling. Memory Management: Virtual Memory; Address Spaces and Translations; Segmentation and Paging; role of TLBs in Virtual Memory; Other caching techniques. Concurrency and Synchronization: Race Conditions; Locks; Semaphores; Concurrent Data Structures. I/O Systems and Storage Devices: Hard Disk Drives(HDDs); Solid State Devices(SSDs); File Systems Design Principles and Implementation; Maintaining Data Integrity. Miscellaneous: Virtualization; Introduction to Distributed Systems. Case Studies: Linux/Unix.

name: Theory Of Computation
description: Finite Automata and Regular Languages: Deterministic and Nondeterministic finite automata and their equivalence, Pumping lemma, the Myhill-Nerode Theorem. Pushdown Automata (PDA) and Context free languages (CFL): Context free grammars (CFG), normal forms, variants of pushdown automata, equivalence of PDA and CFG, pumping lemma for CFLs. Turing Machine (TM) and Computability: Variants of TM and their equivalene, Decidability and Undecidability, Halting problem, reductions, Rice's theorem. Complexity Theory: Time Complexity, P, NP, many-one reductions, NP-completeness, Cook- Levin theorem, Space Complexity.

name: Mathematical Foundations for AI
description: 1. Refresher Module · Data representation (floating point arithmetic, underflow, overflow) · Linear Algebra · Calculus (univariate calculus) · basic probability 2. Multivariate Calculus and Automatic Differentiation · Gradient, Jacobian, Hessian · Finite difference methods and limitations · Automatic gradient computation · Continuous Multivariate Probability distributions · Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) · Conjugate Priors 3. Optimization · Continuous optimization: i. Taylor’s series and connections to optimization methods ii. Unconstrained Optimization: 1. Gradient descent. 2. Gauss-Newton methods and variants.

name: Compilers
description: Overview of Compiler; Brief discussion on Assembler, Linker, Loader Lexical Analysis; Syntax Analysis: Parsers, Top-down, Bottom-UP, LR Parsers, Parser generators; Syntax directed translation; Intermediate code generation and type checking; Run-time Environments; Type checking; Code generation and optimization (data flow analysis, instruction optimization, loop optimization, memory optimization etc). Tools : Flex, Bison (or similar software).

name: Introduction to Data Science
description: Overview of data representation (as vectors, sets, graphs), basics of feature creation (e.g. document to sets/vectors); probability basics, central limit theorem, probabilistic inequalities and concentration bounds; Singular Value Decomposition/PCA and best-fit subspaces, low- rank approximation, eigenvectors and power method; mixture models (gaussians, document- topic models, graph models); applications of matrix factorizations to mixture model learning; Modeling and estimation; maximum likelihood, Bayesian estimators, regression, sampling methods (e.g. unbiasing, bootstrapping, confidence intervals); hypothesis testing (basics and augmentations e.g. non-parametric, multiple hypothesis testing, Bayes Factor); Unsupervised methods; clustering (k-means/median, kmeans++); graph analytics: ranking methods (pagerank, HITS) and connection to random walks, algorithms for community detection and connection to eigenvalues, triangle counting; Introduction to memory efficient approximate computation, Bloom filters, approximate counting (Morris’ algorithm, Count Sketch, Count-Min sketch, AMS sampling); Distributed paradigms for big data e.g. MapReduce, introduction to Hadoop and Spark (examples e.g. pagerank and kmeans); Ethical and privacy issues in data science, introduction to privacy models; introduction to causality and counterfactuals, bucket testing;

name: Foundations of AI: Multiagent Systems
description: Introduction to AI. Flavors of AI: strong and weak, neat and scruffy, symbolic and subsymbolic, knowledge-based and data-driven. The computational metaphor. What is computation? Church-Turing thesis. The Turing test. Searle's Chinese room argument; Representing Knowledge. Production rules, monotonic and non-monotonic logics, semantic nets, frames and scripts, description logics; Algorithmic, game-theoretic and logical foundations of multi-agent systems. Distributed optimization and problem solving, non-cooperative game theory, learning and teaching, communication, social choice, mechanism design, auctions, negotiation, coalitional game theory, logics of knowledge and belief, collaborative plans and social systems; Fairness, Ethics, and Bias. Algorithmic fairness: Individual fairness, Group fairness. Applications of fairness in AI: Fairness in natural language processing, Fairness in computer vision, Deepfakes, Federated learning.

name: Operating Systems
description: Operating systems basics: Evolution, history and design principles; Processes and threads: Kernel/user level operation, thread/process synchronization, system calls and APIs, interrupt handling, CPU scheduling; Memory management: virtual memory, address spaces and translations, segmentation and paging, TLBs and other caching techniques; Concurrency and synchronization: Race conditions, locks, semaphores, concurrent data structures; I/O systems and storage devices: Hard disk drives, solid state devices, file systems design principles and implementation, maintaining data integrity; Advanced topics: Virtualization, distributed systems.

name: Computer Networks
description: Introduction to Computer Networks: Layered Network Protocol Architectures: OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Overview of Telecommunications, Cellular networks & Internet Architecture; Physical and Link Layers: Basics of communications; Delay, Loss and Throughput. Physical media types and their important bandwidth and bit-error-rate characteristics; Wired and Wireless media including copper cables, optical fiber and wireless. Framing; Error control: Bit-parity, CRC and Hamming Codes; Medium Access Control (MAC): Shared media systems; Bus, Star and Ring topologies; TDMA, FDMA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Ethernet and IEEE 802.3; IEEE 802.11 including CSMA/CA protocols; Shared and Switched Ethernet; Introduce protocols such as ARP, RARP and ICMP; Network Layer: Design Principles: Protocols, Addressing and Routing. Study of Internet Protocol (IP) suite; Hierarchical network architectures; IPv4 and IPv6 addressing and headers; Routing protocols: distance-vector and link-state approaches; Interior and Exterior Gateway Protocol concepts; Routing Algorithms including Dijkstra's algorithm and distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm; Example protocols: OSPF, RIP, BGP; Transport Layer: Connection oriented and Connectionless protocols: TCP and UDP; Details of TCP: Buffer Management, Flow Control and Congestion control; Study of TCP variants such as Reno, Tahoe, Vegas and CUBIC. Reliable transmission and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go-back-N, Selective Repeat; Application Layer: Study of Protocols and Applications: DNS, FTP, HTTP, and SMTP. Socket Interface and Socket programming; Advanced topics and recent trends in Computer Networks and Future Internet: Information Centric networking, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Network Function Virtualization (NFV), Internet of Things (IoT), Docker and Container Networking (CNIs), Programmable switches, OVS, and SmartNICs; Miscellaneous: Introduction to Datacenter Networks, Different RFCs; Case Studies: Linux/Unix TCP IP Stack; Network Simulators: NS-3, and Mininet; Optional: NS-2, OMNet,

name: Ethics of AI
description: Introduction to AI Ethics and Accountability - Discuss the critical role ethics play in AI research and deployment, emphasizing the potential societal impacts and the necessity for responsible innovation to prevent harm and promote trust. -Identify stakeholders responsible for AI outcomes, including developers, organizations, and policymakers, and discuss the allocation of accountability. Algorithmic Bias and Fairness - Introduction: Case studies on biased AI systems and explore sources of bias. - Fairness Definitions: Sufficiency, Separation and Calibration and their relationship with each other and accuracy - Fairness Approaches: Explore one in-processing, pre-processing and post-processing approach with application to real-world dataset for mitigating bias Privacy - Case studies on Data Privacy - Reconstruction and Membership Inference Attacks - Introduction to Differential Privacy - Differentially Private - Stochastic Gradient Descent Interpretability - Motivation and Goals for Interpretability - Overview of Model Agnostic Methods - Overview of Model Specific Methods. - Neural network interpretation Global Perspectives and Regulations - International policies and guidelines on AI ethics. - Comparative analysis of AI regulations across countries. - The role of organizations in setting ethical standards.

name: Computer and network security
description: Software security. Basics of OS; OS security; injection vulnerabilities; buffer overflows; access control; sandboxing; malware: viruses and worms; writing secure code. Web security. Web basics; web security model; cross-site scripting; SQL injection; session managements with cookies; https protocol. Network security. Basics of networking; security of TCP and DNS protocols; firewalls, VPNs and intrusion detections; denial of service (DoS) attacks. Brief overview of mobile security

name: Databases
description: Introduction to RDBMS; Structured Query Language (SQL); Relational Algebra; Entity-Relationship Model; Relational Database Design; Storage and File Structure; Application Development; Indexing and Hashing; Query Processing; Query Optimization - Transactions (Serializability and Recoverability); Concurrency Control; Recovery Systems; Introduction to no-SQL databases.

name: Computer Networks
description: Introduction to Computer Networks: Layered Network Protocol Architectures: OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Overview of Telecommunications, Cellular networks & Internet Architecture. Physical and Link Layers: Basics of communications; Delay, Loss and Throughput. Physical media types and their important bandwidth and bit-error-rate characteristics; Wired and Wireless media including copper cables, optical fiber and wireless. Framing; Error control: Bit-parity, CRC and Hamming Codes. Medium Access Control (MAC): Shared media systems; Bus, Star and Ring topologies; TDMA, FDMA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Ethernet and IEEE 802.3; IEEE 802.11 including CSMA/CA protocols; Shared and Switched Ethernet; Introduce protocols such as ARP, RARP and ICMP. Network Layer: Design Principles: Protocols, Addressing and Routing. Study of Internet Protocol (IP) suite; Hierarchical network architectures; IPv4 and IPv6 addressing and headers; Routing protocols: distance-vector and link-state approaches; Interior and Exterior Gateway Protocol concepts; Routing Algorithms including Dijkstra's algorithm and distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm; Example protocols: OSPF, RIP, BGP. Transport Layer: Connection oriented and Connectionless protocols: TCP and UDP; Details of TCP: Buffer Management, Flow Control and Congestion control; Study of TCP variants such as Reno, Tahoe, Vegas and CUBIC. Reliable transmission and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go- back-N, Selective Repeat. Application Layer: Study of Protocols and Applications: DNS, FTP, HTTP, and SMTP. Socket Interface and Socket programming. Advanced topics and recent trends in Computer Networks and Future Internet: Information Centric networking, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Network Function Virtualization (NFV), Internet of Things (IoT), Docker and Container Networking (CNIs), Programmable switches, OVS, and SmartNICs. Tutorials will also include hands-on programming and problem solving. Miscellaneous: Introduction to Datacenter Networks, Different RFCs. Case Studies: Linux/Unix TCP IP Stack. Network Simulators: NS-3, and Mininet. Optional: NS-2, OMNet,

name: Software Engineering and Testing
description: Basics of software engineering: software versus product; why software engineering; development styles; Software development life cycle: models; classic; waterfall; agile; scrum; devOps; Phases: requirement analysis; feasibility study; software design; software architecture; coding; testing; release; maintenance; Types of testing: unit; system; regression; white box; grey box; black box; Software maintenance: evolution; change analysis; test-case selection; prioritization; reduction; debugging; fault localization, version control; Software reverse engineering: decompilation; binary lifting; Advanced topics: differential testing; domain specific testing, flaky tests, test parallelization, test dependency detection.

name: Human-Computer Interaction
description: Introduction: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI); the role of an effective design (example of good and bad design) in the interaction design process; Concepts: describes major design principles associated with HCI e.g., visibility, flow, affordance, metaphors, feedback, consistency etc.; Understanding users and data gathering: Overview of how to plan and run successful data gathering sessions including interviews, design questionnaires and run observations; Requirements formulation: describes how to interpret results from data gathering sessions into requirements for design, explains the process and purpose of literature reviews and goes through how to develop scenarios and use cases; User interfaces and modalities: introduction to the many different types of user interface and modalities, and how they are used; Design, prototyping and construction: explain why to prototype and how to construct prototypes (low and high-fidelity), and how to begin construction based on designs; Evaluation: why is evaluation important, goals, methods, case study; Data analysis: discuss the qualitative and quantitative data and describes how best to analyze the results from data gathering sessions; Group course project (low/high-fidelity prototype) submission and presentation; Future directions: what is next for HCI and interaction design?

name: History of Computing and its Applications to Domains
description: Introduction: A Historical Perspective of Computer Systems - Hardware, Firmware and Software. Key inventions and Proliferations. Development of computer networks, Internet and computing platforms - the philosophy of computers, necessity and innovations, including the history and evolution of Indian ICT. Advancements: Proliferation of Computers in various domains of science and engineering, business, finance, healthcare, medicine, finance and governance. Evolution of Utility computing, Clusters, Grids, Data Centers, Cloud. Cybercrimes and Cybersecurity, Policies, Computer Ethics and Societal Impact. Future Prospects: Quantum Computing, Quantum Communication. Reflections on the lessons learnt by the History and evolution of computing.

name: Combinatorics with Applications in Computer Science
description: Elementary Counting Techniques, averaging, pigeonhole principle, inclusion-exclusion, double counting; Extremal Set Theory, Sunflower Lemma, Intersecting Families, Chains and Antichains; The Linear Algebra Method, Dimensionality Arguments, Orthogonality and Rank Arguments, Eigenvalues and Graph Expansion, Polynomial Method, and Combinatorics of Codes; The Probablistic Method, Linearity of Expectation, Second Moment Method, Lovász Local Lemma, Random Walks, Derandomization. Ramsey Theory: Ramseyan Theorems for Numbers, Hales–Jewett Theorem.

name: Advanced Data Structures
description: Amortised Analysis, Fibonnaci heaps, Splay Tree, ScapeGoat Tree, Lower Bound for Binary Search Tree, Tango Trees, Geometric view of BST, Successor Problem: van Emde Boas, Link-Cut Trees, Euler Tour Trees, Dynamic Connectivity, Lower Bound, Tries, LCA Query, String Matching, Suffix Tree, Suffix Array, Fault Tolerant Data Structures: Stack, Linked List, BFS Structures, Reachability. Hashing : Universal, Simple Tabulation, Cuckoo. Cache Oblivious Data Structures: B-Tree. Succint data structures: binary trees, rank, select.

name: Algorithms
description: Review:Greedy, Dynamic Programming, Applications of BFS and DFS, Shortest Paths, Data Structures. Network Flow: Max-Flow Min-Cut Duality Theorem, Preflow-push algorithm, Applications. NP-completeness: Complexity class P and NP, Polynomial-Time reductions, Examples. Approximation Algorithms: Greedy approximation, Local search, rounding, primal dual. Randomized Algorithms: Skip lists, hash tables, algebraic techniques, coupon collector problems.

name: Computer Systems
description: Introduction to Instruction Set Architecture (ISA); RISC vs. CISC; Performance Metrics; In-Order and Out- of-Order pipelines; Data and Control Hazards; Exception Handling; Cache Hierarchies; Cache Coherence; Virtual Memory; Memory Hierarchy; Multiprocessor Systems; Simultaneous Multi-Threading; Disks and Flash Memory; Performance Benchmarking; Case Studies – Intel; Operating System basics; Processes and Threads; Thread/Process Synchronization; CPU Scheduling; Memory Management; I/O Systems and Storage Devices; File Systems; Virtualization; Case Studies – Linux/Unix; Networking and Internet; Name Resolution and Routing; Naming Schemes; Inter-process Communication; Remote Procedure Calls; Distributed File Systems; Co-Ordination and Agreement; Replication; Case Studies – Google/Facebook services

name: Natural Language Processing
description: Text processing fundamentals: Tokenization, Stemming, Spell Correction, etc.: Language Modeling: N-grams, smoothing; Morphology; Parts of Speech Tagging; Syntax: PCGFs, Dependency Parsing; Distributional Semantics; Topic Models; Lexical Semantics; Word Sense Disambiguation; Information Extraction: Relation Extraction, Event Extraction; Applications: Text Classification, Sentiment Analysis, Opinion Mining, Summarization; Deep learning for NLP; Representationl Learning

name: Advanced Algorithms
description: ● Approximation Algorithms: Greedy and combinatorial methods; Local search; Dynamic programming and approximation schemes; Linear programming rounding methods (randomized, primal-dual, dual-fitting, iterated rounding); Semidefinite program based rounding; Metric methods; Hardness of Approximation; Unique Games ● Randomized Algorithms: Game-theoretic techniques; Moments and deviations; Tail inequalities; The probabilistic method; Markov chains and random walks; Algebraic techniques. ● Parameterized Algorithms: Bounded-Depth Search Trees; Kernelization; Iterative Compression; Color Coding; Divide and Color; Treewidth; Introduction to W-hardness and the hardness of kernelization. ● Exact Algorithms: Branching; Dynamic programming over subsets; Inclusion-Exclusion; Algebraic techniques; Measure and Conquer; Local Search; Hardness results conditioned on SETH and ETH. ● Suggested Problems: Vertex Cover; Set Cover; Feedback Vertex Set; Sparsest Cut; k-Cut; Multiway Cut; Facility Location; Satisfiability and variants; Constraint Satisfaction

name: Distributed Systems and Cloud Computing
description: Introduction to Distributed Systems: Fundamental concepts of processes and synchronization; locks and barriers; Semaphores and Monitors, communication concurrency control. Logical Clocks, Vector Clocks and Clock Synchronization. Lab exercises based on synchronization using Go programming language. Distributed Consensus: Transactions and Leader election Algorithms. Fault tolerance: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) /BASE (Basically Available, Soft-state, Eventual consistency) properties, (Consistency, Availability, Partitioning) CAP theorem, Replication and Redundancy models. Lab exercises based on fault tolerance using Go programming language. Interaction: Message passing interface (MPI), remote procedure call (RPC) and rendezvous; (manager/workers, heartbeat, and pipeline), probe/echo, broadcast, token passing, and replicated servers. Interconnection topologies, load balancing, memory consistency model, scalability and performance studies, scheduling, storage systems. Process/VM Migration. Security of distributed systems and data sharing, authentication and authorization, protocol security. Cloud Computing: Characteristics and components of Cloud, service and deployment models, hybrid & multi-cloud architecture, virtual machines, containers and serverless computing. Among recent advances in Distributed Stream Processors, Google Cluster Architecture, Amazon Lambda, Kubernetes. Lab exercises on Kubeless/Kubernetes/Serverless Platforms. Labs will include hands-on programming in the Go programming language and working with different distributed tools and platforms.

name: Computational Complexity Theory
description: ● Fundamentals: Definition of Turing Machines, Time and Space, Diagonalization, Hierarchy Theorems. ● Poly-time (P), Nondeterministic poly-time (NP), and completeness: Definitions of P and NP, Turing reductions and many-one reductions, NP-completeness of Satisfiability (SAT). ● Space complexity: Logspace (L) and Nondeterministic Logspace (NL), Savitch’s Theorem, logspace reductions, NL-completeness of directed graph reachability, NL=coNL (coNL indicating complement of NL), PSPACE (polynomial space). ● Randomness and Interaction: Bounded-error probabilistic polynomial-time (BPP) and randomized-polynomial time (RP), Polynomial Identity Testing (PIT), Valiant-Vazirani Theorem, Polynomial hierarchy (PH) and its characterizations, PSPACE (polynomial space), PSPACE-completeness of Quantified Boolean Formulas (QBF), interactive proofs (IP), Arthur-Merlin classes, IP = PSPACE. ● Non-uniformity: Boolean circuits, Nick’s class (NC), Matching in Randomized NC (RNC), Karp-Lipton Theorem. ● Cryptography and Complexity: One-way functions and Pseudorandom generators. ● Counting: #P, Toda’s theorem, The permanent ● Circuits: Alternating circuits of constant depth (AC0), Alternating circuits with counting (ACC0), Threshold circuits (TC0), Nick’s class level 1 (NC1) etc., AC0 lower bounds. ● Branching programs: Barrington’s Theorem, Monoid programs. ● Exact complexity: Exponential-time hypothesis (ETH) and Strong exponential-time hypothesis (SETH), sparsification lemma. ● Randomness vs Hardness: Derandomization implies circuit lower bounds.

name: Theoretical Foundations of Machine Learning
description: Concentration of measure – various concentration inequalities; PAC learning, VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, generalization bounds, sample complexity. Generalization in deep learning setting – double descent; Optimization – random projection, randomized numerical linear algebra and its applications in efficient optimization (e.g. regression, matrix factorization, second order descent); Beyond the worst-case analysis of machine learning algorithms – algorithms for non-negative matrix factorization, tensor decomposition, sparse recovery, dictionary learning; Kernels and their applications in ML --- kernel trick, Representer theorem, Support vector machine with kernels, Green’s theorem, Mercer’s theorem, Bochner’s theorem; learning kernels; Subset selection and its variants --- using submodularity, using importance sampling (coresets). Applications in data valuation, continual learning, and in optimization; Introduction to Gaussian Process (GP), neural network-GP equivalence, neural tangent kernel; Learning in stochastic settings -- Introduction to online learning and multi-armed bandits; Gittin’s index, Thomson sampling, adversarial bandits, contextual bandits.

name: CS Theory Toolkit
description: Logic: Propositions, connectives, quantifiers, rules of inference, induction, strong induction, structural induction.

name: Incentives and Machine Learning
description: 1. Introduction to Incentives a. Multi-agent Interactions and Equilibrium b. Mechanism Design - VCG c. Optimal Auctions d. Ad Auctions

name: Ethics of AI
description: Algorithmic Fairness and Accountability, where students study operationalized fairness criteria, constrained optimization for fair models, and the socio-technical dimensions of bias through case studies in vision and NLP; the Interpretability and Explainability module introduces inherently interpretable models, post-hoc explanations (LIME, SHAP, Integrated Gradients, SmoothGrad), counterfactual and concept-based explanations (TCAV, Network Dissection), and connects interpretability with fairness; Mechanistic Interpretability and Representation Engineering delves into circuits, compiled transformers, and emergent reasoning in large models (e.g., Chain-of-Thought prompting, interpretability in diffusion and vision-language models); Robustness and Adversarial ML explores vulnerabilities in AI systems, the history and theory of adversarial examples, adversarial training, robustness–accuracy trade-offs, and the security of large models under adversarial or distributional shifts; Privacy and Security in AI explores data protection, membership inference, reconstruction attacks, and the theoretical and practical aspects of Differential Privacy and DP-SGD; Ethics of Large-Scale AI Systems examines the societal, legal, and governance implications of LLMs and generative models, including transparency, accountability, and global regulatory trends.

name: Technopreneurship: Product Proposition
description: The course intends to promote contextual innovation that is feasible and marketable. The students will get an opportunity to apply the theoretical knowledge of engineering to design a solution that works. The course structure and pedagogy will require creation of functional teams within students, simulating professional practice This course also aims to integrate entrepreneurial activity with the academic curriculum and promote innovations that are technologically strong. Design process to be central to the stage wise progress; cyclic iterative development will be integral to development of the product solution. Various tools and methods to find a focused issue and lead towards an effective solution will be employed suitably. Topics covered ▣ Introduction to business innovations - Drivers of successful innovations (usability, desirability and feasibility) - Opportunities for business innovations (organization structure, product offerings, customer experience) and their intersection with product innovation ▣ Define the project brief: opportunities for product innovation - Defining a need statement that leads to the investigation in target market segmentation - User profile: Investigate and define demographics of the target user as applicable. - Environment/ context: establishing the seasonal, geographical, social and environmental needs etc. as applicable - Prior art and/or existing solutions/trend analysis in the market and potential gap areas ▣ Concept development: iterative, cyclic design process - Application of design thinking tools such as mind maps, fishbone diagrams to be used for data mapping and analysis - Ideation (multiple ideas based on the opportunities): use of design methods such as Brainstorming/ SCAMPER / Synectics for ideation. - Consolidation of concepts using progressive sketches (schematic >> product concept) and low fidelity mock-ups using materials available around ▣ Product proposition - Consolidated proposition with user, context and design opportunities - Medium fidelity prototype(s) as a proof of concept: the prototype(s) should explain the working of the product and interaction with the user (combined or separate as required)

name: Creativity, Design and Doing
description: Design as a phenomenon: The meaning of design as perceived by an ‘informed’ user, lay person, designer, artist and the one who sells a designed product; Craftsmanship: Design in relation to Craftsmanship / the object culture / the built environment. Design as a social, political and environmental act; Why design? Doing more with less energy, resources and time. Design for comfort, convenience, pleasure, productivity, ease of operation, safety, equity; How does one design? The process from conceptualization to concretization; What is creativity? The importance of design thinking and creativity; Design and skills: Be informed, imagine, visualize, sketch, draw, simulate and make.

name: Technopreneurship: Product Development + Business Strategy
description: The course will involve a major project; where the students work parallely on multiple fronts to make the product solution ready for business incubation. Introductory sessions for each topic and mentoring will be provided to the students. The project is driven by student teams; seeking mentorship as they face roadblocks. Timely evaluations and feedback from a curated panel of members from the industry and business experts are integrated in the structure.

name: Visual Design for Academia
description: Overview of various types of visual presentation artifacts required by academia; Planning the visuals; Types of content and corresponding types of graphics; Visual representations of information: Mind maps and concept maps; Principles of the gestalt theory of visual perception, which include: similarity, continuation, closure, proximity, figure/ground, and symmetry; Typography: types and categories of fonts, weights, kerning, leading, spacing, using appropriate typography for the respective graphic; Color theory: meaning, associations, color schemes, contrast, etc; Copyright information for graphics available online: Licenses and their scope; Visualization and representation of research data: Principles of data visualization, types of data representation, and applying visual design principles while presenting; Student independent projects for designing academic artifacts: Diagrams, Posters, Presentations.

name: Information Design for eLearning
description: Types of eLearning such as learning objects, simulations, short courses, tutoring systems and MOOCs; Writing learning goals; six levels of Bloom’s taxonomy; writing assessment questions for any eLearning module at different levels of the taxonomy; Principles of backwards design and constructive alignment; designing e-learning material by aligning learning goals and assessment tasks with learning activities; eLearning Design principles emerging from learning sciences and instructional design such as understanding learners, role of prior knowledge, long and short term memory and importance of feedback; eLearning Design principles emerging from learning sciences and instructional design such as drill and practice, discovery learning, scaffolding and collaboration; Multimedia principles for designing effective multimedia for their eLearning that can help improve cognitive processing; Basics of visual communication in the context of eLearning, such as typography, colour, layouts, etc.

name: Fundamentals of 3D Modeling and Animation
description: The course follows a spiral learning structure where core concepts—form, light, composition, and motion—are introduced through simple analog experiences and revisited in digital form with increasing complexity. Each spiral loop integrates both tactile and digital modes of learning, allowing students to first explore ideas through drawing, paper modeling, origami, or shadow play, and then translate those understandings into 3D modeling and animation using latest tools such as Maya, 3DS Max, and Blender. Introduction to form and space through sketching and observation of basic 3D shapes. Exploration of structure by constructing geometric forms using folds and surfaces. Understanding 3D environments, navigation, and creating simple shapes in a virtual space. Developing complex forms using transformations, editing techniques, and modifiers. Studying the behavior of light and shadow through observation and model interaction. Applying lighting techniques to create depth, focus, and contrast in modeled scenes. Composing visual scenes by arranging elements with attention to balance and hierarchy. Using viewpoint and framing tools to guide visual storytelling within the scene. Exploring surface qualities through materials, colors, and textures. Investigating the principles of motion through simple sequencing and visual timing. Animating objects with transformations to convey movement and rhythm. Final project development, rendering, and presentation of animated scenes.

name: Technology and Design Situations
description: Technological influences on design: From Bauhaus to AI; From Human Factors to Human Actors (The four waves of design): Productcentric, Human Centered Design (Human Factors), Social and Cultural factors in design, Life Centered Design; Human-Machine Interactions and its (re)configurations; Technology as Design Material; Technology as Tool, Agent, and Organism; Design Frict ion; Speculative Design; Critical Design; Ludic Technologies.

name: Systemic Approach for Design Field
description: Wicked problems; multidisciplinary approach; systems thinking; collaboration; design; sustainability; circular approach; small interventions; contextual enquiries; stakeholder identification; solution proposals; teamwork; interdepartmental involvement; problem solving; system complexity; communication; executive aspects; seamless functioning; industrial systems; industrial revolution; Industry 4.0; IoT; AI-based decision making; service design evolution; product-service relationship; brand concept; marketing; innovation; technology-management-design integration

name: Design Process and Methodology
description: What is design?; Design and Society: Significance of design, Role of a designer, Design History: Global and India; Design Appreciation: Cultures around Design, Artefact analysis, Trends in Design; Introduction to design processes: Problem Identification, Primary Research Methods, Problem-Framing, Exploration, Creativity, and Divergence techniques, Convergence; Drivers of Innovation: Manufacturability, Usability and Viability of design solution; Iterative and/or cyclic nature of design process

name: Elements of Design
description: Elements of design. Structural parts: point, line, shape, 3-D forms. Colors, Textures, Patterns, Space, Typography, etc. Principles of visual compositions: Emphasis, Balance, Rules of third, Contrast, Scale, Harmony, Structural aspects such as symmetry, visual grids, Layouts, etc. Visual Hierarchy, Grouping, Gestalt laws. Elements of interaction design, Principles of Interaction and Media, Formal analysis

name: Electronic Devices
description: Properties of Semiconductors: Introduction to semiconductors; Energy bands, semiconductor in equilibrium, charge carrier in semiconductors, intrinsic, extrinsic semiconductors, Fermi-Dirac statistics, charge neutrality, carrier transport, drift, diffusion, excess carriers in semiconductors; Fundamental semiconductor devices: Semiconductor diodes - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-ideal characteristics; Metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistor - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-idealities characteristics; Bipolar junction transistor - Basic structure, operation, I-V characteristics, ideal and non-ideal characteristics; Special semiconductor devices: Optoelectronic devices and modern semiconductor devices (for example: light emitting diodes and lasers; solar cells, multi-gate devices: FinFETs and nanosheet FETs, power mosfets, HEMTs, other advanced electronic devices, etc.).

name: Electrical Machines
description: Transformers: Role of transformers in power system, working principle, single phase transformer, equivalent circuit, voltage regulation, losses and efficiency; three phase transformers; autotransformer; instrument transformers; Linear and rotating machines: Principles of electromechanical energy conversion – forces and torques in magnetic field system, field energy and co-energy; DC Machines: Applications, constructional details, generating and motoring modes, classification of machines, terminal characteristics, losses and efficiency, starting and speed control of DC motors; Synchronous machines: Generator – Three phase EMF generation, circuit model, terminal characteristics, voltage regulation, parallel operation and load sharing, Motor – creation of travelling magnetic field, starting methods, speed control; Induction Machines: Induction motor – Workhorse of industries, constructional details, working principle, circuit model, terminal characteristics, starting methods, speed control; Special machines and applications: Single-phase induction motor, permanent magnet motors (BLDC and PMSM).

name: Power Systems
description: Introduction to power systems: Electricity supply value chain - generation, transmission, and distribution; Key stakeholders involved in electricity business; Power and energy outlook in India; Modeling of power system components; Circuit fundamentals, Active and reactive power calculations, Three-phase circuits, Single line diagram and per unit (PU) system representation; Transmission line parameters and their calculations; Transmission line modelling and performance analysis – short, medium and long transmission lines; voltage regulation and transmission line efficiency; Power flow analysis – network modeling, power flow solution methods: Gauss seidel (GS), Newton-Raphson (NR) method and DC power flow ; Economic operation of power system – optimal dispatch of generation, automatic generation control (AGC), and frequency control; Short circuit analysis – symmetrical faults, symmetrical components and sequence networks, unsymmetrical fault analysis; Power system stability – swing equation and equal area criterion of stability; Introduction to electricity market operation and framework, smart grid and renewable energy integration, electricity tariff and billing.

name: Unveiling the Semiconductor World
description: Theme Topic Introduction Overview of the Semiconductor Ecosystem & Course Roadmap System Architectures & IC Design IC & SoC design: RISC-V, AI Accelerators EDA & IP for IC Design Tools & IPs for Chip Design (EDA, CAD, PDKs) Semiconductor Manufacturing & Fabrication Wafer fabrication, process node advancements, chip manufacturing System Packaging & Assembly OSAT services, 2.5D & 3D packaging, heterogeneous integration Compound Semiconductors & Power Electronics GaN, SiC-based devices for power and RF applications Automotive & EV Semiconductors ADAS, BMS, power modules for EVs RF & Wireless Communication Chips RFIC, mmWave, 5G/6G chipsets Memory & Storage DRAM, NAND flash, SSDs, emerging memory technologies AI, Edge Computing & Custom Hardware AI accelerators, edge inference, neural processors Semiconductor Equipment & Materials Lithography, deposition, etching, CMP, fab consumables Secure & Defense Semiconductor Solutions Secure microelectronics for defense and aerospace Photonics & Optical Communication Chips Silicon Photonics & Hybrid Integration, Co-packaged Optics (CPO) Wrap-Up & Career Opportunities Careers in Semiconductors + Q&A

name: Semiconductor Devices
description: Overview of Semiconductor Devices, History & current Industry; Introduction to Semiconductors: Bandgap, Doping, Electrons and Holes in Semiconductor, Temperature dependence, Carrier Transport (Drift and Diffusion), Basic Semiconductor Equations; PN Junction Diode: Physics and Operation, Zener Diode, Optoelectronic Devices like Photodiodes and Light Emitting Diodes; Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT): Physics and Operation, Heterojunction Bipolar Transistors (HBTs); Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (MOSFET): Physics and Operation, Modern MOSFETs like FinFET, GAAFETs; Memory Devices: Volatile and Non-volatile Memories, Flash Memory Devices, Introduction to Emerging Non-Volatile Memories.

name: CMOS Circuit Design
description: Overview of CMOS technology and applications, Semiconductor ecosystem: Design to tapeout, MOSFET I-V characteristics, Threshold voltage, regions of operation, Capacitances in MOSFETs, CMOS inverter introduction, DC transfer characteristics (VTC), Noise margins; Circuit simulation basics, introduction to SPICE models and simulation of inverter and logic gates; λ-based layout design rules, Stick diagrams, Inverter layout & simulation, Rise/Fall Time, Dynamic Power, Energy-Delay Product; logical effort introduction, Delay and power analysis, Logical effort method for delay estimation, fan-out and sizing, NAND, NOR, XOR, transmission gates, Logic gate sizing, Layout and delay trade-offs, Latch, D-FF design using transmission gates and CMOS logic, AOI/OAI gates, Basics of Interconnects, Overview of CMOS Common-source amplifier, gain and frequency response, Current mirrors, Differential Amplifiers, OTA, 6T SRAM bitcell sizing, Read/write path, stability, Sense amplifier basics, memory subsystem.

name: Electromagnetic Waves
description: Review of static electric and magnetic fields; electromagnetic (EM) waves and applications; Transmission lines: concept of distributed elements, transmission line equations, phase and attenuation constants, propagation constant and characteristic impedance, lossless, low-loss and distortion-less lines, travelling and standing waves; reflection coefficient and SWR, input impedance, impedance matching – quarter and half-wave lines, equivalent reactive elements, load impedance measurement, analysis of open-circuited and short-circuited lines, stub matching, power flow in a transmission line, maximum power transfer condition, graphical representation of a transmission line, Smith chart, transmission line calculations using the Smith chart, pulse propagation, various types of transmission lines; Maxwell’s equations, displacement current, time-varying potentials, Lorentz conditions, boundary conditions at media interface; EM wave propagation - in lossy dielectrics, in lossless dielectrics, in free-space and in conductors, skin effect and skin depth, intrinsic impedance, complex permittivity and loss tangent, power flow and the Poynting vector, phase and group velocity, reflection of EM waves; Waveguides: parallel plate waveguide, rectangular and cylindrical waveguides, cut-off frequency, TE, TM and TEM modes; EM radiation and antennas: retarded potentials, Hertzian dipole, short loop antenna, antenna characteristics and radiation parameters, Friis equation, standard antennas – dipole, array, aperture and horn.

name: Engineering Electromagnetics
description: The EM spectrum, technological importance of each of the spectral bands, material properties and responses to frequencies; Freespace and guided waves; Maxwell’s equations as generalization of circuit laws, lumped and distributed circuit representations, power and energy in waves and relation to power and energy in circuits; Linearity and reciprocity, boundary conditions; The transmission line and its distributed circuit model, reflection and standing waves, impedance matching and impedance transformation, Smith chart, microwave circuits and networks. Relevance of TL concept to power system transients, IC interconnects and RF ICs; The waveguiding of EM energy, metallic and dielectric waveguide, modes, cutoff and evanescence. Phase and group velocity, rectangular and circular waveguides. Relevance of waveguide concept to optical fiber links and silicon photonics. Basics of EM radiation, performance metrics of antennas including directivity, gain and bandwidth, common antennas and applications, Dipole and aperture antennas, antenna arrays.

name: Communication Systems
description: Components of a communication link, channel signal to noise ratio, channel capacity; Modulation and demodulation; Analog and digital communication links; Review of signals and spectra, signal vector space; Review of random processes and power spectral density; Analog communication Baseband vs carrier communications, Amplitude modulation, QAM, Angle modulation (PM, FM); Narrowband and wide-band FM. Transmission bandwidth analysis, signal generation and detection; Frequency division multiplexing; Pulse code modulation, sampling theorem, aliasing, analog-to-digital conversion and quantisation noise; Uniform and non uniform quantisation, time division multiplexing; delta modulation, DPCM; Digital communications basics, Baseband and carrier-modulated digital, various line codes and their PSD, pulse shaping, inter-symbol interference, Nyquist ISI criterion, Duo-binary signalling and differential encoding; Noise analysis - Gaussian, Rayleigh and Rician distributions; Eye diagrams, timing extraction, Detection - non-coherent and coherent, BER analysis of PCM; Digital bandpass modulation, ASK, PSK, FSK, BER analysis.

name: Analog Circuits
description: Review of BJT and MOS transistor operation; BJT and MOS transistor biasing schemes, small signal models; CE/CS, CB/CG and CC/CD amplifiers; BJT and MOSFET high frequency models, SPICE models and simulation; frequency response of CS amplifiers; Miller’s theorem; current mirrors; active loads; cascode amplifier; differential amplifiers; feedback topologies and properties, stability, Barkhausen criterion, effect of feedback on amplifier poles, Bode plots, gain and phase margins; positive feedback and sinusoidal oscillators, Wien bridge oscillator, other op-amp based RC oscillators; multivibrators, square and triangle waveform generation; precision rectifier circuits; filter types and specifications, Butterworth and Chebychev filters first and second order filters, second order LCR resonators, active filters based on inductor replacement; data conversion, D/A converter circuits, A/D converter circuits.

name: Analog & Mixed Signal Circuits
description: Analysis of Linear Systems and Non-linearity in Analog Electronics: Review of Electronic Components, Two-port Matrix Analysis, Sources of Noise, Non-linearity in Amplifiers, Dynamic Range, Signal-to-noise Ratio, Fundamental Relationships between Power, Speed and Accuracy; Operational Amplifier Circuits (Analog Filters): Introduction to Operational Amplifiers; Transfer Function and Frequency Response; Poles, Zeros and Stability Analysis; Low Pass, High Pass, Band Pass, Band Stop and All Pass Filters; Continuous Time and Discrete Time Filters; Oscillators and Waveform Generators: Positive and Negative Feedback; Barkhausen’s Criterion; Sinusoidal Oscillators; RC Phase Shift Oscillator; Wien Bridge Oscillator; Other OpAmp based RC Oscillators; Relaxation Oscillators; Monostable and Astable Multivibrators; Phase-Locked Loops: Principles and Properties (Acquisition/lock, Phase Noise); Basic architecture and Building blocks; Applications of PLL: Up/Down Conversion, Frequency Synthesizers, FM Demodulation, Clock and Data Recovery; Analog-to-Digital and Digital-to-Analog Conversion: Principles and Properties of Data Conversion (Resolution, Linearity, Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)); Sampling and Quantization; Building Blocks: Sample & Hold/Track & Hold; ADC Architectures: Nyquist and Oversampling A to D converters; DAC Architectures

name: Digital Signal Processing
description: Module 1: Discrete Time Signals and Systems Introduction To Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Signals and Systems, Linear Phase

name: Electrical Machines
description: Transformers – working principle, single phase transformer, equivalent circuit, voltage regulation, losses and efficiency; three phase transformer; autotransformer; instrument transformers. Principles of electromechanical energy conversion – forces and torques in magnetic field system, field energy and coenergy; DC machines – constructional details, generating and motoring modes, classification of machines, terminal characteristics, losses and efficiency, starting and speed control of DC motors; Alternator – generation of three phase emf, circuit model, terminal characteristics, voltage regulation, parallel operation of alternators and load sharing; Synchronous motor – creation of travelling magnetic field, starting methods, speed control; Induction motor – constructional details, working principle, circuit model, terminal characteristics, starting methods, speed control; Special machines – universal motor, single phase induction motor, stepper motor, servo motor, permanent magnet motors, switched reluctance motors; Selection of motor for specific application; Engineering aspects of electric machine performance and operation.

name: Power Systems
description: Energy sources;Structure of power system; Basic concepts of 3-Øelectrical systems; Modeling of power system components - transmission lines,synchronous machine, loads, transformers, etc.; Per unit system; Line parameters and their calculations; Transmission line performance and analysis, Power flow – formulation and solution methods like Gauss seideland Newton-Raphson method; Economic operation of power systems – economic dispatch of generation, unit commitment, automatic generation control, and frequency control; Fault analysis–symmetrical faults, symmetrical components, sequence models, unsymmetrical faults; Power system stability–swing equation and equal area criterion of stability; Introduction to switchgears and protection.

name: Power Electronics
description: Power semiconductor devices – diodes, thyristors, BJT, MOSFET, GTO, IGBT, MCT; Drive and protection circuits; AC-to-DC converters – uncontrolled and controlled, single phase and three phase, performance parameters, effect of source inductance; DC-to-DC converters – buck, boost, buck-boost and cuk; DC-to-AC converters – voltage source and current source, square wave and pulse-width-modulated, single phase and three phase, performance parameters; AC-to-AC converters; Resonant converters – zero-voltage and zero-current switching; Applications of power electronics – power supplies, motor drives, industrial applications, power system; Harmonics and mitigation.

name: Communication Systems
description: Review of signals and spectra, band-limited signals, analysis of signals, distortion in transmission; linear CW modulation, methods of generation, bandwidth efficiency, synchronous and asynchronous detection, frequency division multiplexing; exponential modulation, narrowband PM and FM, transmission bandwidth, generation and detection, de-emphasis and pre-emphasis filtering; pulse modulation, sampling theorem, aliasing, PAM, PWM, PPM, time division multiplexing; pulse code modulation, delta modulation, DPCM; review of random processes and power spectral density, signal space; Noise analysis; Digital communications basic, line codes and their spectra, pulse shaping, inter-symbol interference, Nyquist criterion for distortionless transmission, equalization; Basics of digital bandpass modulation, ASK, PSK, FSK.

name: Digital Signal Processing
description: Introduction to discrete-time signals and systems; linear time invariant (LTI) systems and properties, linear phase systems; brief review of Fourier representations; sampling and reconstruction of continuous-time signals; the z-transform, properties and applications to LTI systems; the discrete Fourier transform (DFT), properties, efficient DFT computation by FFT, effects of finite word length; linear and circular convolutions, block convolutions for long sequences; Signal analysis by DFT, spectral analysis by periodogram and autocorrelation estimates; brief review of analog filter design; IIR filters, stability, design by impulse invariance, bilinear transformations, frequency transformations of low pass IIR filters; FIR filter design by windowing method and Parks-McClellan algorithm, finite precision numerical effects; decimation and interpolation of signals, quadrature mirror filters and perfect reconstruction, subband decomposition; introduction to discrete wavelet transforms.

name: Electric Vehicle Technology
description: Introduction to Electric Vehicles(EV) : Battery electric vehicles; The IC engine/electric hybrid vehicle; Fuelled electric vehicles; Electric vehicles using supply lines; Support systems; Efficiency and Pollution aspects. Basics of Vehicle dynamics: Power train components of EV system; Dynamic axle loads; Aerodynamics; Acceleration and braking Performance; Force – Velocity Profiles; Approach to EV modelling. Battery Energy Storage: Types of traction batteries; Basics and Parameters; Battery charging, discharging and State of Charge; Circuit modeling of batteries; Safety issues; Future trends on battery management systems; Alternative energy sources. Electrical Machines and Controllers: Choice of Motors; Torque-Speed characteristics of the motor and load; Motor Cooling, Efﬁciency, Size, Mass and Rating. Power Electronic Converters for drives. Energy recovery through regenerative Braking; Safety and performance monitoring; Ancillary Systems: Battery charging stations; Data Acquisition, safety and performance monitoring systems; Vehicle supervisory control and communication systems.

name: Electrical Systems Lab
description: Laboratory experiments on analog, pulse, and basic digital modulation and demodulation techniques; different transmission lines, antennas, and passive devices; digital filter design techniques, signal filtering, effects of finite word length; process control (variables: position /temperature), time response analysis, determination of transfer function for DC motor, design of lag-lead compensator; power flow analysis, stability, and fault analysis; integrated design problems.

name: Analysis and Synthesis of Linear Circuits
description: Module 1: Introduction to circuit analysis techniques: Motivation and Basics; Importance of circuit theory, practical applications in Electrical/Electronic systems, review of circuit elements, circuit models and network theorems, Laplace transforms, transfer functions, Topological description of network; Network variables; Source transformation technique; node-voltage analysis; mesh-current Analysis. Module 2: m-port Network Tellegen's Theorem; Passive and Lossless Network; Z-parameter, Y-parameter, Transmission matrix and h parameters; network Interconnection; Characteristic impedance and propagation function. Module 3: Transient and steady state analysis Transient response in RL, RC and RLC circuits; network equations and solutions using Laplace transform, initial conditions. Fourier analysis for periodic signals; Fourier transform, Steady- state response - amplitude and phase spectra, resonance. Module 4: Network Functions Driving point function, transfer function, concepts of poles and zeros, Bode plots; Impulse response and convolution. Module 5: Graph theory Fundamental theorem of graph theory; loop analysis; two basic facts of loop analysis, loop analysis for linear time-invariant networks, properties of the loop impedance matrix, cut-set analysis; cut-set analysis for linear time-invariant networks, properties of the cut-set admittance matrix. Module 6: Passive Network Synthesis Elements of realizability theory; causality and stability, Hurwitz polynomials, positive real functions, elementary synthesis procedures, properties of L-C immittance functions, synthesis of L-C driving point immittances, synthesis of R-C impedances or R-L admittances, properties of R-L Impedance and R-C admittances. Poles and zeros, Foster‘s reactance theorem, Foster and Cauer forms of LC Networks, Foster and Cauer Canonic forms. Synthesis of RLC networks Module 7: Filters Maximally flat low-pass filter approximation, transient response of low pass filter, synthesis of low pass filters, magnitude and frequency normalization, Frequency Transformations.

name: Principles of Digital Communications
description: Optimum detection and matched filtering in AWGN, Inter-symbol interference (ISI), Nyquist criterion, and pulse shaping, Wireless channel models: flat and frequency-selective fading, Modulation performance in AWGN and fading channels; Motivation and coding gain, Linear block codes: Hamming codes and cyclic redundancy checks (CRC), Convolutional codes: encoding, trellis representation, and Viterbi decoding (hard decision); Turbo coding principles: concatenation, interleaving, puncturing, constraint length, and free distance, Iterative and soft-decision decoding (BCJR, log-MAP), LDPC code construction, sparse parity-check matrices, and belief-propagation decoding, Coding gain–complexity trade-offs and examples from modern standards (LTE, Wi-Fi, 5G); Symbol-timing recovery: early–late gate and Gardner algorithms, Carrier-frequency offset and phase recovery (PLL concepts, differential detection), Sensitivity of synchronization to SNR, multipath, and Doppler effects; Impact of multipath and ISI on detection, Pilot-aided channel estimation: correlation, least-squares (LS), and minimum mean-square-error (MMSE) estimators, Linear equalizers: Zero-Forcing (ZF) and MMSE, Nonlinear equalization: decision-feedback equalizer (DFE); Effects of imperfect synchronization and channel estimation, Error propagation in equalizers; performance–complexity trade-offs, System-level integration of coding, synchronization, channel estimation, and equalization in a complete single-carrier link, Case studies from LTE, NR, and Wi-Fi receivers.

name: Wireless System Design and Implementation
description: Introduction: an overview of software-defined radios (SDRs) and PlutoSDR, I/Q sampling, frequency allocations, antennas, and regulatory disclaimers regarding transmissions; Analog receivers: implementations of analog receivers using SDRs, introduction to the filter design for audio signals, recording UHF-based analog wireless microphones and/or AM/FM radio; Digital receivers: the burst transmissions by the car key fobs, the energy detection-based synchronization, demodulating amplitude shift keying (ASK) signals and bit decoding; Synchronization and data decoding: Introduction to packet synchronization using correlation, revision of the pulse position modulation, building aircraft trackers using ADS-B-based broadcasts at 1090 MHz; Wireless Protocols: introduction to various packet formats such as RFID and AlectoV1 for ISM-band weather stations and perform full-stream data recovery to get live weather updates.

name: High Voltage DC and Flexible AC Transmission Systems
description: High Voltage DC (HVDC) Transmission: comparison of AC and DC transmission; Types of HVDC links; Major components; Selection of converters configuration; Converter charts; Converter control; Misoperations of converter; Protection against over currents and over voltages; Harmonics and suppression; Ground return; Flexible AC Transmission Systems (FACTS): Principle of compensators - shunt compensators and series compensators; FACTS controllers – based on Thyristors, based on self- commutated switches; Applications of FACTS controllers - stability improvement and congestion management in power system.

name: Digital Image Processing
description: Fundamentals — Visual perception, Image sensing and acquisition, Image sampling and quantization; Intensity transformations — nonlinear transformations for enhancement, histogram equalization; Spatial filtering — convolution, linear and order statistic filters, unsharp masking. Image Transforms — discrete Fourier transform, discrete cosine transform; Frequency domain filtering — DFT, image smoothing, specialized filters (Gaussian, Laplacian, etc); Image restoration — using spatial filters, Wiener filter; Introduction to color spaces and color image processing; Morphological image processing — erosion and dilation, opening and closing, hit-or-miss transform, thinning and shape decomposition; Image segmentation — edge detection, thresholding, regionbased segmentation, watershed algorithm; Image compression — fundamentals, lossless coding, predictive coding, transform coding.

name: Applied Statistical Bioelectric Signal Analysis
description: Empirical Modeling and Approximation; Fourier Analysis applied to Biomedical Engineering; Probability concepts and Signal characteristics; Introduction to Random processes and Signal properties; Random Signal Modeling and Parametric Spectral Estimation.

name: Advanced Signal Processing
description: Review of discrete time signals and systems, Z-transform, DFT/FFT, Digital filter structures, IIR and FIR filters; Frequency Domain Analysis - Frequency-Domain Characterization of Linear TimeInvariant Systems, Correlation Functions and Spectra at the Output of LTI Systems, Linear TimeInvariant Systems as Frequency-Selective Filters, Inverse Systems and Deconvolution; Multirate Digital Signal Processing - Introduction, Decimation and Interpolation, Sampling Rate Conversion by a rational factor, implementation, applications, digital filter banks, Two-Channel Quadrature Mirror Filter Bank, M-Channel QMF Bank; Power Spectrum Estimation - Random Signals, Correlation Functions and Power Spectra, Estimation of Spectra from Finite-Duration Observations of Signals, non-parametric methods for Power Spectrum Estimation.

name: Restructured Power Systems: Operation and Management
description: Fundamentals of electricity markets: Privatization and deregulation; Types of electricity markets - Pool, Bilateral and Multilateral; Components of deregulated power systems; Independent system operator (ISO) and Power exchange (PX): Functions and responsibilities; Pricing mechanisms, and energy trading arrangements; Transmission pricing paradigm and congestion management; Ancillary services and system security management in deregulation; Distributed generation in deregulated markets; Electricity market developments in India; IT applications in electricity markets.

name: VLSI Design
description: Review of MOS device operation, combinational and sequential logic design; CMOS logic families including static, dynamic and dual rail logic. Fabrication of MOS transistors, Circuit Layout: Design Rules, Parasitics. Arithmetic blocks (ALUs, FIFOs, counters), memory; data and control path design, Logical Effort. Introduction to hardware description languages (verilog), Analysis and synthesis algorithms including circuit, switch and logic simulation, logic synthesis, layout synthesis and test generation. Chip design examples, Floor-planning, Packaging.

name: Microelectronics Lab
description: Familiarization with Synopsis and Silvaco TCAD tools; Simulation of different Semiconductor processes; Structural design of BJT, nMOS and pMOS transistor using process simulation; Simulation of semiconductor devices (Diodes and Transistors) to obtain I-V plots; Parameter extraction of a MOS transistor; Simulation of a CMOS Inverter to obtain delay and other parameters; Familiarization with compact models and transistor-level circuits. Basic concepts of hardware description languages (Verilog/VHDL); Introduction to FPGA and FPGA programming; HDL programming to understand hierarchy, concurrency, logic and delay modeling; HDL programming to understand Structural, Data-flow and Behavioral styles of hardware description; RTL S thesis usin tools; Writin Test-bench desi , Post-s thesis simulation of di •tal desi s.

name: Special Electrical Machines
