description: Permanent Magnet Brushless D.C. Motors - Fundamental equations; EMF and Torque equations; Torque speed characteristics; Rotor position sensing; Sensorless motors; Motion control. Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors- Construction; Principle of operation; EMF and torque equations; Starting; Rotor configurations; Dynamic model Synchronous Reluctance Motors-Constructional features; axial and radial flux motors; operating principle; characteristics.

name: Power Electronic Converters
description: Switched mode power supply — principle, control, design; Power conditioners; Uninterruptible power supplies. DC motor drives — adjustable speed drives; Induction motor drives — variable frequency drives, static slip power recovery scheme, vector control; Synchronous motor drives — trapezoidal, sinusoidal excitations, load commutated inverter drives; Drives for brushless machines. Space heating, air-conditioning, induction heating, electric welding. Converters for HVDC transmission and FACTS; Interconnection of renewable energy conversion systems. Harmonics and electromagnetic interference — mitigation by passive and active power filters.

name: Lasers
description: Fundamental wave and quantum properties of light, light-matter interaction;, discrete energy levels; Radiative transitions and emission linewidth, energy levels and radiative properties of matter, radiation and thermal equilibrium;Absorption and stimulated emission, population inversion, gain and gain saturation, laser oscillation above threshold, population inversion requirements in 2-, 3-and 4-level systems, laser pumping; Laser resonators, stable resonators, Gaussian beams, special cavities; Specific laser systems, solid-state lasers, pulsed lasers; Semiconductor lasers, edge-emitting and surface emitting lasers, quantum cascade lasers; Modulation of lasers; electro-optic, acousto-optic and direct current modulation; Frequency multiplication of laser beams, introduction to nonlinear optical effects.

name: Solar Photovoltaics: Physics, Technologies and Applications
description: Introduction: Photovoltaic (PV) history, PV economics, Solar energy conversion; Physics: Crystal structures, atomic bonding, types of semiconductors, energy band diagram, p-type and n-type semiconductors, doping and carrier concentration, diffusion and drift of carriers, continuity equation, P-N junction and its properties, dark I-V equation of P-N junction, junction under illumination, solar cell parameters, measurements of solar cell parameters, short circuit current, open circuit voltage, fill factor, cell efficiency, optical losses, electrical losses, surface recombination velocity, quantum efficiency, I-V characteristics; Technologies: Crystalline/Poly-Crystalline silicon solar cell technologies, Amorphous silicon solar cell technology, Thin film solar cell technologies, Characterization of solar cell materials and devices, Nanostructured solar cells; Applications: System components; Cells, modules and arrays; Interfacing PV modules to loads; Power Conditioning and Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) Algorithms; Inverter control topologies for stand-alone and grid-connected operation; Stand-alone PV systems, Grid-connected (utility interactive) PV systems.

name: Physics of Transistors
description: Transistor as a black box; Energy bands; Density of states; Fermi level; Recombination- Generation in semiconductors; Carrier Transport; Poisson and continuity equations; 1D energy band diagrams; Bipolar junction transistors (BJTs); Metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) electrostatics; MOS capacitors; Poly Silicon gates and quantum mechanical effects; MOS transistor equations; Ballistic MOS transistor; Scattering theory; Effective mobility; MOS transistor scaling; 2-D MOS electrostatics; Short channel effects; V T engineering; Series resistance and effective channel length; MOS transistor leakage; High voltage MOS transistors; SOI MOS transistors; High electron mobility transistors (HEMT): Reliability of MOS transistors

name: 3D Computer Vision
description: Review of linear algebra, calculus of variations, signals and systems; Camera and image formation – optics; Feature detectors – edge and corner detection; Feature descriptors – SIFT, SURF, feature matching; Shape from X – Reflectance map, BRDF, Shape from shading, Photometric stereo, depth from defocus, depth from focus, RGB-D images; Single view geometry – finite projective cameras, camera parameters, point correspondences, estimation of camera matrix, direct linear transformation (DLT); Two view geometry – homography, epipolar geometry, estimation of fundamental matrix, image rectification, stereo correspondence, shape from stereo; Three view geometry – trifocal tensors; Motion – optical flow field, Estimation of dense and accurate optical flow field; Multi view geometry – structure from motion, triangulation, factorization, bundle adjustment; Internet vision – mining community photo collections (Flickr, Facebook, etc.).

name: Asynchronous Circuit Design
description: Synchronous and asynchronous systems: advantages and challenges of each paradigm, Classification of asynchronous circuits:self-timed, speed-independent, delay-insensitive; hazards isochronic forks and arbitration; Communication: two-phase, four-phase, hybrid handshaking schemes;Graphical representations: Asynchronous finite state machines, Petri nets, Timed event structures; Control circuits: synthesis of speed-independent control circuits from signal transition graph specifications;Data-path circuits: design of efficient data-path circuits with completion detection/indication; Pipelining; Micropipelines; Globally asynchronous locally synchronous systems; Multiple clock domains and interfacing between independent clock domains; Case studies: some recent (commercial) asynchronous IC's; Current research trends.

name: Dynamic Behaviour of Electric Machines
description: Principles for electric machine analysis; Behaviour of iron-cored winding to DC and sinusoidal excitations; Behaviour of machine winding to converter fed excitations; Steady state behaviour of induction and synchronous machines – balanced and unbalanced operations; Behaviour of induction and synchronous machines to converter fed excitations; Characterizing dynamic behaviour of converter fed motors – electronicallycommutated DC motor, switched reluctance motor and synchronous reluctance motor.

name: CMOS Analog IC Design
description: CMOS Analog IC Design will broadly cover the following topics: Analysis, design theory and simulated design of CMOS op-amps; digital-to-analog and analog-to-digital converters; and phase-locked loops (if time permits).

name: IC Design Laboratory
description: Familiarization with Synopsis and Silvaco TCAD tools; Familiarization with Sentaurus Device Mixed-Mode Simulation tool; Familiarization with Cadence design tools; MOS transistor simulation and MOS model parameter extraction; Mixed-mode device simulation of CMOS inverter and CMOS switches; Simulation of CMOS based amplifiers (Single stage, Cascade, simple Cascode and Gain- boosted Cascode); Simulation of Current mirrors and Current amplifiers; Simulation of Single stage and 2-stage operational amplifiers; Simulation of Folded-Cascode operational amplifier; Design of common-mode feedback (CMFB) circuit; Layout of a 2-stage operational amplifier; Simulation and layout of Bandgap reference circuits; Simulation of switched capacitor based circuits. Basic concepts of System Verilog, Circuit designs based on standard cell library, synthesis using design synthesis tools, and functional and timing simulations; Design of additional standard cells, simulations, library extraction; Computer-aided design of integrated circuits, Examples may include data path circuits, controllers, memory design with interfacing circuits.

name: Analog IC Design Lab
description: Familiarization with Cadence design tools; Extraction of Metal Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) model parameters; Design and Simulation of Complementary MOS based amplifiers (Single stage, Cascade, Simple Cascode and Gain-boosted Cascode); Design and Simulation of Current mirrors, Current sources and Current amplifiers; Design and Simulation of Voltage and Current references; Design and Simulation of Operational Amplifiers (single stage and multi stage); Pole-zero compensation and effect of feedback; Simulation of Operational Amplifiers with double ended output; Common Mode Feedback (CMFB) circuits; Design of fully-differential amplifier; Simulation of switched capacitor based circuits; Analog and Mixed-signal layout techniques

name: Carrier Transport in Advanced Semiconductor Devices
description: Introduction: Advancements in MOSFET scaling: bulk MOSFETs, thin-body SOI MOSFETs, multi-gate FETs, nanosheet FETs, 2D material FETs; Review of classical carrier transport: drift-diffusion and continuity equations, Einstein relation, effective mass, density-of-states, Fermi-Dirac statistics, classical size effects; Limitations of drift-diffusion approach in describing modern MOSFETs: quantum confinement effects, Review of Schr odinger equation and its solutions. Band-structure modeling beyond effective-mass approximation: Review of electronic band-structures (E-k dispersion); Full band-structure from Density-Functional-Theory (DFT); Characterization of band structures using effective mass, density-of-states, and electronic bandgap; Band-structure modeling approaches: effective-mass based Hamiltonian, k.p approach, tight-binding Hamiltonian, and Wannier function approach. Semi-classical carrier transport in semiconductors: Introduction to the probability current, Fermi’s golden rule, Carrier scattering mechanisms in semiconductors, Electron-phonon interactions, The relaxation time approximation, The Boltzmann Transport Equation (BTE), Validity of the relaxation time approximation, Limitations of semi-classical transport. Carrier transport in nanostructures: Atomic-size conduction channels; Landauer formula; Conductance quantization; General Landauer–Büttiker approach: transmission function, scattering matrix, recursive Green’s function; Open and closed system description using Non-Equilibrium Green’s Function (NEGF): channel Hamiltonian, electrode self-energies, retarded, advanced, lesser and greater Green’s functions, spectral function, charge density matrix; Transmission function and current. Applications of quantum transport in advanced nanoelectronics devices: High mobility and short-channel MOSFETs; Tunneling field-effect transistors (TFETs); Transport in low-dimensional materials, e.g., two-dimensional materials; Quantum computing devices; Molecular devices.

name: Heterostructure Devices
description: Module 1: Physics of Heterojunctions Submodules: Overview of Metal-Semiconductor (MS) junctions: Ideal MS contacts, Schottky diode, the origin of interface/surface states, practical considerations; Overview of Heterojunctions: Materials consideration, semiconductor alloys, lattice-matched systems, energy band-diagrams, types of heterojunctions (type-I, type-II, type-III); Effects of quantum confinement; Hot electrons and Ballistic electrons in heterostructures; Formation of two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG); Quantum wells, Superlattices.

name: Digital VLSI Circuits Laboratory
description: Basic concepts of hardware description languages (Verilog/VHDL); Introduction to FPGA and FPGA programming; Structural, Data-flow and Behavioral styles of hardware description; RTL Synthesis using tools; Writing Test-bench designs; Post-synthesis simulation of digital designs and analysis; Introduction to Spice Simulator, VLSI Circuits include basic gates, complex gates, simple datapath circuits such as adders, comparators, etc., latch and flip-flop design, register files, Logical effort examples, Memory cell design and analysis, Introduction to layouts; Standard cell design; Understanding the Digital PDKs, Spice Models, Delay and Power calculations using Spice Tools, Automation for faster digital design and analysis.

name: VLSI System Design
description: Review of VLSI design concepts – Delay, Power, Standard Cell design; Interconnects: Interconnect Modeling and its impact; Datapath Subsystems: Data path circuits including adders, multipliers, dividers; Memory Array Subsystems: Different memories including SRAM, DRAM, MRAM, novel memories; Memory array, Crossbars, controllers, Peripherals; Digital ASIC Flow: Power distribution, Clock tree design, Static timing analysis, Fault models and introduction to design for test (DFT); Design Specification to final IC design: System Verilog and Circuit design based on standard cells; Floor-planning and Placement: I/O and power planning; clock planning; Routing (local and global); Design Rules and Post layout verification.

name: Memory Device Technologies and Applications
description: Module 1: Introduction and Overview Review of a computer system; Memory hierarchy; Semiconductor memory overview; Memory comparison, tradeoffs, and challenges; volatile & non-volatile memories

name: Smart Grid
description: Introduction to smart grid: what is the smart grid, smart grid benefits and strength to grid operators and consumers, smart grid to support fundamental transition toward 3Ds – decarbonization, decentralization and digitalization, energy outlook in India, energy transition and roadmap toward possible net-zero and sustainable energy future in India Smart grid architecture and building blocks of the grid transformation: electric infrastructure, industry structure, control structure, digital infrastructure and regulatory structure; network instrumentation - IT/OT solutions, IEDs, sensors, RTU, PMU, AMI, etc, grid analytics and data-driven grid, advanced asset management Smart grid operation and management: optimal power flow and security constrained economic dispatch (SCED), transmission line power transfer capability calculations, grid congestion management, renewable energy integration and grid balancing; electricity market optimization - electricity trading (day-ahead and real-time), green-term ahead market, renewable energy certificate (REC) market; AI/ML assisted forecasting tools and data analytics to handle grid uncertainties and improved grid operation Smart grid flexibility and transactive energy market: ancillary services - frequency regulation, contingency reserve, reactive power and voltage control, flexibility reserves; energy storage optimization, distributed energy resources optimization and management; demand side energy management – peer-to-peer energy trading and demand response (DR) programs

name: Power Management IC Design
description: Introduction Applications of Power Management Integrated Circuits (PMICs) Classification of PMICs Mathematical definition of relevant performance metrices Linear Low Dropout (LDO) Voltage Regulator Building blocks of LDO and their design Stability analysis using Bode plots and compensation techniques LDO with NMOS pass-element Overview of Digital LDO Operation DC-DC Switching Voltage Regulators (SWR) Analysis of buck, boost, buck-boost topologies in continuous and discontinuous mode operation Building blocks of closed Conduction and Switching losses in SWRs Voltage-mode and current-mode control of SWRs Stability analysis using Bode plots and compensation circuits Building blocks of closed-loop buck voltage-mode converter and their design Discussion on design tradeoffs Overview of advanced PMICs Switched Capacitor Converters (SCC) Energy Harvesting PMICs Battery Charger circuits

name: Fundamentals of Nanoelectronic Devices
description: Introduction: Advancements in MOSFET scaling: bulk MOSFETs, thin-body SOI MOSFETs, multi-gate FETs, nanosheet FETs, 2D material FETs; Review of classical carrier transport: drift-diffusion and continuity equations, Einstein relation, effective mass, density-of-states, Fermi-Dirac statistics, classical size effects; Limitations of drift-diffusion approach in describing modern MOSFETs.

name: Mixed Signal IC Design
description: Motivation for mixed signal design, Review of signal Analysis techniques -- Fourier, Laplace and Z transforms. Quantization: Quantization noise, Signal to quantization noise ratio (SQNR) Sampling: Nyquist criterion, Sampling by an impulse train, Sample and hold, Follow and hold. Data Converters -- (Nyquist rate): DACs: Resistor chain, R-2R, Current mode, Charge redistribution DACs. Concatenating DACs of different types. ADCs: Single, double and multiple slope ADCs, Successive Approximation, flash, semi-flash and Pipeline ADCs, Self calibration and Digital correction in ADCs. Data Converters – (Oversampled): ADCs: Resolution gain with oversampling, Noise shaping, first, second and higher order Sigma Delta modulation, MASH architecture, Digital filters and decimation for Sigma Delta ADCs. Sinc filters, Half Band FIR filters. DACs: Sigma Delta DACs, Up-sampling by zero insertion and filtering. Mixed Signal circuits for Digital Communication: Phase Locked Loops (PLL), Frequency synthesis and Clock recovery circuits.

name: Advanced Wireless Communications
description: Introduction – motivation, types of wireless networks, applications and design challenges in modern wireless systems;

name: High Frequency Engineering
description: Introduction - High Frequency, Frequency range, RF, Microwave, MM wave and Tera Hertz, Concept of Insertion-phase and Transit-time, Their Significance, Features , applications; High Frequency Network Representations - Various matrix representations, Z, Y, h, ABCD Matrix, Scattering Matrix, Generalized Scattering Matrix, Shifting of Reference Plane Properties, Different Matrix Conversions; Passive Circuits Design – Definition of Coupling Co-efficient, Isolation, Directivity, Power Divider, Power Combiner, Directional Couplers, Design of Hybrid, Rat-Race, Backward-wave Couplers, Different Filter Design; Amplifier and Oscillator Design - Transistor’s Scattering Matrix Representation, Definition of various Gains, Stability Criteria/Circles, Unilateral case, Figure of Merit, Constant Gain Circles, Definition of Noise, Design of Low Noise Amplifier, One port Oscillator design; Introduction to High Frequency Systems- - Wireless Link design, Radar, Parameters, Radar Equations.

name: Advanced Radiating Systems
description: Definition, Types and Parameters of Antennas: Radiation Patterns-Fields (E&H), Concept of Near and Far Fields, Solid Angle, Beam Width, Radiation Efficiency, Radiation Intensity, Directivity, Gain, Efficiency, Input Impedance, Radiation Resistance, Bandwidth, Circular Polarization, Antenna Noise Temperatures, Power Handling Capability: Voltage and Current Breakdown, Weathering Effect on Antennas; Concept of Electric and Magnetic Current Distribution of Antennas, Vector and Scalar Potentials (Electrical and Magnetic), Derivation of Radiation Patterns for Ideal, Small and Half Wavelength Dipole Antennas, Monopole, Loop, Yagi-Uda Antennas; Principles of Antenna Array: Concepts, N element linear arrays – uniform amplitude and spacing, Directivity of Broadside and End fire arrays, Half Power Beam Width, Main lobe, Nulls, Side lobes, Inter-element spacing, Pattern multiplication, Electronic beam scanning, Mutual Coupling, Grating lobes, Planar Arrays: array grid (Rectangular & Triangular), Selection of radiating elements for electronic scanning, scan loss, active impedance Array Synthesis for beam formation: Binomial, Dolph-Tchebycheff, Taylor distribution and Sulkaunoff method; Microstrip Antennas (MSAs): Principle of radiation of Rectangular Microstrip Antenna, Feeding Techniques of MSAs, E & H field, Basic design equations, Circular and Triangular MSAs, Compact MSAs, Broad banding techniques. Printed Dipole and Monopoles – principles and broad band techniques; Babinet–Brookner Theorm, Slot Antennas, Horn Antennas: E-plane Sectoral Horn, H-plane Sectoral Horn, Pyramidal Horn, Conical Horn, Aperture Matched Horn, Corrugated Horn, Broadband Horn Antennas Reflector Antennas- Planar, Angular and Curved Reflector Antennas: Parabolic Reflector; Front feed, Cassegrain –feed and Gregorian- feed, Spherical Reflector Antennas; Concept of Frequency Independent and Travelling Wave Antennas, Self-Complementary Antennas, Biconical Antenna, Log Periodical Antennas, Helical Antennas: Normal and Axial Mode, Spiral Antennas.

name: Fundamentals of Wireless Localization
description: Fundamentals of wireless localization and key applications - navigation, tracking, mapping, etc.), Coordinate systems: 2D/3D, local vs. global reference frames, Overview of measurement modalities: Time of Arrival (ToA), Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA), Angle of Arrival (AoA), and Received Signal Strength (RSSI); Delay estimation using correlation and matched filtering, ToA and TDoA measurement models, Synchronization and error sources, Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) and performance limits for range estimation; Multilateration and positioning from range measurements, Linear and nonlinear least squares (LS, NLS) approaches, Weighted least squares and geometric interpretation, Maximum Likelihood (ML) and Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) estimators; Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) and CRLB derivations, Geometric and Position Dilution of Precision (GDOP/PDOP), Impact of geometry and noise on positioning accuracy; Array signal modeling and steering vectors, Classical AoA estimation: Bartlett and Capon methods, Subspace-based methods: MUSIC and ESPRIT, Resolution limits and performance bounds; Joint ToA–AoA–TDoA localization models, Identifiability and rank conditions, Non-line-of-sight (NLOS) bias models and mitigation strategies, Robust estimators (e.g., M-estimators, RANSAC principles); Sequential estimation and motion models, Kalman and Extended Kalman Filters for target tracking, Handling time-varying and nonlinear systems; Hybrid localization systems (e.g., ToA + AoA), Trade-offs among accuracy, complexity, and bandwidth, Case studies: GNSS, cellular, Wi-Fi, and UWB localization systems.

name: Quantum Error Correction
description: Review of postulates of quantum mechanics, Review of classical error correction codes –repetition code and linear codes; Quantum operations and channels, Independence and low weight errors, The 9-bit Shor code, Discretization of quantum errors, Quantum error correction criterion, Degenerate and non-degenerate codes; Pauli group, Symplectic representation, Stabilizer codes, CSS codes, Erasure codes, Clifford group, Group action on Pauli group, Encoding operations and Gottesman-Knill Theorem; Formal fault-tolerance definitions, Local stochastic noise, Level reduction theorem, Concatenated codes, Proof of the threshold theorem, Assumptions of the threshold theorem

name: Smart Power Distribution System
description: Foundation of Smart Distribution System: power distribution system transformation; distributed energy resource (DER) grid integration and operational challenges; power quality and grid reliability; digitalization of distribution systems - advanced metering infrastructure (AMI), smart meters, sensors and IoT devices, communication technologies, cyber-physical power distribution systems; role of distribution system operator (DSO), relevant Indian electricity grid code and regulatory framework; Smart Distribution Network Management Systems: advanced distribution management system (ADMS) – core components, architecture and functions; distributed energy resource management system (DERMS) – core components, architecture and functions; integration of ADMS and DERMS for end-end solutions; industry technologies & terminology; overview on software tools and simulation platforms for distribution network management system; Demand Side Management & Energy Efficiency: Concept of demand side management (DSM) and energy efficiency programs; need of DSM and energy efficiency measures in India; Demand Response (DR) and various types of DR Programs; Demand flexibility and relevant regulation and obligation; DSM targets and its assessment, DSM cell in distribution companies; Renewable consumption obligation (RCO), Energy efficient technologies, Bureau of energy efficiency (BEE) and its functionalities; Energy efficiency in distribution system and potential use cases, relevant Indian electricity grid code and regulatory framework; Digital Technologies in Distribution System: AI/ML applications in distribution systems – forecasting, grid optimization and control, state estimation and anomaly detection, data-driven predictive asset management, Emerging technologies - digital twins, blockchain platform, P2P energy trading, and edge intelligence for smart distribution grid operation and management.

name: Advanced Semiconductor Devices
description: Advanced MOSFET Scaling and Limits: Basic MOSFET operation, I-V characteristics; Breakdown, transport mechanism; Moore's Law and scaling principles; Short Channel Effects (SCEs); Moore Devices: Non-Planar MOSFETs: FinFETs (Fin Field-Effect Transistors) and their advantages; Gate-All-Around FETs (GAAFETs/Nanosheet FETs; Tunnel FETs, Negative Capacitance FETs; More-than-Moore Devices: Heterostructure devices for RF and High-Power Applications

name: AI for Electrical Engineering
description: Task Environment, its properties, Artificial Agent architecture ; Uninformed Search Strategies and different types of Local Searches and its applicability for Electrical Circuit analysis using graph theory ; Informed Search Strategies, choice of heuristics and their applicability in path finding problem solving for printed circuit board; Adversarial Game Playing and its applicability in solving industrial process optimization problem, automation in manufacturing, power system security assessment, etc.; Problem solving under constraints and its applicability in electrical circuit design; Decision making using logical agents, solving using resolution and resolution refutation and its applicability in digital logic circuit design, building virtual models for simulation of physical circuits; Planning in Real World and its applicability in process industries; Decision making under Uncertainty and its applicability in biomedical signal processing, predictive maintenance.

name: Wireless System Design and Implementation
description: Introduction: an overview of software-defined radios (SDRs) and PlutoSDR, I/Q sampling, frequency allocations, antennas, and regulatory disclaimers regarding transmissions; Analog receivers: implementations of analog receivers using SDRs, introduction to the filter design for audio signals, recording UHF-based analog wireless microphones and/or AM/FM radio; Digital receivers: the burst transmissions by the car key fobs, the energy detection-based synchronization, demodulating amplitude shift keying (ASK) signals and bit decoding; Synchronization and data decoding: Introduction to packet synchronization using correlation, revision of the pulse position modulation, building aircraft trackers using ADS-B-based broadcasts at 1090 MHz; Wireless Protocols: introduction to various packet formats such as RFID and AlectoV1 for ISM-band weather stations and perform full-stream data recovery to get live weather updates.

name: Field Practices in Earth System Sciences
description: Goals/ tasks 1. Field geology (rock, feature, structure identification on different spatial and temporal scales): Training for developing skills as an independent field geologist. Focus is to learn what to look for in the field and how to identify and scientifically explain the feature you observed. 2. Learning a few easy mobile devices (compass, brunton, clinometer) and topo sheet etc. 3. Mapping and /or sample collection techniques: Rock/ sand/soil/water sample collection for various analytical purposes. Collection of oriented rock samples and learning how to procure samples for various geochronological measurements. 4. Reading/analyzing features and documentation. 5. Report writing: A detailed field report has to be submitted within 10 days of completion of the fieldwork and students will be evaluated on the basis of the report and performance in the field. Skills that can be achieved 1. Identifying geological and geomorphic features, sample collection and process interpretation. 2. Bridging field data with model simulations. 3. Marine Structures, Life and Pollution. 4. Features of the biodiversity/national forest: Components, Conservation and Survey Prospect areas 1. Mountain belt/ hills: Understanding the geology of the hills and rock formations, finding structural features showing active tectonics and surface features, erosion etc. These areas need extensive geological mapping. It will be a mapping intensive course. (Kutch-Bhuj area, Aravalli range near Gujarat-Rajasthan border, Deccan traps in northern Maharastra, Northwestern Himalaya etc.) 2. Beach / coastal areas: Study of sedimentary features and the role of sea. Understanding the vegetation. Study of nearby hills and their geology. Their role in sediment transport and above all, how sea level change occurs and how does it affect the landscape and surface processes (e.g.: Cambay area, Karnataka coast, south-Maharastra coast etc.) 3. National parks and Sanctuaries: Geology, vegetation and fauna of the national park areas. Understanding the behaviour of the fauna. Mapping will not be possible in the national parks, but in nearby areas. (e.g. Gir National park, Ranthambore national park, Bandhavgarh National Park, Pench Tiger reserve etc.). 4. River systems and water bodies: Understanding the river systems and their evolution. Role of tides in river confluences near sea and related deposits. How vegetation and fauna depend on the dynamic nature of the delta. (e.g.: Narmada delta). 5. Mining Areas: Mine visits are essential for understanding how geology and engineering gets coupled. The focus is to learn the geology and genesis of the ore mineral, understanding the mining techniques, safety protocols and reserve estimation. (e.g.: Coal mines in central India, manganese mines in Maharashtra, lignite mines in Gujarat). 6. Landslides and landfill: Understanding the processes related to landslides, different type of landslides, methodology for slope stability analysis. For each year one/two prospect areas will be selected by the instructors.

name: Elements of Earth System Science
description: Dynamical Earth’s System: Defining the Earth system an its properties; Earth system components: Lithosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere and sociosphere; Processes and characteristics of different earth components, Interaction between Earth’s components: mass and energy exchanges, Biogeochemical cycles: Water, Oxygen, carbon and nutrients cycles, Regulation of major biogeochemical elements. Coevolution of life and Earth’s planet: Geological time scale, Origin of Earth, Origin of life, Origin of cycles, Changes in Earth’s system components through time. Anthropocene: Coupled human-earth system, Anthropogenic mass transport and energy generation, Changes in mass energy balances and question of sustainability. Models of earth system: Growth, decay, oscillation, spreading, and reactions; Carbon, water and nutrient cycle modes, Diffusion of mass and energy models. Prediction and uncertainties: Sensitivities, Uncertainties in earth system, risk, management, sustainability, Environmental management in high risk and high uncertain systems

name: Introduction to Earth Sciences
description: Origin of Earth Overview of planet formation with emphasis on Earth, meteorites, layers of the earth, Earth’s magnetic field.

name: Drone Data Acquisition, Processing and Interpretation
description: DGCA guidelines on drone types as well as the laws and regulations for flying drones in India. Drone data acquisition methods and techniques, with nadir and oblique data acquisition and automated and manual data collection methods. Image processing using software’s (such as Agisoft and/or Pix4D), with an introduction to structure-from-motion methodology and development of 3D Digital Outcrop Models (DOM’s). Introduction to lidar, thermal, multi and hyperspectral drone datasets. Data interpretation using open-source software’s (such as cloud compare).

name: Archaeology: Theory and Methods
description: Overview and Introduction: Why study or practice Archaeology? Defining the discipline: its nature, scope, and key concepts, its place among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory), and in the contemporary world; Theoretical development: Antiquarianism to culture-Historical approach, paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology, controversies, current and future trends, and Archaeology in India; Archaeological methods through case studies: Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post-excavation techniques and analyses, data and contexts, contribution of sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology, contribution of technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer science) in Archaeology; Cultures and Chronologies: mapping human origins and evolution to cultures, cultures (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts), simple to complex societies to civilizations, explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies, cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management, laws and legislations; Branches/Types of Archaeology: Social archaeology, Environmental archaeology, Public archaeology, Underwater archaeology etc.

name: Human Evolution
description: Introduction: subjects, theories, methods and concepts employed in the study of human evolution, history of evolutionary thought, concepts of adaptation, natural selection, speciation, mutation, heredity, variation, micro and macro evolution, Linnaean system of classification, geological time scale, prehistoric timeline, context and material culture in Archaeology, dating techniques, palaeoenvironmental settings, process of fossilization and taphonomy; Assessment of the human fossil record during the Miocene- Pliocene- Early Pleistocene epochs (between 23 and 5 million years ago to 1.2 million years ago), primate evolution, Hominoidea superfamily; divergences, the earliest hominins, Genus Australopithecus, Kenyanthropus and Paranthropus, key fossil finds (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics); Assessment of the human fossil record during the Pleistocene (2.5 million years ago to about 20,000 years before present) epoch, earliest Homo and other Homo species before the emergence of Modern Homo sapiens, recent fossil discoveries from Africa and Eurasia (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics), dispersal theories and the extant human diversity, using DNA to trace the most recent common ancestor; One-day field visit to nearby prehistoric sites and museums; Laboratory: human osteology and morphometrics

name: Earth Surface Processes in the Anthropocene
description: Introduction: Introduction to earth surface processes and historical development in concepts, Source of energy, Mass conservation and geomorphic transport laws, Process interaction in shaping the earth surface, Nonlinear and complex behavior of earth systems. Specific Earth Surface Processes: Weathering and formation of soils, karst, slope and catchment erosion processes, fluvial, eolian, glacial, periglacial and coastal processes, and resultant landforms, Water and sediment flux in river systems. An introduction to the earth surface of India. Process Dynamics and controls: Rates and changes in surface processes; Techniques for process measurement, Geochronology of surface and its sediments. Earth system response to external controls i.e. tectonics, sea level/base level change. Geomorphic connectivity, time lag and complexity in signal propagation, Landscape evolution models, Evolutionary trajectory of the landscapes in response to external forcing. Predictability of Earth’s surface processes. Anthropocene: Anthropocene in the context of formal geological classification, Signature for the Anthropocene, Human as an external forcing on Earth’s surface processes, Natural hazards and resource management in Anthropocene; Nonlinear behavior of earth systems and challenges in natural resource managements,

name: River Morphology and Ecology
description: Introduction to River Science, Introduction to geomorphic concepts: threshold, sensitivity, connectivity, hierarchy and complexity. River basin approach: Sediment source and catchment erosion processes, Transition between hillslope and fluvial processes, Longitudinal river profiles, Sediment load and sediment yield, Sediment and nutrient transport process in rivers, Erosion and sedimentation processes in channel, Geochemical proxies to study sediment dynamics in a river basin. Drainage Network: Quantitative analysis, Role of drainage network in flux transfer, 3-dimensional connectivity in a river basin, Hydrological response of a river basin, Processes in confluence zones, Evolution of drainage network. River processes and morphology: River fluxes, energy distribution and patterns of alluvial rivers - braided, meandering and anabranching channels; Hydrological, sedimentological and ecological characteristics and their interrelationship in different channel patterns; Dynamics of alluvial rivers; Different classification approaches in fluvial geomorphology and its applications. Glacio-fluvial interaction: Sources of water in river system, Hydrological budgeting in the glaciated mountainous region, Spatial variability of glacial melt component in the Himalaya. Bedrock channels: Stream Power law and Bedrock incision process; River response to climate, tectonics and human disturbance; Quantitative analysis of bedrock channel processes and evolution of fluvial landscapes. Stream Management: Fluvial hazards and their causes, Humans and rivers, Ecosystem based approach to stream management, Concept of river health, Environmental Flow (e-flow) – definition, data requirement, different approaches for e-flow estimation.

name: Quantitative Geomorphology
description: Introduction to Geomorphic processes. Diffusion equation and its applications in modelling of geomorphic processes: Hillslope erosion processes, channel bed sediment transport process, groundwater dynamics. Numerical simulation of landforms through transport equations.Advection-diffusion equation and its applications in modelling of geomorphic processes: river response to tectonic process, knick point migration in river channel. Numerical simulation of processes through advection-diffusion equations.Stochastic processes in Geomorphology and its modelling. Graph Theory and its applications in modelling of geomorphic processes.

name: Modeling of Earth System and Sustainability
description: Purpose of mathematical modeling in Earth system science and its methodology; Mathematical representation of the Earth system and its components; Equilibrium algebraic models: energy and mass balance at earth surface, Thermodynamics and chemical reactions in Earth system; Growth and decay ODE models: Biogeochemical cycles; Transport process PDE models: Advection and diffusion processes in the Earth system; Distribution of events: Probability distribution of disaster events; Control of risks in earth system: Optimization models, sustainability models; Numerical algorithms.

name: Critical Zone System Science
description: Open system framework and introduction to Critical Zone, Components of Critical Zones: Introduction to climate, landforms, soil, water and biota components; Multiscale Geophysical imaging of the Critical Zone: Magnetic, Electrical, Electromagnetic, GPR, NMR and Seismic methods for subsurface imaging. Geochemistry of the Critical Zone: Chemical analysis of soil, surface water and groundwater. Process models in Critical Zone: Radiative energy and mass balance on the Earth’s surface, Thermodynamics of solutions and mixtures, Rate of entropy production, Geochemical kinetics: rate models, Chemical reactions in soil-water interaction, Conservation laws and constitutive relationships for porous media, Richard’s and Boussinesq equations for unsaturated and saturated media. Heat conduction and advection in sub-surface, Mass and energy transport in river and landform evolution; Ecosystem processes: Growth and decay and Advection-diffusion-reaction models; Process - structure integration in the Critical zone: Geological and climatic drivers of critical zone processes, Modifications by anthropogenic forcing.

name: Engineering Seismology and Seismic Hazard Analysis
description: Introduction of earthquake hazard, historical perspective for effects of earthquakes, tsunamis, liquefaction and landslides, major earthquakes in India and in the world, overview of seismic zonation and codes, national and global seismic hazard mapping programs. Overview of plate tectonics, types of faults, earthquake source, source models. Quantification of earthquake size: earthquake energy, magnitude scales, magnitude relations, intensity scale. Wave propagation and attenuation: body waves, surface waves, geometrical spreading and inelastic attenuation, scattering. Earthquake recordings and ground motion parameters: displacement, velocity and acceleration motions, response spectra, spectral acceleration, Fourier spectra. Earthquake location and magnitude determination using seismic records. Brief overview of probability theory: Basic principles, subjective probability, conditional probability theorem, random variables and distributions. Seismic hazard analysis methods: deterministic and probabilistic methods, types of sources and source zonation, earthquake catalogues and seismicity analysis, ground motion models, ground motion simulations, hazard integral and hazard computation, types of uncertainties and their treatment in typical seismic hazard studies, UHS (Uniform Hazard Spectrum). Examples of Seismic hazard studies, e.g., GSHAP (Global Seismic Hazard Assessment Program), and other international programs. Overview of site-specific seismic hazard analysis and micro-zonation, local site-effects, site response analysis methods and site-classifications. State of the practice of seismic hazard analysis post Fukushima incident and examples of some site-specific seismic hazard studies for NPPs (Nuclear Power Plants) around the world. Overview of seismic risk and vulnerability, difference between hazard and risk.

name: Near Surface Geophysics
description: Physical fields of the Earth: Gravitational field, Geomagnetic field; electromagnetic field, seismic wave-field, thermal field. Properties of the Earth and processes: Mechanical properties, density, elasticity, seismic waves; Electrical properties (conductivity and resistivity) of the rocks; Magnetic properties of rocks, soils, Remanent magnetization; Gravity anomalies, Earth’s heat, temperature inside the Earth; Heat transport in the Earth. Measurements of physical fields and rock properties: Gravimeter and gravity measurements, Satellite based gravity measurements, working principle of magnetometer, seismometer and GPR (Ground Penetrating Radar). Methods of geophysical interpretation: Direct and inverse methods, gravity, magnetic electrical surveying and electromagnetic surveying; Seismic reflection and refraction data processing and interpretation; GPR data processing and interpretation. Applications: Applications of geophysical methods in the field of groundwater, geotechnical, civil infrastructure, mining, hydrocarbon exploration using existing data.

name: Ocean and Global Change
description: Module 1: Physio-Chemical Oceanography, Ocean Circulation and Dynamics Oceans, their divisions, distribution, characteristics (temperature, salinity, and density), and masses. Ocean observation concepts and techniques; Chemical properties and biology in the ocean; Photosynthetic Primary production and light; Primay production and Nutrients; Geographic variations in primary production; Ocean Circulation and Dynamics I: Ocean Circulation (Wind-driven currents; Open ocean surface currents; Equatorial surface currents; High Latitude surface currents; Upwelling and Downwelling; Coastal currents; Eddies; Thermohaline Circulation; The conveyor Belt); Ocean Circulation and Dynamics II: Ocean-atmosphere Interactions (water and heat budget; Interannual climate variations El Nino and other Oscillations; climate and ocean surface water properties; Ocean Circulation and Dynamics III: Waves and Tides; Summarizing Module 1 with the global fluxes and the deep circulation Module 2: Introduction to Palaeoceanography Introduction to Ocean and its evolution through time and its influence on Climate; K/T boundary (Chixulub + Deccan Basalts) and Early Paleogene (carbon addition and global warming); Antarctica Glaciation and influence on Ocean and Global Change (Eocene-Oligocene Boundary); Messinian Salinity Crises (Miocene); The last glacial maxima and deglaciation, and Younger Dryas.; OAE’s (Ocean Anoxic Events) Module 3: Anthropogenic effects on Ocean and Global Change Sea-level fluctuations and Coastlines, Coral reefs and Ocean Acidification; Plastic Pollution; Future Predictions on Ocean and Global Change

name: Carbonate Sedimentology
description: Carbonate Sedimentology Overview: Introduction to carbonates, historical overview, Carbonate chemistry and mineralogy, components, texture, fabric – rock classification. The carbonate factory: microbes and algae, single cells, shells, echinoderms and colonial invertebrates. Carbonate Depositional Systems: Warm water neritic realms; Muddy Peritidal carbonates; Carbonate Lagoons; Sand Bodies; Evaporites, lacustrine, springs; Modern and Ancient Reefs; Carbonate Slopes; Deepwater Pelagic Carbonates; Coolwater neritic realms; Basics elements of carbonate stratigraphy; Sequence stratigraphy overview; Carbonate Ramps vs Platforms . Porosity, Permeability, and Diagenesis: Carbonate diagenesis overview & chemistry; Diagenetic environments, Paragenetic sequence, Burial graphs; Porosity types and classification.

name: Physics of the Lithosphere
description: Introduction to Lithosphere: Definition of the Lithosphere; Continental drift, Sea Floor Spreading; and the Plate-tectonics theory; Plate motion using Euler’s Theorem; Plate Tectonic Reconstructions, Gplates. Energetic of Lithosphere: Heat transport and production in Earth’s Interior; Cooling of the oceanic lithosphere; Temperature distribution in subduction zones; Continental geotherms; Plate tectonics through ages and The supercontinent cycle and evolution of life Dynamics of Lithosphere: Driving Mechanism of Plate Tectonics: Slab Pull and Ridge Push; Rift and Subduction initiation; Accretionary prisms formation; Isostasy and Flexure; Sedimentary, metamorphic, and ore generation The Indian Lithosphere: Indian plate motion through ages; Geodiversity of Indian plate; The Indian continental geotherm; Distribution of earthquakes

name: Statistical Seismology
description: Earthquake Physics Stick slip with blockslider model and its stability, Single slip dynamics: various friction models, Friction laws, Regularized rate and state-dependent friction and its invariant formulation

name: Materials of Earth’s Deep Interior
description: Introduction to the Earth’s interior: Origin of the Solar System: Types of planets, Proto-Sun, planetesimals. • Chemical and isotopic composition of meteorites: Types of Meteorites, difference and similarities with Earth composition. • Planetary models • Hadean Earth: Magma Ocean, Core formation, Atmosphere formation. • Internal structure of the Earth and its significance: Overview to Layers of the Earth. • Major Seismic discontinuities: Gutenberg, Moho etc. Crystal Structure: • Crystal Systems and Space Groups: Introduction to Space groups and fundamental crystallography. • Symmetry: Crystal symmetry with applications to deep earth. • Major minerals in the earth’s interior: Olivines, Pyroxenes, Spinel, Perovskite etc. • Phase transitions. • Phase diagrams. • Experimental techniques in mineral physics: X-ray diffraction and Spectroscopy. • Introduction to synchrotron sources • Theoretical techniques and their applications: Density functional theory and its applications to Earth sciences. • Equations of State: Birch Murnaghan, Vinet, Mie-Grüneisen. • Thermodynamics and its applications in geophysics: Laws of thermodynamics, Grüneisen parameter and its importance. Mantle: • Composition of the upper and lower mantle: Composition of the Early Mantle and its evolution. • Heat Flow: Fundamentals of heat-transfer. • Geochemical signatures of the mantle. • Seismological signatures of the mantle: Heterogeneities, Anisotropy of Mantle. • Elastic constants, Hooke’s Law: Fundamentals of Elasticity; Elastic tensor. • Elastic moduli and their applications: Calculation and experimental techniques for determination of elastic constants. • Elastic anisotropy • Pressure and temperature profile of the mantle: Geothermal gradient, Adiabats, Isentropes. • Constraints and uncertainties on the mantle temperatures. • Rheology: Determination of viscosity; rheological Models.

name: Advanced Field Techniques in Earth Sciences
description: Quantitative Topographic Analysis: Principles of topographic mapping, methods of topographic profile construction, interpretation of natural factors controlling topography, topographic control over earth surface processes (including natural hazards); Natural Processes of geomaterials: Igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic processes and their products, characterization of Earth forming materials including economic resources, primary and secondary features in rocks; Geometrical analysis of structural features: Measurements of orientation of various 1D and 2D structural elements (e.g. tectonic fabrics), techniques of high-resolution structural mapping, large scale structural mapping of geological terrains and construction of geological sections, applications of stereographic projections in field analysis; Identification and mechanics of tectonic structures: Characterization of tectonic features: Fabrics, folds and fractures, interpretation of structural mechanics from geometrical data, reconstruction of deformation history from structural records; Post-processing of field data and geological reconstruction: Preparation and interpretation of geological maps, graphical presentation of geological data, reconstruction of geological evolution of the study area; Field report preparation.

name: Computational Tools for Earth System Modeling
description: Introduction to computational tools for geodynamical modelling and Python, Finite Difference Method, Implicit and Explicit solution to PDEs, Geometrical and global indexing, Grids, discretization of equations and conservative finite differences. Boundary conditions, Special boundary conditions in geodynamic modelling, Staggered Grids, Advection in 1D and 2D, Marker-in-cell method: comparison of different advection methods and their accuracy. Creating thermomechanical code for Numerical solution of commonly occurring settings in Earth System Modelling.

name: Geobiology
description: What is Geobiology? Basic introduction to the course. What is Life? Life as a geologic agent. Origin of Life, it’s Theories and Evolving concepts, Building blocks of life, Amino Acids, Biomolecules (Miller-Urey Experiment), Major transitions in origin of life. Biogeochemical cycles: Introduction to basic concepts in biogeochemistry: Isotopic nomenclature, isotopic calculations including mass balance, processes that cause isotopic fractionation in organic matter, pyrites and carbonates including C, H, N, O and S. Carbon, Hydrogen, Oxygen, Sulfur, Nitrogen Cycles. Origin of Life and Precambrian Era: Concept of chemical equilibrium, alkalinity, weathering of carbonate rocks, Dissolved Inorganic Carbon, Redox reactions, photosynthesis, Biogeochemical redox reactions, Precambrian Banded Iron Formation, Stromatolites and microbial processes, sources and sinks for Carbon, Precambrian life. Neoproterozoic glaciations (Snowball earth). Biomineralization: Concept of biomineralization, Mineral-organic-microbe interface, Geobiological agents, Geobiology of Archaen, Proterozoic, Phanerozoic, and Anthropocene Era Hands on Data Analysis: Trace element data analysis, interpretation in Foraminifera (Biomineralization Concepts).

name: Sequence Stratigraphy
description: History of sequence stratigraphy, basic terminology, definitions and sequence stratigraphic method applied to cores and well logs. Vertical stacking, surfaces and systems tracts, the accommodation succession method, and the sequence stratigraphy hierarchy. Fundamentals of seismic stratigraphy, seismic response of stratigraphic surfaces, seismic reflection termination mapping and seismic facies analyses. Sequence stratigraphic method applied to non-marine, shallow-marine, and deep-marine depositional settings utilizing well logs, cores, outcrops, and seismic. Wheeler diagram, hydrocarbon play element recognition and mapping, reservoir architecture, interpretation and mapping techniques from exploration to production business scales. Practicals: 1) Lithostratigraphy vs chronostratigraphy, 2) Beds, bedsets, and parasequences, 3) Incised Valleys in outcrop and cores, 4) Well-logs to cores, 5) Idealized depositional sequence, 6) Tank experiment, 7) Seismic forward models and idealized depositional sequence, 8) Terminations interpretation in seismic, 9) Sequence, sequence sets, and composite sequence, 10) Well-log seismic tie, 11) Chronostratigraphy cycle charts, 12) Wheeler diagram

name: Climate of the Past
description: Climate and Climatic Variation, Introduction to Paleoclimate Reconstruction, Base Maps for Paleoclimatology, Refining Paleolatitudes, Proxies: Elemental and Isotopic Proxies (δ18O for changes in salinity, δ13C for carbon cycle, Mg/Ca for paleotemperature, Boron isotopes for paleo-CO2 reconstruction respectively), Biological proxy datasets would be used. Proterozoic and Phanerozoic Case Studies: Neo-Proterozoic (Snowball Earth), Phanerozoic (Permian-Triassic, Paleocene Eocene Thermal Maximum, Eocene Oligocene Transition, Pliocene-Pleistocene Transition, Holocene Climatic Optimum etc).

name: Fluid Flow and Reactions in Reservoir Rocks
description: Introduction to rock properties governing fluid flow: porosity, permeability, permeability tensor, capillary pressure, relative permeability, Darcy’s law for single- and two-phase systems, drainage and imbibition, saturation functions, pressure build-up. Groundwater flow: Vadose and phreatic zones, groundwater table, aquifer-aquitard, fluid potential, groundwater flow direction, hydrostatic equilibrium, conductivity-storativity-transmissivity, impact of heterogeneity and anisotropy, Darcy’s law for groundwater systems, transient groundwater equation, tracer tests, diffusion and dispersion, 2 phase groundwater contamination. Groundwater geochemistry: Major and minor groundwater species, TDS-conductivity-alkalinity-redox potential, activity and fugacity, activity variation with concentration, phase solubility, delta-delta plots, Stiff and Piper plots, reactive contaminants, contaminant retardation, acidic contaminants and reaction with rocks, contamination remediation. Geological CO2 storage: CO2 density variation with depth, gas migration under different force balances, viscous-capillary-gravity equilibrium, structural-capillary-solubility-mineral trapping, lithological heterogeneity, Leverett-J scaling, snap-off and capillary heterogeneity mechanisms, impact of salinity on solubility, mineral dissolution and precipitation, impact of flow rate on reaction kinetics.

name: Stratigraphic Evolution of Indian Continent
description: Introduction: Geological Time Scale, Introduction to Biostratigraphy, Lithostratigraphy Chronostratigraphy and Magnetostratigraphy. Paleogeography Precambrian and Phanerozoic Stratigraphy: Archaean cratons of Peninsular India; Proterozoic mobile belts; Stratigraphy of Cuddapah and Vindhyan basins; Stratigraphy of Paleozoic – Mesozoic of Spiti and Kashmir, Gondwana Supergroup, Jurassic of Kutch, Cretaceous of Trichinopoly, Tertiary and Quaternary sequences of Assam, Bengal and Siwaliks Stratigraphy of the Petroliferous basins of India Introduction to Anthropocene: Should it be included in Geological Time Scale? An ongoing debate Hands on exercises/ class assignments: Paleogeographic Maps (Precambrian to Recent), correlating them with stratigraphic evolution and paleoclimate. Data from Landmark research articles within Indian sedimentary basins will be used for hands on exercises (covering stratigraphic important events from Precambian and Phanerozoic) Case study: Paleoclimate and Sedimentation in the Indo-Gangetic basins Case study: BioStratigraphy of Petroliferous Basins of Bombay offshore, Kutch, Kerala Konkan regions, focus on paleoclimate studies of Paleogene and Neogene in Indian subcontinent Case study: Permian Triassic (Guruyur Ravine, Spiti) K/Pg (Deccan, Meghalaya)

name: Applied Micropaleontology
description: Evolution of various Microfossil Groups through Geological time scale, Taxonomy, and biostratigraphy of Calcareous nannofossils, Foraminifera, Diatoms, Dinoflagellates, Pollens and Spores, Siliceous Microfossils and Ostracods. Quantitative Biostratigraphy and Paleoecology, Environmental Micropaleontology: Monitoring and sustainability, Applications in: Sequence Stratigraphy, Paleobathymetry, reconstruction, Carbonate Microfacies, Paleoceanography and Climatic Variations, Case studies in petroleum exploration, pollution monitoring, coral reef health, eutrophication, ocean circulation.

name: Environmental Geosciences
description: Land and groundwater. Soil as a resource, soil formation, catchment, soil erosion, soil salinization, groundwater use and recharge, groundwater contamination, interplay of soil and groundwater fluctuations, arsenic contamination Coast and Sea. Topics: coastal vulnerability due to eutrophication, sea level rise, ocean acidification, global carbon cycle, carbon credit calculation Conventional and unconventional resources:.Fossil fuels, oil and gas formation, accumulation and production, geological carbon storage, resource extraction via mining, acid mine drainage, mine site remediation

name: Reservoir Modelling and Simulation
description: Definition of modelling and simulation; building conceptual geological models; meshing; discrete and continuous rock properties; pixel and object based modelling; model realizations; boundary conditions; fundamental equations; key equation discretization and solution methods; reactive transport and reaction path modelling; overview of thermodynamic database.

name: Anthropocene: Human Impact on the Earth
description: Introduction: Geological time scale, Evolution of Anthropocene concept, Human as a geological agent, Climate change and CO2 dynamics from the geological time scale to present. Boundary of the Anthropocene.

name: The Prehistory of Homo Sapiens
description: Module 1: Introduction to hominin evolution, Fossil and genetic evidence for anatomically modern humans, Competing models of human origins: “Out of Africa” vs. Multiregional Module 2: Dispersal and Global Migrations. Migration routes out of Africa: inland and coastal, Archaeological and genetic evidence of dispersals, Early migrations into Asia, Europe, and Sahul, Colonization of the Americas Module 3: Encounters, Adaptations, and Innovations. Interactions with Neanderthals, Denisovans, and other hominins, Genomic insights into admixture and adaptation, Responses to environmental and climatic challenges Module 4: Regional Diversity and Ecological Impact. Development of regional lithic and cultural traditions, Emergence of symbolic and ritual practices, Human role in Pleistocene megafaunal extinctions

name: Ceramics in Archaeology
description: Overview of ceramics, their origin, types (pottery, stoneware, faience), and artistic significance (figurines). Includes the earliest examples globally and in India. Theoretical framework on the importance of studying ceramics; Covers typology, seriation, classification, and documentation. Explores ceramic production processes, how pots were made, open-pit and updraft kilns. Discusses decoration types and cultural significance, scientific approaches to ceramic analysis including provenance, petrology, microstructural, macrostructural and manufacturing technology. Includes functional analysis (residue Analysis) with examples, and the importance of ethnographic surveys and studies; The importance of stoneware and faience technology in ancient times. Various production methods and scientific investigations. Comparable faience technology in Egyptian and Mesopotamian civilizations. Experimental research conducted by scholars and different methods of analysis; Examination of figurines within archaeological contexts, focusing on their cultural and social importance; technology of production. Analysis of bricks, including their production processes, architectural applications, and evolution over time; Ethnographic surveys to the potter’s workshop to understand the manufacturing processes of pottery; Hands-on handling and better understanding of pottery from various cultural stages, spanning from the Harappan period to the Historical period.

name: Harappan Civilization
description: "Introduction to Harappan Civilization, discovery, terminology, chronology, geographical distribution, contemporary cultures, important sites and their role in defining the civilization; Understanding the sedentary lifestyles of humans around 10000 years before present, emergence of villages, towns and cities; regional chalcolithic cultures; other contemporary Bronze Age Cultures, i.e. Egyptian, Mesopotamian and Chinese; Integration of regional chalcolithic cultures around 2600 BCE into Harappan civilization; characteristics that define the Harappan culture; Architecture, Arts, Burial Practices / Disposal of dead, Society and Religion; Technologies (bead, shell, metal, stoneware bangle, steatite, faience, seals, etc.), Writing systems, issues related to non-decipherment; authors of the Harappans; trade relations, both internal and external intermingled with scientific methodologies to study various aspects; Causes of decline, transformation of Harappans into rural settlements and way of life; continuity; Visit to a nearby Harappan archaeological site; Hands-on handling artefacts and ceramics from Harappan context "

name: Chalcolithic and Iron Age in India
description: Definitions, sources, and methods, Chalcolithic Cultures outside the periphery of Harappan Civilization; OCP and Copper Hoards, Malwa Culture, Jorwe Culture; Painted Grey ware and the beginning of Iron Age, Northern Black Polished ware and cultural progress in Iron Age; Iron age the second urbanization, spread of Iron and Megalithic Culture in Southern India

name: Fundamentals of Structural Geology
description: Concept of Stress & Strain: Traction & Stress, normal and shear stress, stress in two-dimension, Strain in rocks, Longitudinal and shear strain, principal axes of strain, strain ellipse concept, Planar & Linear Structures: Morphological features of foliations and lineations. Brief idea of origin of foliation and lineation and its relationship with tectonic stress. Ductile behaviour of Rocks: Fold and its morphology; Geometric and morphological classification of folds; mechanism of folding. Brittle behaviour of Rocks: Classification of fractures, Faults and Joints; Relation of Joints to Folds; Fault zone terminology; Geometric classification of faults; Anderson analysis of faulting; Shear zone and shear sense indicators. Interpretation of topographic maps; Stereographic projections of planes and lines; Orientation of planar and linear features; fold and fault problems through graphical and stereographic projection methods. Interpretation of geological maps with unconformity, fault, fold and igneous bodies

name: Origins of Agriculture
description: Concepts and theories of domestication and the Neolithic Revolution, Epipaleolithic hunter-gatherer background, Domestication of wheat, barley, cattle, sheep, goats, and pig, Key archaeological sites: Jericho, Çatalhöyük, Abu Hureyra, Impact of agriculture on subsistence and social systems; Spread of agriculture into Europe through migration and exchange, African domestication: sorghum, millet, yams, and cattle pastoralism; In this module, students will explore Asia's diverse contributions to early domestication, with a focus on East, Southeast, and South Asia. Topics: China: millet and rice domestication, Southeast Asia: root crops, early rice cultivation, maritime adaptations, South Asia: zebu cattle, mung bean, urad, horse gram, regional innovation and integration of agriculture into cultural traditions; Mesoamerican domesticates: maize, beans, squash. Andean domesticates: potato, quinoa, llama, North American domesticates sunflower, goosefoot, Environmental and cultural diversity in agricultural origins

name: Dynamic Earth Surface and Nature Hazards
description: Introduction: Introduction to earth surface processes and historical development in concepts, Source of energy, Mass conservation and geomorphic transport laws, Process interaction in shaping the earth surface, Nonlinear and complex behavior of earth systems; Specific Earth Surface Processes: Weathering, karst, catchment erosion processes, Hillslope processes, river, eolian (wind), glacial, periglacial and coastal processes, and associated hazards, Water and sediment flux in river systems, dynamic rivers, flood hazards and bank erosion; Climate change and challenges in future prediction - geomorphic connectivity, time lag and complexity in geomorphic systems, Challenges in natural resource management, Major natural hazards in India.

name: Archaeology: Theory and Methods
description: Overview and Introduction: Why study or practice Archaeology? Defining the discipline: its nature, scope, and key concepts, its place among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory), and in the contemporary world; Theoretical development: Antiquarianism to culture-Historical approach, paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology, controversies, current and future trends, and Archaeology in India; Archaeological methods through case studies: Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post-excavation techniques and analyses, data and contexts, contribution of sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology, contribution of technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer science) in Archaeology; Cultures and Chronologies: mapping human origins and evolution to cultures, cultures (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts), simple to complex societies to civilizations, explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies, cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management, laws and legislations; Branches/Types of Archaeology: Social archaeology, Environmental archaeology, Public archaeology, Underwater archaeology etc.

name: Human Evolution
description: Introduction: subjects, theories, methods and concepts employed in the study of human evolution, history of evolutionary thought, concepts of adaptation, natural selection, speciation, mutation, heredity, variation, micro and macro evolution, Linnaean system of classification, geological time scale, prehistoric timeline, context and material culture in Archaeology, dating techniques, palaeoenvironmental settings, process of fossilization and taphonomy; Assessment of the human fossil record during the Miocene- Pliocene- Early Pleistocene epochs (between 23 and 5 million years ago to 1.2 million years ago), primate evolution, Hominoidea superfamily; divergences, the earliest hominins, Genus Australopithecus, Kenyanthropus and Paranthropus, key fossil finds (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics); Assessment of the human fossil record during the Pleistocene (2.5 million years ago to about 20,000 years before present) epoch, earliest Homo and other Homo species before the emergence of Modern Homo sapiens, recent fossil discoveries from Africa and Eurasia (origins, description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics), dispersal theories and the extant human diversity, using DNA to trace the most recent common ancestor; One-day field visit to nearby prehistoric sites and museums; Laboratory: human osteology and morphometrics

name: Engineering Graphics
description: Introduction to the engineering design process and the importance of technical Graphics/Drawings; Integrated design and 3D modelling, visualization - sketching & computer aided drawing, geometrics - geometry construction, shape description, multi- view drawings - orthographic projection, isometric views, axonometric projections, auxiliary & section views; Dimensioning; Assembly drawings.

name: Introduction to Electrical Systems
description: Circuit elements – active, passive, time-variant, time-invariant, linear, non-linear, unilateral, bilateral; Sources – independent and dependent; Electric circuit and analysis – Ohm’s law, Kirchhoff’s laws, loop and node analyses, limitations of lumped circuit analysis; Network theorems – Superposition, Thevenin, Norton, Maximum power transfer; Natural and forced responses to DC excitation – RL, RC and RLC circuits; Sinusoidal steady state analysis; Polyphase circuits. Magnetic field – Biot-Savart law, Ampere’s circuital law, Faraday’s laws, Lenz law; Magnetic materials, characteristics, losses, coupled circuits. Transformers – single phase and three phase transformers, auto-transformers. Electro-mechanical energy conversion systems – DC generator and DC motor; AC Machines – synchronous generator and motor, three phase and single phase induction motors; Stepper motor. Power system - generation, transmission, distribution, costing of electricity.

name: Introduction to Analog and Digital Electronics
description: Introduction to signals and spectra, analog and digital signals, basic amplifier characterization, frequency characteristics and Bode plots; Ideal operational amplifiers, inverting and no-inverting amplifier circuits, instrumentation amplifier, integrators, differentiators; effects of finite (frequency dependent) gain, DC imperfections, and slew rate on performance; terminal characteristics of ideal and practical diodes, rectifiers, limiters and clampers, voltage doublers, Zener diodes; terminal characteristics of MOSFETs and BJTs; biasing, small signal analysis, simple amplifier circuits; basic feedback theory, simple oscillators; number systems; Boolean algebra and logic gates, minimization with Karnaugh maps; adders, comparators, decoders, encoders, multiplexers; sequential circuits – basic flip-flops, asynchronous and synchronous counters, registers; programmable devices – PLA, PAL and ROM; Memories.

name: Electrical and Electronics Lab
description: Frequency response of RLC circuits; Power factor improvement; Power measurement in balanced and unbalanced three phase circuits; Modeling the magnetic system by an equivalent electric circuit; Performance of single phase induction motor; Speed control of stepper motor. Diode clipper, clamper and rectifier circuits; Transistor amplifier and oscillator; Operational amplifier circuits; Combinational digital circuits; Sequential digital circuits.

name: Manufacturing and Workshop Practice
description: Introduction to Manufacturing; Historical perspective; Importance of manufacturing; Classification of manufacturing processes, Engineering materials; Woodwork; Fitting Basics of Casting, Metal Forming; Basic concepts of plastic deformation; Hot & cold working; Common bulk deformation processes (Rolling, Forging, Extrusion and Drawing); Common sheet metal forming processes; Machining; Chip formation and generation of machined surfaces; Tools - geometry, materials, Common machining operations (turning, milling drilling ,shaping etc). Grinding & other Finishing processes; Introduction to unconventional machining processes (EDM, ECM, UCM, CHM, LBM) etc., Welding & Other Joining Processes, Fundamentals & classification of Joining processes, Welding, Brazing and Soldering, Adhesive bonding, Mechanical fastening, Principles of heat treating; annealing, normalizing, hardening and tempering, Manufacturing of Polymer and Powder Products, Classification of polymers, Introduction to extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression and transfer molding; Powders & Green compacts from powders including slip casting of ceramics; Sintering; Manufacturing for Electronics; Special Processes like Chemical Vapor Deposition, Etching, Physical Vapour Deposition; Modern Trends in Manufacturing.

name: Basic Science Engineering Manufacturing and Workshop Practice I
description: Introduction to Manufacturing; Historical perspective; Importance of manufacturing; Classification of manufacturing processes, Engineering materials; Woodwork; Fitting Basics of Casting, Metal Forming; Basic concepts of plastic deformation; Hot & cold working; Common bulk deformation processes (Rolling, Forging, Extrusion and Drawing); Common sheet metal forming processes; Machining; Chip formation and generation of machined surfaces; Tools - geometry, materials, Common machining operations (turning, milling drilling ,shaping etc). Grinding & other Finishing processes; Introduction to unconventional machining processes (EDM, ECM, UCM, CHM, LBM) etc., Welding & Other Joining Processes, Fundamentals & classification of Joining processes, Welding, Brazing and Soldering, Adhesive bonding, Mechanical fastening, Principles of heat treating; annealing, normalizing, hardening and tempering, Manufacturing of Polymer and Powder Products, Classification of polymers, Introduction to extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression and transfer molding; Powders & Green compacts from powders including slip casting of ceramics; Sintering; Manufacturing for Electronics; Special Processes like Chemical Vapor Deposition, Etching, Physical Vapour Deposition; Modern Trends in Manufacturing.

name: Basic Science Engineering Manufacturing and Workshop Practice II
description: Introduction to Manufacturing; Historical perspective; Importance of manufacturing; Classification of manufacturing processes, Engineering materials; Woodwork; Fitting Basics of Casting, Metal Forming; Basic concepts of plastic deformation; Hot & cold working; Common bulk deformation processes (Rolling, Forging, Extrusion and Drawing); Common sheet metal forming processes; Machining; Chip formation and generation of machined surfaces; Tools - geometry, materials, Common machining operations (turning, milling drilling ,shaping etc). Grinding & other Finishing processes; Introduction to unconventional machining processes (EDM, ECM, UCM, CHM, LBM) etc., Welding & Other Joining Processes, Fundamentals & classification of Joining processes, Welding, Brazing and Soldering, Adhesive bonding, Mechanical fastening, Principles of heat treating; annealing, normalizing, hardening and tempering, Manufacturing of Polymer and Powder Products, Classification of polymers, Introduction to extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression and transfer molding; Powders & Green compacts from powders including slip casting of ceramics; Sintering; Manufacturing for Electronics; Special Processes like Chemical Vapor Deposition, Etching, Physical Vapour Deposition; Modern Trends in Manufacturing.

name: Computing
description: 1. Elementary Programming Techniques

name: Data-Centric Computing
description: Algorithms for processing data: Understanding time complexity – Asymptotics (big-O notation and examples); Storing sequential data: arrays and linked lists, data structures for storing tabular data; Organizing data and finding items – Searching (linear/binary search), Sorting – sorting almost sorted data (bubble sort, insertion sort), fast sorting in the worst-case (heapsort), using randomization to sort faster (quicksort), sorting without random-access ex. linked-lists (mergesort); Data Analysis: Tools - Spreadsheets (Excel/Google Sheet/Calc); data manipulation specific Python libraries e.g. Pandas; introduction to SQL; Answering queries over tabular data: map-filter-reduce, joins and unions. Using vectorized operations for efficiency. Techniques: Data structuring (tidy data) and cleaning (dealing with multiple labels for the same data, missing data) Summarizing data (averages, variance, moments), functions of tables (loading, cleaning, normalizing); Visualizing data introduction to matplotlib; plotting various statistics; categorical distributions, numerical distributions, overlaid graphs; Deriving Conclusions: Anomaly detection techniques in data: tests e.g. GRIM test, Benford’s law; Statistical traps: Correlation vs causation, Base-rate fallacy / prosecutor's fallacy. Simpson's paradox. Data censoring; Will Rogers effect, lead-time bias, and length time bias. Means versus medians. Importance of higher moments. Misleading visualizations; Making Predictions: Linear regression; basics of classification (train, test, validation); creating features; naive Bayes classification, logistic regression and its interpretation; introduction to clustering (k-means). Confidence in predictions: prediction intervals, confidence intervals.

name: Probability, Statistics, and Data Visualization
description: Computational Tools: Statistical Data Analysis using Python/R, Introduction to Relevant Libraries ; Basic Probability: Set Theory and Probability Spaces, Axioms, Probability Assignment for Discrete and Continuous Spaces, Combinatorics, Conditional Probability, Independence, Mutual Exclusiveness, Law of Total Probability, Bayes Rule, Examples ; Random Variables: Discrete and Continuous Random Variables, Probability Mass Function (PMF), Probability Density Function (PDF), Transformation (Function) of Random Variable, Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), Moments, Characteristic Function, Examples of Random Variables, Applications; Two Random Variables and Random Vectors: Joint PMF and PDF, Conditional PMF and PDF, Joint and Conditional Moments, Joint Characteristic Function, Covariance and Correlation Coefficient, Covariance Matrix, Dependence and Correlation, Transformation, Examples and Applications ; Statistical Inference: Confidence Interval, Hypothesis Testing - Critical Value test, p-Value test, Z-test, and t-test, Neyman-Pearson Test, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (P-R) curves, Applications ; From Data to Visualization: Data - Numbers, Strings, Sequences, Tables, Functions on Tables, Visualization - Categorical Distributions, Numerical Distributions, Overlaid Graphs. Statistical Visualization - Line Charts, Bar Charts, Contour Plots Spatial data visualization, network visualizations using Python libraries, visualizing probability distributions. Interactive Visualization - Curve Fitting, Regression, Clustering, Linear and Non-Linear Classification. Dimensionality Reduction (e.g. n-D to 2-D) - Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE). Examples and Applications in various engineering disciplines.
