Canonical form and first integrals of the Euler equations, Legendre and canonical transformations, Noether's theorem, principle of least action, conservation laws; Variational methods in statics (principles of minimum potential energy and virtual work), extended-Hamilton’s principle, Hamilton-Jacobi differential equation, examples of variational method and its application in continuum mechanics, elastodynamics, evolution equations and boundary conditions for one- and two-dimensional structural members (bars, beams, membranes); Variational problems with fixed and movable boundaries, broken extremals, Weierstrass-Erdmann condition and general jump conditions (examples from continuum mechanics); Deﬁnition of the second variation and the strong/weak minimizer, necessary and suﬃcient condition for a minimum, Legendre and Jacobi conditions, Weierstrass E-function, field of extremals; Minimum characterization of eigenvalue problems, Sturm-Liouville problems, Rayleigh quotient for vibration of continuum, buckling and stability analysis of elastic columns; Direct methods of solving variational problems, Ritz method and the method of finite differences

name: Probabilistic Machine Learning
description: Probability refresher: Probability theory, discrete distributions, continuous distributions, joint probability distributions, sampling from different distributions (e.g. using Box-Muller transform), uncertainty modelling, information theoretic concepts: (KL-divergence, entropy); Bayesian concept learning: Likelihood, Prior, Posterior, Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE), Maximum A-Posteriori estimation (MAP), Full Bayesian Estimation with Conjugate Priors (Beta-Bernoulli model for the coin toss example, Normal-Normal model for estimating parameters of Normal distribution); Bayesian supervised methods: Regression: Bayesian linear regression, Robust linear regression via alternative likelihood (e.g. Laplace, Student-T); Classification: Bayesian Logistic regression, Bayesian Naive Bayes; Latent Variable modelling: Gaussian Mixture Model, Probabilistic principal component analysis (PPCA); Approximate Inference: Sampling based strategies: Rejection sampling, Importance sampling, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), Metropolis Hastings, Gibbs sampling, No U-Turn sampler (NUTS); Variational inference: Mean field approach, Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), Reparameterization trick, Stochastic Variational Inference, Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI); Gaussian Processes (GP): Multivariate Normal distribution and its properties, Kernels, GP regression, GP classification, Approximate Inference and Sparse GPs; Bayesian Optimization (BO) and Active Learning (AL): AL - Query by committee, Uncertainty sampling, Expected model change, BO - Acquisition functions, GP based BO, Random Forests based BO; Probabilistic Deep Learning: MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, Bayesian neural networks (BNNs), Deep GPs.

name: Flexible Electronics: Materials, Methods and Devices
description: Overview of flexible electronics their Materials Systems: Background and history of flexible electronics, trends, emerging technologies, applications, research trends; Mechanics of active material and substrate: Stress and strain analysis, failure modes, mechanics of stretching, mechanical characterization; Materials for flexible devices- synthesis and processing: Organics and inorganics materials – polymeric semiconductors, silicone based material, transition metal oxides, paper-based material, textile based material, other materials; Patterning processes: Ink-jet printing, screen printing, gravure, flexographic printing, imprint lithography, spray pyrolysis and 3D printing. Design rule and considerations. Critical mechanisms involved in printing, practical examples, and solutions to challenges of formulating conductive ink; Flexible and Stretchable Sensors and Actuators: Design strategy and functional material, challenges and system integration. Flexible and stretchable temperature sensors, UV sensors, strain sensors, pressure sensors, gas sensor, Actuation principle, characteristic of an actuator, electroactive polymer and actuator; Optically, thermally, shape memory, pneumatic driven actuation; Wearable devices as human-machine interface: Sensor-actuator- microcontroller integration, design issues and consideration, Body interface, activity monitoring, speech – gesture –motion – emotion recognition.

name: Smart Renewable Energy Systems
description: Design of smart renewable energy systems: Centralized and distributed energy systems; Fundamentals of renewable energy system (RES) with storage: Photovoltaic (PV) cell characteristics, series and parallel interconnections, insolation based PV sizing; Grid connected wind energy conversion system: selection of generator; Battery backup design; RES penetration in power system and challenges; Role of power electronics in RES: electrical and thermal design of power converter for RES; Smart renewable energy system (SRES): application of artificial intelligence (AI) and internet of things (IoT) in RES.

name: Scientific Computing using C++
description: C++ and OOP: OOP fundamentals (abstraction, encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism), classes, object construction and destruction, operator overloading, class hierarchies, C++ templates, generic programming, standard template library (STL), template hierarchies and introduction to Curiously Recurring Template Pattern (CRTP), metaprogramming; Using scientific libraries: C++ integration with other languages (C, Fortran, Python), ordinary differential equations and linear algebra using Eigen and Boost, phase-field calculations using PRISMS-PF, computational fluid dynamics using Open LB, classical molecular dynamics using LAMMPS, data visualization using Matlab and Python; Multi-threading: using promise and future objects in C++ standard library, multithreading using POSIX and OpenMP, thread synchronization using mutex, common pitfalls (deadlock, starvation, race conditions), multithreaded computation using scientific libraries.

name: Advanced Transportation Electrification Technology (ATET)
description: Overview of Electric Vehicle Technology: Need for transportation electrification. Understanding the architecture of electric vehicles (EV). Design of EV subsystems based on application: considering range, weight, volume, charging time constraints.; Modelling and Management of Energy Storage in EV: Fundamentals of fuel cells and batteries for EV applications; Introduction to equivalent circuit models. Role of battery management system (BMS) in electric vehicles; Data driven modelling of battery; Charging Technology: Charging requirements and standards: covering issues related to charging infrastructure development. Design of power electronics: wired, wireless inductive and capacitive charging; Transportation Electrification Impact and Upcoming Trends: Impact of EV charging on the power grid. Life-cycle costing with payback period and net present cost. Upcoming trends in transportation electrification and ramifications for future power systems.

name: Computer Vision
description: Mathematical Basics: Linear Algebra, Calculus, and Optimization. Classical Computer Vision: Camera and Image Formation, Image Analysis and Processing, Feature Detectors, Feature Descriptors and Matching, Shape from Shading, Photometric Stereo, Depth from Defocus, Single View Geometry and Camera Matrix, Two View Geometry, Homography, Epipolar Geometry, Fundamental Matrix, Shape from Stereo, Multiple View Geometry – Structure from Motion, Active Depth Sensors, Motion – Optical Flow Field. Learning for Computer Vision: Overview of Discriminative and Generative Models, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Overfitting, Underfitting, Regularization, Optimizers, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Transformers, Variational Autoencoders (VAE), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Diffusion Models, Recent Advances. Computer Vision Applications: Image Classification, Semantic Segmentation, Object Detection, Homography Estimation, Depth Estimation, Optical Flow Estimation, Image Restoration, Image Relighting, NEural Radiance Fields (NERF), High Dynamic Range (HDR) Imaging, and other relevant applications.

name: Deep Learning
description: Mathematics for Deep Learning - Calculus, Matrices, and Probability. Classification and Regression Problems.

name: 5G and Beyond
description: Overview of 5G: Evolution, standardization, application potential in IoT, AR/VR, Smart infra and Autonomous Vehicles. Core, RAN and air interface, protocol stack and layers. 5G physical layer, air interface and RAN: Millimeter wave propagation characteristics, Modulation and demodulation. multiplexing and multiple access. OFDM, OFDMA. Multiple antenna techniques, Air interface protocol stack, frequency and time domain structures, numerologies. radio interface and access protocol. 5G virtualization: Software defined networks, Architecture and Principles: Control and Data Planes, separation of control and data, SDN use cases. Network virtualization, NFV, service management and orchestration, Multi-access Edge computing in 5G and B5G networks: MEC Architecture, Mobility, service continuity, traffic routing. 5G Automation: Network Slicing, Requirements for slice automation and 5G packet core services. End-2-End network slicing and service Assurances. Cloud RAN Architecture, challenges and Non-public 5G networks.

name: Advanced Computer Architecture and Systems
description: Introduction to Computer Architecture and Systems: Overview of computer architecture, historical context and evolution. Performance metrics and measurements, benchmarking techniques, Amdhal’s law, Gustafson-Barsis law, Little’s law and their implication. Instruction set Architecture, MIPS/RISC-V and CISC. Parallel Architecture: Instruction Level Parallelism: pipelined processor design, superscalar and out-of-order execution techniques. Dynamic scheduling, register renaming, scoreboarding, Tumasulo’s algorithm, speculative execution. Multi-core and many core systems, Data level and thread level parallelism. Memory Hierarchy: Cache and Memory hierarchy, advanced techniques for cache optimization. Distributed Shared memory, Coherence protocols (MSI, MESI, MOSI). Memory Consistency models, Sequential Consistency and relaxed consistency models. Advanced cache and memory bandwidth control and monitoring using allocation technologies, RDT. GPU Architecture and Programming: Massively parallel processors, SIMT model, synchronous and asynchronous memory updates, unified virtual addressing and unified memory. Fundamentals of CUDA programming. Advanced topics on Heterogeneous and Emerging Architecture: Interconnection networks, Network-on-chip, Accelerators: near, in-memory and neuromorphic computing, NPU, DPU and TPUs. Approximate computing, intermittent and energy-harvesting computation.

name: Matrix Methods for Signal Processing, Data Science, and Machine Learning
description: Vectors and Matrices. Matrix Factorization: Eigendecomposition, QR, and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Subspaces, Rank, and Nearest-Subspace Classification. Norms and Procrustes Problems.

name: Mechanics of Composite Materials
description: 1. Review of solid mechanics, foundations of tensor analysis

name: Semiconductor Packaging
description: Module 1: Introduction to Semiconductor Packaging (8 hours) • Brief overview of ICs highlighting FEOL and BEOL to pad • Scaling guidelines for Front-end (Moore’s Law) and Back-end • Anatomy and function of semiconductor packaging • Various types of packages (difference in choice of materials and designs) • Surface mount and through hole packages

name: NeuroAI: Neuroscience & Artificial Intelligence
description: Foundations of NeuroAI: Introduction to Neuroscience for AI Researchers, Introduction to AI for Neuroscientists (Multilayer Perceptron, Gradient Descent, Backpropagation), Biological vs. Artificial Neural Networks; Neural Architectures Inspired by the Brain: Hierarchical and Modular Brain Networks, Cortical Columns and Their Role in AI, Recurrent and Attention-based Models Inspired by the Brain, Spiking Neural Networks and Neuromorphic Computing; Building AI Systems from Brain Principles: Implementing Brain-Inspired Learning Rules (Hebbian, STDP, etc.), Memory and Working Memory in AI Models, Predictive Coding and Energy-Efficient AI, Hands-on: Building a Novel Architecture Using Brain Principles; AI for Neural Data Analysis: EEG, fMRI, and Electrophysiology Data Processing, Machine Learning for Decoding Brain Activity, Functional Connectivity and Network Neuroscience, Generative Models for Neural Data Simulation; Cutting-Edge Research and Applications: AI for mental disorders and neurodegeneration prediction, The Future of Brain-Inspired AI

name: Process Data Analytics, Optimization, and Control
description: 1. Basics of optimization: variables, constraints, objectives, linear, nonlinear, mixed-integer, convex, nonconvex, formulation and solution using computer solver 2. Basics of process control: proportional, proportional-integral, and proportional-integral-derivative controllers, dynamic & multi-variable process modeling using computer software, and closed-loop simulation under different controllers 3. Data analytics: outlier removal, dimensionality reduction, principle component analysis, feature selection, k-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, support vector machines, random forest classification 4. Data-driven modeling and optimization: design of experiments, sampling, response surface model, neural network model, validation, surrogate-based optimization 5. Model predictive control (MPC): introduction to MPC, data-driven state-space model development, MPC controller development using state-space model, closed loop simulation under MPC. The course will use datasets familiar to engineers, such as industrial process data, energy & environmental systems data, and semiconductor manufacturing data.

name: Photonics – Principles and Applications
description: Basic properties of light and classical treatment of light-matter interaction; origin of refractive index, absorption, dispersion; basic optical phenomena - interference, diffraction, birefringence; Generation of light: basic operation of light-emitting diodes and lasers; Lab demonstrations of important temporal and spectral properties of these sources; Controlling the properties of light to convey information; modulation of intensity, phase and polarization; electro-optic modulators, acousto-optic modulators, polarizers; splitters, combiners, multiplexers; Propagation of light through dielectric waveguide structures – conventional and unconventional optical fiber; rectangular waveguide structures; metal-dielectric waveguides; Detection of light: p-i-n photodiodes, avalanche photodiodes; quantum efficiency, responsivity, spectral and temporal response; Applications in engineering and science such as optical fiber communication, optical sensing of physical quantities (displacement, speed, strain, vibration, temperature) and chemicals (high-resolution molecular spectroscopy to identify different gases), plasmonics, biophotonics, imaging, light detection and ranging (LIDAR) as used in distributed sensing and autonomous cars. Lab demonstrations of sensing displacement, vibration, temperature and gas leaks.

name: Classical Information Theory
description: 1. Review of Real Analysis (rigorous and abstract) and probability theory; 2. Information measures: entropy (joint, relative and condition); 3. Asymptotic Equipartition Property (AEP) and typicality; 4. Variable length lossless compression: prefix codes, the Kraft-McMillan inequality and Huffman code; 5. Reliable communication and channel capacity – One shot and Asymptotic; 6. Information measures for continuous random variables; 7. AWGN channel and its capacity; 8. Lossy compression and rate distortion – One shot and Asymptotic; 9. Topics in Network Information Theory (MAC, Broadcast Channel Coding and Slepian Wolf Theorem); 10. Basic Hypothesis Testing and Error Exponents (Time Permitting).

name: Physics Inspired Machine Learning for Engineering Design
description: Introduction to physics inspired machine learning and analytics for engineering design; embedding physical principles and constraints into data-driven models; physics-guided feature engineering and dimensional analysis; regression, classification, and clustering with physics priors; surrogate and reduced-order models for efficient design optimization; hybrid modeling combining machine learning architectures with governing equations; physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and extensions; neural operator learning and applications in PDE. Uncertainty quantification, probabilistic modeling, and physics-informed optimization. Multi-domain applications overview including EE (power systems, signal processing, communications), ME/CE/CH (thermal-fluid, structural health monitoring, materials discovery), Semiconductor manufacturing and process optimization.

name: Machine Learning
description: Learning Foundations: Introduction and applications, learning paradigms, bias-variance tradeoff, empirical risk minimization, generalization, overfitting; Supervised Learning: linear and logistic regression, SVMs, decision trees, ensemble methods; Optimization: gradient descent, stochastic gradient descent, convexity, constrained optimization; Unsupervised Learning: PCA, k- Means, NMF, hierarchical clustering; Deep Learning: MLPs, CNNs, RNNs, autoencoders, autograd; Reinforcement Learning: conceptual introduction, Q-learning, value/policy intuition; PAC learning, Inductive bias, Deriving loss functions via Maximum Likelihood Estimation, convergence analysis and adaptive optimization, Gaussian mixture models, self-supervised pretraining.

name: RFIC Design
description: Introduction to RF and Transmission Line Theory covering signal behavior, reflection coefficients, VSWR, and impedance matching using the Smith Chart for lumped and distributed elements; RF Network Representation focusing on S-parameters for circuit characterization and shifting of reference planes; Wireless Architectures and CMOS Integration exploring transceiver blocks, IC design constraints, and the transition from discrete components to Silicon microelectronics; High-Frequency Transistor Modeling and Low Noise Amplifiers (LNA) focusing on f, T, fmax, and parasitic effects to implement inductively degenerated CMOS topologies with optimized noise figures; Mixer Design and Frequency Translation encompassing active Gilbert Cells and passive CMOS switching mixers while addressing conversion gain and port isolation; Oscillators and Frequency Synthesis covering negative resistance theory, LC and Ring Oscillator topologies, phase noise, and the basics of Phase Locked Loops (PLL); Amplifier and CMOS Power Amplifier (PA) Design focusing on stability, Class A-C operations, and efficiency (PAE) for large-signal performance; System Integration addressing transceiver challenges such as IQ imbalance, DC offsets, and the role of integrated filters in signal chains.

name: Foundation Programme
description: Introduction to IIT Education & UG Curriculum, Departments and Academics, Values and Ethics; Comprehensive Viva-Voce; Introduction to Engineering, engineering graphics, computers and computing, and electrical systems; Introduction to and sensitization about social issues, global & Indian history, challenges (social, cultural, religious, economic, political, and technical) facing the contemporary society, technology and development, and role of scientists and engineers; Skill development workshops; Field Trips to places of social, cultural or scientific interest; Technical Visits to shop floors, local industries, etc.; Small innovative projects to dissect or develop some products or ideas.

name: Introduction to Engineering
description: This course will provide a bird’s eye view of all the engineering disciplines to the first year students. In addition to providing information, this course will also open avenues to appreciate and develop engineering aptitude by looking at various real world problems. This course will have invited lectures from eminent academic and industrial personalities, interaction sessions with IITGN faculty members, panel discussions, hands-on activities in laboratories, tours to certain industrial and research facilities, group discussions, etc.

name: Cultures of Communication
description: This course provides an induction for a graduate student to enter into fields of thought, action and communication with other researchers from divergent disciplines as well as ideologies. Structured like a foundation, the course will focus on specific themes each year to be handled by a group of faculty members. Specific themes may include: listening ‘silences’; decoding the visual; the uncertainty principle; peer group evaluation; conversation across disciplines; philosophical assumptions and knowledge making; activism as communication; How to ‘read’ arguments in quantitative and qualitative research. The course will include activities such as the following: Panel discussions, field visits, invited speakers, group discussions, self-analysis through diagnostic tests; cross-disciplinary perspectives through observations studies.

name: Writing
description: Communication Mechanics and Style Perfecting the formal register of writing Syntax and grammar Working on tone, pitch and style Styling the content for different kinds of target audience Research Styles (APA) Use of rhetoric as the trope of persuasion in writing Using different media Framing argument Critical thinking Acknowledgment of scope Situating the work in relevant field(s) Literature review Declaration of gap in existing research and the intent of plugging it Framing research question(s) Defining and using technical jargon The art and craft of writing Difference between descriptive and analytical Distinction between referential and argumentative Foregrounding one’s own claims in writing Argument statement and its step-by-step establishment Making strong transitions Coherence, structure etc. Evidence and Proof, models of truth Presentation of Methodology and Data Analysis Research Methods Qualitative Quantitative Referencing Style Thinking beyond disciplinary boundaries Desirability of interdisciplinary thinking and engagement What-is-it-like to be interdisciplinary Imperative of intedicipliary approach Communicating with general audiences Online and social media

name: General Education 1 “Experiencing Hindustani Raga Music”
description: Survey of various fundamental aspects of Hindustani raga music including: (1) a general overview of classical Indian music history; (2) a delineation of basic theoretical concepts relating to raga and tala including arohi, avarohi, raga taxonomy, thāt theory, melakarta system, etc.; (3) a review of different raga music genres (dhrupad, khayal, thumri) along with a survey of influential artists of the twentieth century; and (4) an exploration of Indian aesthetics (rasa theory) and its relation to the idea of transcendence through art.

name: English Studies
description: This course is designed to provide students with the necessary tools to improve their practical use of communicative English. To this end, the course will include the following four patterns of learning. Listening: accurate, complete and coherent grasp of basic content in lectures as well as dialogues/conversations; Speaking: development of confidence in the use of English language for basic spoken expression at the interpersonal level; Reading: culturally familiar semi-technical and non-technical readings for the purposes of basic comprehension of content, as well as advanced comprehension of underlying compositional structures, development of ideas, and modes of assimilation (understanding, retention, analytical/critical engagement); Writing: vocabulary (scope and correctness/appropriateness), correct grammatical expression (agreement, tenses, sentence structure), clear and coherent development of ideas in a composition through an understanding of the process of writing from pre-writing through revision.

name: Pleasures of English Studies
description: This course will introduce students to the nuances of English Studies. The following are the broad objectives of the course: Develop critical, creative, and analytical thinking abilities appropriate for responding to a variety of rhetorical situations and genres; Foster appreciation and sensitivity for some of the world’s seminal writings and creative projects; Introduce the concept of using reading and writing as a process of inquiry; Emphasize interactive and active learning.

name: Foundational Sanskrit
description: Verb Basics: Sounds and Technical Terms used for different sets of sounds; Introduction to Verbs and their classifications; Conjugation of verbs in Present Tense; Indeclinables; Svara (Vowel) and Visarga Sandhi. Noun Basics: Forming declensions of nouns; Meanings of six Kārakas and cases used for them; Adjective and Pronouns; Prefixes; Vyañjana (Consonant) Sandhi. Sentence, Tense and Voice: Active and Passive voice; Imperfect Tense; Participles - Gerund, Infinitive and Past; Declensions of consonant ending nouns and pronouns; Understanding Verb and Noun forms as the two basic components of a sentence. Functions: Reading simple prose; Reading simple poetry; Writing short essays; Understanding some most common Sanskrit chandas (metres); Reading selected verses from the classical Sanskrit literature.

name: Chinese Language & Culture for Beginners
description: This course will focus on the basic skill of Chinese pinyin; writing of the characters; more than 100 words and expressions for day-to-day use and functioning; forty key sentences for responding to situations; introduction to Chinese culture.

name: Chinese Studies –2
description: This is a comprehensive Chinese course which will focus on the skills of Chinese reading, speaking, listening and writing. The vocabulary will be enlarged to 300-500 words and students will master more than 100 key sentences. This course will also continue to build on the understanding and knowledge of Chinese culture.

name: Japan Studies
description: Theoretical Framework: How to View and Assess Society, Culture, and Institutions; Demographic and Geographic Features of Japan; Japanese History I: Ancient Period to Medieval Period; Japanese History II: Modern Period to 1953; Japanese History III: 1954 to1989; Japanese History IV: 1990 to Today; Japanese Culture I: Values, Beliefs, and Norms; Japanese Culture II: Tradition and Modernity; Characteristics of Japanese Language, Communication and Human Relations; Japanese Economy: Framework of Understanding Economy; Japanese Economy: Miracle to Stagnancy – Impact of Globalization; Japanese Politics and Japan’s International Commitment: Gap between What Ought to Be and What It Is; Current Critical Social Issues

name: Urdu script and poetry
description: Alphabets and Nuqtas; Alif, Alif Mudd, and bai gharana; Jeem Gharana and short vowels; Dal gharana and non conectors; Sen to Ghain; Fe to Du chashmi he; Letters and words with du chashmi he; Long vowels; Long vowels and Introduction to Poetry; Markers; Hamzah; How to make plural; Reading & writing Prose and Poetry.

name: Urdu poetry interpretation
description: Structure of various poetic genres such as ghazal, nazm, qasida, marsia, rubaiyat, etc. Examples of each poetic form written by masters such as Meer, Ghalib, Ustad Zauq , Jigar, Faiz, saHir and others in the context of their background and lives.

name: French studies : Level 2
description: Contents: At the end of the course the students will be capable to communicate effectively on the following subjects: Talking about personal effects, one’s job, daily routines, telling time and talking about the weather. The course will combine elements of language (vocabulary, structure, listening, pronouncing, speaking, reading, and writing) and cross cultural competences. The students will be exposed to authentic language in spoken and written form. Methodology: Through activities students will be inspired to use their skills in French language and will improve them by working on several small projects during the class.

name: Gujarati Studies
description: This course introduces non-Gujarati students to the basics of Gujarati Language (largely spoken, to a lesser extent, written), literature and culture. Starting with learning basic Gujarati language using the manual A step by step course towards mastering Gujarati, it will attempt to draw some insights into the psycho-Socio aspects of Gujarati linguistics. The course will also incorporate aspects of Gujarati culture and ethos as revealed in the literature, grammar, idioms, proverbs and poetry. The course will also introduce the students to the Basics Gujarati Literature and its thousand year old history. Through short tour of surrounding tourist spots of cultural, literary and linguistic significance, we also intend providing exposure to the Gujarati art and expression, beliefs and perceptions and if possible, taste and cuisine. Through basic exposure of Language, Literature and culture, the course intends taking one closer to the Gujarati world.

name: Economics
description: Introduction (How do economists view the world? Importance of Economics in our daily lives); Individual decision making (Scarcity, choice, MRS, MRT); Strategic interactions (self-interest, social interest, altruism, game theory); Understanding the market (Demand-Supply, Competitive versus Monopoly cases, revenue, profit and cost (sunk/variable/fixed)); Growth and its measures: GDP, per capita income; Development and its measures: inequality, poverty; Money, its meaning, its functions, its forms, Prices in the context of inflation/deflation, The monetary system; Market failures (Externalities - positive and negative), Public goods; Trade (absolute advantage versus comparative advantage), International trade (import duty, export promotion, exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation); Economics of Environment (trade-off between environment and growth, Environmental externalities); Personal Finances (The power of compounding, Investment and instruments of investments); Economics of Innovation and Technology (unemployment, Creative Destruction, AI Economics)

name: Japanese Language for Beginners
description: "Introduction to Japanese Language & its different script; Vocabulary and Introduction to Japanese Grammar; Basic sentences referring to things, demonstrative nouns and ordinal numbers; Polite Form of Japanese Verbs, Particles and Japanese Clock; motion verbs like to go, come and return and related particles; Japanese Calendar including dates, months, days of the week etc; Vocabulary referring to living & non living things; counting things (counters); Continued usage of adjectives and related particles."

name: Advance Japanese Learning
description: "Introduction to Kanji; Kanji Reading and its usage and related vocabulary; Introduction to Japanese Te form and its various usage grouping and conjugation of verbs, present continuous forms.; requesting and granting permission.; expressing continuous actions and occurrences; Introduction to Japanese Nai form and its various usage; introduction to dictionary form +koto ga dekimasu, express your hobby, and things you can perform; Polite style & Plain style; Japanese ta form of verbs and Conjugations & Narimasu; expressing your thoughts and how to quote what someone says; noun modifier and its usage; toki and its various usage; keremasu and Te agemasu, moraimasu and kuremasu; Tara forms and kute forms and its usage."

name: Mandarin for Beginners
description: "Basic vocabulary, grammar, and various language points of Mandarin language; setting language learning in an immersive experience of the localization of traditional culture in Mandarin speaking societies; introduction to Chinese characters and pinyin system; teaches knowledge of cultural activities and festival celebrations; basic conversation"

name: Mandarin for Beginners - II
description: "Advanced recognition and reading of Mandarin characters, deconstructing complex sentence structures; usage of homophones and advanced grammar; terminology and grammar conventions; will continue from where; elements introducing greater exposure to culture of Mandarin-speaking societies, in conjunction with short courses. Will start with fourth lesson of the first textbook."

name: Introduction to Writing - I
description: Descriptive Writing: Ice-breakers, To-do list, Informal and formal email writing, Description using sensory words, strong verbs, metaphors, and similes Narrative Writing: Thesis statement, Storytelling and components of a narrative, Reading and analyzing personal narratives, Summary writing, Drafting, revising, and copy-editing. Reflective Writing: Socratic questions, Reflective thinking (what, how, and why) for forward-thinking, Reflective writing, Peer-feedback giving, Group communication Fundamentals of Academic Writing: SMART goals, Elements of academic communication, Evaluating sources, Identifying “fake news”, or misinformation, IEEE citation style and practice.

name: World Civilizations and Cultures
description: Comprehensive overview of several important civilizational developments in the history of the world. The course will focus on how multiple cultures from around the globe have developed varying scientific, artistic and philosophical modes of knowledge in their pursuit to understand the human condition, society and the world at large. Civilizations to be covered in class include, but are not limited to, the Indus, Vedic, Mesopotamian, Egyptian, Han, Mayan, Aztec, Greek and Roman. Emphasis will be placed on the advancement and transmission of scientific knowledge, the implications for cross-cultural interactions, and the plurality of global thought.

name: Exploring India’s Scientific and Technological Heritage
description: Introduction to history of science; Science and technology from Neolithic to Vedic and Harappan times; Astronomy & mathematics in pre-classical times; The great classical savants: from Aryabhata and Brahmagupta to Bhaskara II; The Kerala school of mathematics and astronomy; Indian perceptions of the physical universe; Ayurveda in principle and application; Chemistry and metallurgy; Water management; Other technologies in classical and medieval times; Cultural and social specificities in Indian science & technology; Concluding thoughts on science in India.

name: Introduction to Philosophy
description: What is it to philosophize, Different aspects of philosophy: Metaphysics, Epistemology, Ethics, Logic; Philosophy in ancient Greece: Heraclitus, Parmenides, Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle; Philosophy in ancient India: Orthodox and heterodox schools; Modern Western philosophy: Rationalism and empiricism; Metaphysics: Problem of universals; Epistemology: Knowledge as justified true belief, Gettier counter-examples; Ethics: Meta-ethics and normative ethics, Different ethical theories; Analytic philosophy: Frege, Russell, Wittgenstein, Austin, Quine, and Kripke.

name: Sanskrit Literature
description: Reading and interpretation of the Epics and sacred texts; Distinct linguistic features of narrative verses and prose selected readings from the Kathäsaritsägarab and Paficatantram; Aesthetic aspects of Sanskrit Kavya literature selected excerpts from the works of Kälidäsa (RaghavamSam) and Bhäsa (Urubhafigam); Philoso h of Vedic literature - selected verses from the U anisads

name: Pondering in English: The World Around Us
description: Era for collective decision making: Introduction to the 21st-century skills and 5C’s (critical thinking, creative thinking, communication, collaboration, and (global) citizenship); Cultural literacy and global etiquette: Elements of culture, Interpersonal communication theory (s.a. Buber’s I-Thou and I-It), Culture-dependent assumptions and codes of conduct; Negotiation for decision making: De Bono’s “Six Thinking Hats”, Multicultural decision-making, Conversation strategies in cooperative/competitive contexts; Current topics in the world: Analysis and synthesis of gathered information, Current global events and the latest news; Review of an editorial piece: Analytical reading and writing, Language of claiming and rebutting.

name: Introduction to Sociology
description: Sociological perspectives: what is sociology?; answering and asking sociological questions; “sociological imagination” (C.W Mills); Sociological theories: Emile Durkheim; Karl Marx; Max Weber; Pierre Bourdieu; McDonaldization of society (George Ritzer); Inequality: social stratification, class and caste; gender (education, employment and health inequalities with special focus on India); sexual inequality (social construction of heterosexuality/ heteronormativity); Sociology of Education: Pierre Bourdieu and cultural capital; sociology of science and technology (SCOT); sociology of professions- the making of the Indian engineers; women and science/technology education; Culture; Media and Representation: popular culture; mass media; media representation; cultural imperialism debates; role of social media Social Movements: Theories of social movements; Social movements in India; ICTs, social media and social movements; Sociology of Globalization:-what is globalization?; globalization debates; consequences of globalization; globalization and development (Stiglitz); in defense of globalization or making globalization work.

name: Introduction to Sociology
description: General psychological principles: Cognitive, Abnormal and Social Psychology;Memory, what is memory, how do we remember what we remember, and with how much accuracy?;Learning – what are the different types of learning? The schedules of reinforcement and their effects on learning etc; Personality – different perspectives on personality. How is it measured? What are the cultural influences on an individual’s personality?;Psychologicaldisorders – how do we define abnormal? The problems of labeling and defining. Disorders of mood and personality. Psychosis; Social cognition: What are attitudes and how are they formed?

name: Classical Indian Literature in English Translation
description: Comprehensive overview of several important works of classical Indian literature in Sanskrit, Prakrit, Pali, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Persian and other languages. All texts will be read and analyzed in English translation with some references to the original texts. Works to be covered will include excerpts from the following texts, authors and compendiums: Vedas, Upanishads, Tripitika, Sattasai, Sangam, Puranas, Mahabharata (including the Bhagavad Gita), Ramayana, Asvaghosha, Kalidasa, Bhavabhuti, Amir Khusrau, Basava and others. We will also explore the greater history and evolution of literary cultures in India by contextualizing premodern literary production within a framework of synchronic social, religious and political developments. Students will also be encouraged to produce their own translations of classical literature.

name: When you cannot experiment: social science methods
description: Social status; economic status; poverty; caste; gender; social networks; social support; social capital; income inequality; neighbourhoods; surveys; longitudinal studies; natural experiments; quasi-experiments; interviews; focus groups; propensity score matching; social interventions; social change.

name: Harappan Civilization
description: The discovery of Harappan Civilization, its nomenclature, extent and distribution, evolution of the Harappan culture from the regional cultures, distinct features of Harappan culture like architecture, pottery, copper metallurgy, bead industry, weighing system, writing system, trade relations with the Mesopotamians, religion, society & political system, Harappan art, burial practices.

name: Anthropology, Citizenship and Human Rights
description: The course aims at emphasizing the division between public and academic perceptions and constructions of citizenship and human rights grounded on an anthropological approach. These topics will be illustrated in different societies from the viewpoint of disadvantaged people and social groups such as migrants, refugees, exiles, homeless, dispossessed. India will be taken as a reference and the students will be stimulated to carry out fieldwork at the margins of the social structure. Euro and ethnocentrism will be criticized. Assessment includes class attendance and participation (10%) and the presentation and debate of one topic of the syllabus (30%).The final paper consists on a report of a fieldwork observation (60%).

name: Introduction to Anthropology
description: Nature of anthropological knowledge , history, method010U', Anthropolow and experience, formulation of anthrop010U of experience, snthrop0100' of exverience and the way how individuals experience their culture; Anthropology and cognition, a priori representations and anthropological analysis, perception and/or action and their cognitive constraints, the nature of cognitive processes in Anthropology, Contemporary themes and perspectives, colonial and postcolonial studies, Europe and its "others": exoticism and the construction of otherness, from Orientalism to the Subaltern Studies; Anthrop010U of globalization, 'Ihe local, the global and the glocal, Nationalism and transnationalism; Migration and diaspora, Ethnicity and cultural minorities, citizenship and human rights; India in the world, Cosmopolitanism and diaspora, the consumption of Indian Culture.

name: Economic Concepts and Issues in Project Analysis
description: Stages of project appraisal, Marketing, Technical, Financial and Economic appraisals; Significance of investment analysis, Financial feasibility analysis or capital budgeting; Five critical parameters of investment analysis, Size or cost of project, Life of project, stream of net benefits, salvage value, discount rate; Overview of investment criteria; Project and environment, externality and valuation notions; Select case study.

name: Introduction to Politics
description: State and institutions: Exploring the nature of the modern state; comparison between Parliamentary and Presidential forms of government (India, UK and US); the judicial system (special focus on the role of the Supreme Court in defining the “Basic Structure” of the Indian Constitution); Election Commission. Political formations and representation: party politics: The ‘Congress system’ and multi-party rule in India; rise of the Hindu nationalist BJP; caste reservations in India and (race related) affirmative action politics in South Africa and United States. Social movements and civil society: role of civil society in deepening or threatening democracy (Latin America and India); NGOs; women’s movements and farmer’s movements. Identity and ethnic politics: meaning and history of secularism (unique features of Indian secularism compared to western concept of secularism) and communalism in Indian politics (ethnic and religious conflict; politics of multiculturalism (India, France and Canada). Political economy: neoliberalism and liberalization of Indian economy; politics and redistribution (critical focus on Employment Guarantee and right to work policies); the remaking of cities in the era of globalization (India, China and USA), rise of middle class politics.

name: Global Political Economy
description: Economics and politics have always been inextricably interlinked. Kingdoms and nation states have survived and thrived on their ability to forge viable economies. Starting from Antoine de Montchrétien’s coining of the term, économie politique has defined world history in modern times. Developments such as industrialization, colonization, division of labor, development of infrastructure and evolution of institutions have transformed the world beyond recognition. In an era of globalization, political economy assumes more importance to understand the past, present and future.

name: Introduction to Linguistics
description: Study of sounds in language: Vocal articulation; International Phonetic Alphabet; Distinct sound units in language, rule-based Phonology Study of meaning in language: Rule-based morphology, Computational Morphology, Semantic roles Sentence structure: Syntactic trees; Functional syntactic approaches Historical Linguistics: Language families, with emphasis on India language map, Comparative Method, Language evolution Language and cognition: Language and the brain, language and metaphor Socio-linguistics: Language and Class, caste, and gender; Code-switching and code- mixing; Professional language Doing field-work in linguistics: Linguistic ethnography; Language documentation; Ethics of fieldwork

name: Classics of World Literature, Politics and Economics
description: In India, few understand the basis of the world we live in. Indian culture is deeply rooted not only in its ancient past but also influences from the Arab World, Iran, Europe and, now the US. Yet we rarely understand the fundamental ideas that underpin our society such as Montesquieu’s idea of separation of powers that emerged from the Enlightenment, the age of Voltaire. Similarly, few delve deep into what lies beneath the idea of the zero that is a product of the uniquely speculative genius of India. This will be a course that will introduce the great minds from literature and philosophy in the context of the society and politics of their time. This is a writing heavy class in which students will spend all their time reading original texts and penning essays on them. The topics that will be covered during the course are as follows: 1. Historical context of the texts 2. Cultural beliefs underpinning the classics 3. Classics defining culture thereafter 4. Philosophical assumptions of the various authors 5. Economic circumstances in which these works were created 6. Critical examination of the texts 7. Great books as bedrock of collective imagination

name: Writing Lab
description: Critical thinking; intelligent reading; effective note taking; organizing thoughts; drawing up outlines writing practice; review and editing Critical Thinking Critical thinking means thinking for oneself and asking pertinent questions. Before penning anything, writers could begin by asking themselves three simple questions: Why am I writing? Who will read me? What impact do I want to have on my readers? Apart from questioning, critical thinking involves exploring multiple perspectives and engaging with divergent points of view. It involves understanding context, logic and argument. Only after deep, clear and hard thinking can writers pen something worthwhile. Therefore, the first session of the workshop is an initiation into critical thinking. Intelligent Reading and Effective Note Taking Reading involves not only deciphering the words on the page or screen, but also understanding what the writer is trying to say. This includes a feeling for the rhythm of the language that is inextricably interwoven with the writer’s ideas. Intelligent readers learn to pose a single question at every paragraph and page: What is the point that the writer is trying to make? Obviously, we comprehend not only speakers but also writers better when we make notes. There are a plethora of techniques to choose from, but jotting down relevant information concisely, precisely and clearly is the essence of good note taking. Therefore, the second session is a deep dive into techniques of reading and note taking. Organizing Thoughts, Drawing up Outline and Writing Clearly Following the note-taking exercises, participants choose issues, themes and topics to write on. This session involves deciding the subject matter of the writing exercise, developing ideas and then drawing up an outline. Participants will learn to develop clarity about the intent behind their writing, to know their audience, and to identify what they wish to communicate to relevant readers. This necessitates drawing up an outline, developing pertinent arguments and adducing key facts. In brief, participants in the third session will draw up a de facto communication strategy to attain clarity of purpose in their writing. Writing Exercise Once participants have validated their communication strategies with the facilitators, they will attack their writing exercise. This involves understanding the anatomy of writing and its components, including logic, drama, rhythm, rhetoric and structure. In the fourth session, participants will produce a first draft, fleshing out their skeletal outline. Proofreading, Citing Properly and Revision Nearly all first drafts are imperfect. They require reading, rereading, corrections and clarifications. Participants will learn that good writing includes proofreading and even rewriting. Critically evaluating one’s own work is both a science and an art. At its essence, it involves an appreciation of conveyed meaning. It also includes the ability to spot the divergence between intended and actual meaning. Along with proofreading, participants will focus on the importance of citing their sources. That is essential not only for getting the facts right, but also for developing credibility with readers. Publications judge writers on the quality of their sources and accuracy with which they cite them. This fifth and final session will demonstrate to participants that revising their initial drafts is like polishing diamonds. It is the final stage of a process that produces a gem. Writing Exercises followed by Reviews Finally, principles have to be applied and the proof of the pudding is in the eating. So, students will have one writing exercise after another in subsequent sessions followed by detailed reviews. Emails, cover letters, essays et al will feature one after another in these sessions.

name: Indian Systems of Ethics
description: Ethics and values: Vedic, Buddhist and Jain schools, Their philosophical and conceptual backgrounds Other texts: Arthashastra, Panchatantra, Tirukural, Subhashitas, Bhakti literature, and their treatment of ethical conflicts Concepts of success and failure in Indian ethics Specificities of Indian ethics vis-à-vis ethical systems in other cultures Historical case studies: From ancient, medieval, colonial and contemporary India, Ethics in polity, Ethics in environmental conservation, Ethics in business Ethics for a fulfilled life.

name: Introduction to Archaeology
description: Overview and Introduction • Why study or practice Archaeology? • Defining the discipline: its nature, scope and key concepts • Its space among other disciplines (Anthropology, History, Prehistory) and in the contemporary world Theoretical development • Antiquarianism to Culture-Historical approach • Paradigms of Processual and Post-Processual Archaeology • Controversies, current and future trends • Archaeology in India Archaeological methods through case studies • Pre-excavation strategy, excavation and post- excavation techniques and analyses • Data and contexts • Contribution of Sciences (chemistry, physics, geophysics, earth sciences, biological sciences and palaeoclimatology) in Archaeology • Contribution of Technology (mapping, remote sensing, viewing/imaging and computer science) in Archaeology Cultures and Chronologies • Human origins and Evolution (Africa, Europe and Asia) • Culture (Palaeolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron age, Early Historical, Medieval and Modern periods) vs material culture (artifacts and ecofacts) • Simple to complex societies to civilizations • Explaining cultural change/adaptation through case studies • Cultural heritage (tangible and intangible), cultural resource management • Laws and legislations Branches/Types of Archaeology • Social archaeology • Environmental archaeology • Public archaeology • Underwater archaeology etc.

name: Creative Writing in Practice
description: Topic 1: Types of creative writing: description v/s reflection, narrative, poetry, genre, medium. Topic 2: Techniques: plotting a story, character formation and development, style, dialogue. Topic 3: Writing Practice and Method: constrained writing, writing games, free- association, collaborative writing, building a text from a given premise. Topic 4: Writers on Writing: Interviews on writing habits, processes, writers’ insights. Some of the activities will include: 1. Writing descriptions 2. Writing reflective/observational pieces on a color, a scene, a photograph or a painting 3. Collaborative writing where one sentence is given to all and together they write a story/poem/text 4. Constrained writing where writers would be asked to use a rule such as, write a story where no character can speak 5. Writing based on free-association 6. Writing based on thought experiment 7. Writing dialogue with a given situation 8. Giving the same premise to everyone and asking them to write different genre pieces (crime, horror, romance, comedy) with that 9. Writing the same content in different forms and styles

name: Introduction to Performance Studies
description: Theme #1: Representation vs. Performance: Distinguish between representation and performance by engaging with theories of Plato, Richard Schechner, Richard Bauman, Paulla Ebron, and Michael Taussig. Performance text: Dharamvir Bharati’s Andha Yug. Theme #2: Performativity: Learn about the theoretical paradigm of performativity which informs and organizes the discipline of performance studies. Theorists include JL Austin, Judith Butler, Uma Chakravarti, and James Baldwin. Performance text: Mahesh Dattani’s Dance like a Man. Theme #3: Ephemerality vs. Endurance: Investigate the ways in which performance is capable of revealing and restaging ephemeral histories by engaging with the debate between Peggy Phelan, Jose Munoz, Rebecca Schneider, Joseph Roach, and Diana Taylor. Also, Dwight Conquergood on performance ethnography. Performance text: Maya Rao’s Walk. Pedagogical strategy emphasizes active learning methods including inquiry-based, experiential, and embodied modes of engaging students in combination with orienting lectures. Specific instructional techniques include: 1. Orienting lectures followed by two-way Q&A (clarifying questions from students for the instructor, and assessment questions from instructor to students) 2. Staggered discussions in pairs, followed by small groups, and finally at class-level (combination of think-pair-share and jigsaw methods of discussion) 3. Seminar discussion at class-level of performance texts and theoretical readings (preparatory readings pre-class followed by in-class discussion using an inquiry- based learning style) 4. Workshop productions of performance texts (experiential and embodied mode of engagement in self-directed groups)

name: Pride and Prejudice: A Critical Introduction to Sociology
description: 1. What is Sociology? • Developing a Sociological Outlook. • How can Sociology help us in our lives? • The development of Sociological thinking.

name: Research Methods in Humanities and Social Sciences
description: This is designed as a foundational course for those interested in in research in the Humanities and Social Sciences. It meant to familiarize students with a broad range of methods and analytical tools commonly employed in research in these fields. The course has been divided in to two broad components ; Quantitative and qualitative Methods. In Quantitative, method students will be taught how to conduct quantitative studies with focus on the understanding research questions, conceptual models, counter factual causal theory, confounding, mediation, moderation, measurement, scale development study designs and thread to validity of causal inferences. The quantitative segment the focus will be primarily on Ethnographic and interpretive methods and Historical research and Cultural Analysis. In the first pat ie, Ethnographic and interpretive research students will learn how to conduct interactive and Focus Group Discussions and analyze the data obtained from field surveys. In historical Research and cultural analysis, on the other hand students taught some basic conceptual and methodological issues pertaining to the historical discipline . the primary focus on the themes such as the ways and means of dealing with the object from the past. The manner of conducting archival research, various orders of evidence, the usefulness and method of studying orality and memory and the cultural significances of objects and images as well as the methods of using material for historical enquiry.

name: Humanism, Anti-humanism, and Posthumanism
description: This course focuses on the emergence and legacies of Humanism into what is now called Posthumanism. We will discuss the origins of humanism, the various forms its takes, and its contemporary manifestations; various critiques of humanism (Anti-humanism); and new forms of humanism and ways in which it is being transformed, discarded or renewed (Posthumanism). The primary focus is on the question of what is the human, and what are its relations to others. To achieve this we approach a broad range of material concerning the human’s relation to animality, exceptionalism, knowledge, identity, subjectivity, technology, and gender, and how each of these relations shift as Humanism, Anti-humanism, and Posthumanism, draw and re-draw the boundaries between them. Keywords: Animality, Exceptionalism, Gender, Humanness and the Human, Knowledge, Ideology, Identity, Subjectivity, and Technology.

name: Introduction to Indian Knowledge Systems
description: Indian knowledge systems, darshana, jñāna and vidyā, their continuous and cumulative nature; Traditions of argumentation (tarka), text interpretation, commentary and knowledge transmission; Social organisation of knowledge; Philosophical systems; Traditions of grammar and language; Systems of aesthetics, Nātyashāstra and the rasa theory; Systems of economy and polity (arthashāstra); Systems of ethics (nītishāstra) and values; Ayurveda; Systems of mathematics and astronomy, their nature, methods and accomplishments; Systems of chemistry and alchemy; Agriculture as an interplay of shāstra and loka paramparā; Systems of architecture (shilpashāstra) in principle and application; Systems of water harvesting and management; Systems of military science and martial arts.

name: Social History of Dissent
description: This course will introduce students to conceptual issues concerning protest history or the history of dissent in India and other parts of the world in the eighteenth, nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The purpose of the course is to understand the constitutive factors determining different modes of protest. The course will pick and choose from a wide variety of movements such as the French Revolution, the Revolt of 1857, some of the nationalist movements that took place in Europe during this period, the nationalist movements in India led by the Indian National Congress and the numerous peasant and 'tribal' rebellions of the colonial period in India, The course will also discuss other modes of protest which have been equally and perhaps more effective at times than direct action in resisting subjection and bringing about change. Students will be exposed to conceptual issues such as the socio-political factors determining specific modes of protests, the role of the mob in a movement, the question of 'agency' or autonomy of marginalized groups in interpreting and changing their conditions and so on. The focus of the course may vary from year to year. However the emphasis will be mostly on protests by peasants, forest dwellers, factory workers, migrants and indigenous and colonized peoples.

name: Perspectives on Indian Civilization
description: Indus civilization: A description of the first civilization on the Indian subcontinent, its main features, achievements and legacy. Post-Harappan transition and the Aryan debate: Will explore the transition of the 2 nd millennium BCE, the grey area between India's two urbanizations. Gangetic civilization: Emerging in the early 1 st millennium BCE, it will lead to India's classical civilization; its treatment here will include several recent findings. Sacred geography and the making of India: The cultural unity of India was an important contribution of the Gangetic civilization, with the concept of sacred geography playing a major part. Early Indian society: A look at some of the dominant features of early Indian society, with inputs from literary, archaeological and epigraphic sources. Woman and child in ancient India: Will examine the place of woman and child in early Indian society as far as the sources allow us to reconstruct it. Knowledge systems and knowledge transmission: India early on attempted to organize and codify all knowledge in well-defined systems; this will explore the architecture of knowledge as well as the principal systems and their interaction. Main schools of thought and belief systems: Among the above knowledge systems, India's various philosophical and belief systems have pride of place and will be outlined here. Architecture and sacred geometry in classical India: This will explore the fundamental principles and practices of classical Indian architecture, as one example from the world of art. India's ecological traditions: A rarely highlighted but culturally important area, with increasing relevance to today's context. An overview of Science and Technology in ancient India: India's contributions to the world of science and technologies are either under-represented or misrepresented (positively or negatively); this will offer a fair portrayal of the high points in those fields. India's interface with other civilizations: Civilizations never live in isolation: India richly interacted with the rest of Asia and the Greek and Arab worlds, and later with Europe. This will explore those exchanges as well as the channels and methods of influence. Ethics and values: India developed its own systems of ethics and values; after studying their basic principles and some of their manifestations in Indian history, case studies will show how they survive to this day in diverse sections of Indian society.

name: Political Thought
description: State and the social contract: Understanding the modern state and various theories about it; human nature and state of nature and the relationship between these, social contract theories; Justice: Modern conceptions of justice, basic tenets of John Rawls’s Theory of Justice, critiques and responses to Rawlsian theory by Amartya Sen, idea of fairness and justice, rejecting binary understanding of justice in favor of degrees; Liberalism and Communitarianism: Liberalism and its basic tenets, key ideals of communitarianism; communitarian critiques of liberalism, politics of Indian communitarianism, specifically Indian criticisms of liberalism in the communitarian tradition; Multiculturalism and Cosmopolitanism: Essential claims of multiculturalism in the West; key debates on citizenship in the context of immigrants, counterclaims of cosmopolitanism, toleration versus accommodation, contemporary cosmopolitanism debates; Civil Society: How is civil society theorized in the West and in India? What is its relation to democracy? ; Political Economy: Investigating the relationship between the state and market, theories of wealth creation and distribution, capitalism and its contradictions, the embedded nature of economics in society and culture; Critical theory approaches to democracy: Democracy and justice, Democracy and difference, deliberative democracy, and agonistic democracy. Secularism: meaning and origins of secularism in the West, Indian debates on secularism.

name: Perspectives in History
description: Idea and philosophy of history; Nature and limitations of the historical narrative; Relations between power and representation and its relevance for history writing; Modernity and its Impact on History writing; The call for a scientific history: What is ‘scientific history’? How did attempts to discover laws of history affect the craft of the historian?; History and theory: Can we identify universal patterns in history? What is the role of theory building in history? How does history compare with the other social sciences?; History from below: What was wrong with conventional history writing? What is so special about the call to write a ‘history from below’? ; The Postmodern Turn in History Writing: What is the validity of the historical narrative? How did the literary turn in the Social Sciences affect the way historians perceive the past?; Nationalism and Its Impact on History Writing.

name: Sociology of Tourism
description: Tourist and the leisure class: Modernity and middle class, high and low culture, postindustrial age. Tourist gaze: construction of tourist experience, ways of seeing. Sight and photography: visual depictions of destinations. Souvenirs and material culture: consumption: media, branding, globalization, Performing and staging tourism: regulation, commodification of people and places. Sustainable and ethical tourism: ecotourism, responsible tourism, social inequality, protecting nature and culture.

name: The Politics of the Environment
description: Environment and Society: changing notions of nature/environment, human impacts on environments and vice versa. Colonialism and environment: mis/use of natural resources and green imperialism. Conservation and development: contradictory relations with environment, ecological equity and justice. Human-animal relations: trust and domination, animal icons and animals in urban society. Environmental movements: local struggles, resource control, social inequity and injustice. Green capitalism and neoliberal environmentalism: markets and commodification of nature.

name: Literary Experiment in European Modernism
description: The notion of experiment; European Modernist literature; Linguistics; Meaning making process; Literary forms; The notion of Truth in Literature; Thought Experiments; Experimental compositional methods; avant-garde; Theory and Practice; Modernist Literary Movements; Technology and Literary Humanities; Mathematical thinking and Literature.

name: Philosophy, Cognition and Psychoanalysis
description: Philosophy of cognition: definition of cognition. epistentolog. Neuro-psychoanalysis: the combination of neuro-cognitivism and psychoanalysis. the neuronal idea of the unconscious. the unconscious and the non-conscious. Inter-disciplinary traffic among philosophy, psychoanalysis and cognitivism: affect in neuro-psyehology and philosophy, debate between cognitive and psychoanalytic therapies. examples from literature. theatre. the use of neural network to understand socio-political netssorks. cognitixe linguistics •conceptual metaphors and their function in different disciplines. Cultures of mind and body: definitions of mind and body in philosophy and psychoanalysis, embodied mind and extended mind debates. plasticity in neuro-science and philosophy.

name: Linguistic Anthropology
description: Topic 1) Speech and language communities: --Difference between “speech” communities comprised of speakers with diverse linguistic repertoires, and ideological projection of ‘language’ community, or affiliation to one language Subtopic A) linguistic differentiation –how diverse speech practices crystallize into languages through ideological and semiotic social processes B) language socialization—how language becomes socialized through both linguistic and cultural activity C) language hegemonies—how certain varieties become to be seen as “prestigious” (language standard), their institutional circulation, and the resistance by certain communities to standardization processes Topic 2) Semiotics—examining language as a sign-producing activity integrated with the social, economic, and material world Subtopic A) political economy of language—how language is part of a political economic system, mediated through its sign-functions conceptions of value B) linguistic reflexivity—looking at how the metalinguistic function allows us to reflect on language use and make semiotic connections between language and social practice and ideologies C) scalar processes –the way language is semiotically scaled from micro to macro- and involved in large-scale processes like globalization D) voice and heteroglossia—the study of language as it indexes certain characters, stereotypes or “voices” [in a Bakhtinian sense) and the interplay of these voices in different genres Topic 3) Conversation analysis: The method used to describe face-to-face interaction A) multi-modal analysis –analysis of conversation beyond the utterance of words but also including gesture, gaze, etc. B) transcription methods—different ways of transcribing face-to-face interaction C) Face to face interaction—analysis of real time talk and assessing how this sheds light on social, cultural, cognitive aspects of language D) Performance—how to analyze performative uses of language and its implication for linguistic and cultural theory Topic 4) Language and thought The study of the way language may condition thought or vice-versa Subtopic A) Grammatical categories and cognition—Examination of the Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis and its criticisms; looking at experiments that relate how grammatical categories may influence perception of shape, colour, tacticity, etc. B) agency and intentionality—examining how agency and intentionality are grammatically embedded in language and what implication this may have on social construction Topic 5) Writing and literacy The study of language in its written mode, including attention to reading, writing, script, and materiality of language A) Visual language Examination of how the graphic aspects of language may offer different semiotic affordances than spoken language B) Textuality The way in which the idea of “text” is understood cross-culturally, and the intersection of oral and written speech in textual production C) Language and media The mediatization of language and its importance for changing literacy practice Topic 6) Language death and revitalization Investigation into the social conditions that cause languages to become endangered or die; whether languages should be revitalized or not; community politics around dead or dying langauges; and critical analyses of programmes of language revitalization.

name: Ancient Indian Architecture
description: Introduction: Tracing the beginning of architectural activities since prehistoric times, elements of Harappan Architecture and other contemporary Indian cultures. Concepts and Architectural Typologies: Textual references (e.g. Brhatsamhita and Samaranganasutradhara) and structural forms; broad characteristics of northern and southern styles (nagara, dravida and vesara); other styles like Bhumija, Kalinga and Varata; principles of composition; alignments and axes in temple forms Beginnings of Architectural forms: Evidences from the historical period onwards, early elements and forms; archaeological evidences; early architectural features like rock-cut, brick-built, wooden and other structural forms Rock-cut architectural forms: Tracing the beginnings of rock-cut architecture of Buddhist, Hindu and Jaina faiths, their evolution and elaboration during the early medieval period; critical analysis of significant examples of all faiths; the role of monasteries, royal patronages and ordinary worshippers in shaping the establishments; spread of architectural forms in various parts of India, influences and regional styles. Structural temples of the past: Architecture of the ancient temples of Buddhist, Hindu and Jaina faiths, their evolution and different styles; tracing their essential elements in the rock-cut architecture and transformation into structural forms; critical analysis of important architectural styles (nagara, dravida and vesara); the role of masons, sculptors and royal patronage; the influence of architectural styles in India and abroad, e.g. Kailasa of Ellora and Angkor Vat, Cambodia. Indo-Islamic Architectural forms: Introduction of a new style of architecture involving arches and domes in India; architecture of tombs, mosques; analysis of regional styles of Indo-Islamic architecture like Mughal, Jaunpur, Sharqi, Deccan, Qutb Shahi styles. Role of architectural heritage in establishing universal cultural values: Whose heritage? Need for preservation and dissemination; role of UNESCO in establishing norms for World Heritage Properties, symbols of universal cultural heritage. Field Trips and onsite Explanations: At the end of the course, field trips to important architectural centres in Gujarat, e.g. Sun Temple, Modhera and Rani-ki-Vav, Patan (both can be covered in a single day), will be arranged for the students to have onsite discussions and explanations. Apart from these architectural centres, a trip to Dholavira in the Kachchh district is also planned to expose the students to site management principles and practical approaches; discussions with the Site Managers at all these places will add further to their knowledge. The field trips will be mandatory and the students will submit individual detailed report of the field trip, which will be graded.

name: Development Economics
description: I. I. INTRODUCTION TO DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS & SOME BASIC CONCEPTS • Nature of poverty across the world. Lives of the poor. • Meaning of poverty [GDP, utility, Sen’s Capabilities approach] • Meaning of Development, its indicators and determinants. II. II. FUNDAMENTALS OF THE EMPIRICAL METHODS OF DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS – CAUSALITY VERSUS CORRELATION • Causation versus correlation. The idea of selection bias and the ‘Counterfactual’. • Introduction to the methods of Impact Evaluation: o Non-experimental (Before-After and Cross-sectional comparison) o Experimental (Randomized Control Trial - RCT) o Quasi-experimental (Instrumental Variable, Regression Discontinuity, Difference-in-Differences) III. TOPICS IN DEVELOPMENT ECONOMICS: 1. Household Economics and intra-household decision-making: Considering households as independent economic units, focus will be on their decision-making processes given their resources – labour and capital. 2. Population growth and Economic Development: After looking at population trends, the focus will be on its relations to economic development and on the policy prescriptions to control population. 3. Labour and Unemployment: The definitions of employment, unemployment and labour force. Basic theories of formal and informal employment, and the labour markets in developing countries. 4. Migration: Why do people migrate despite high urban unemployment [Harris-Todaro model]? What incentivizes migrants to send remittances? Role of policies in guiding migration. 5. Education: Education as the first component of human capital. Role of human capital in economic development. Demand-side and supply-side determinants of schooling. 6. Health and Nutrition: Health and nutrition as the second component of human capital. Demand-side and supply-side determinants of health achievements, and the related policies. 7. Agricultural transformation and rural development: Role of agriculture vis-à-vis industry in economic development [The Lewis Model]. Microeconomics of farmer behaviour and agriculture. 8. Sustainable development and Environment: Managing resource use while ensuring environmental sustainability. Role of negative externalities (market failures), property rights (private, open-access, CPR), environmental activities as public good. Kuznets Environmental curve, GDP and pollution.

name: History, Concepts and Theories of Development
description: 1. Philosophical Doctrines of Development • The European setting of development. • The design of development. • The Indian reiteration.

name: Quantitative Research Methods in Social Sciences
description:  Sampling and Measurement  Descriptive Statistics  Probability Distributions  Statistical Inference: Estimation  Statistical Inference: Significance Tests  Comparison of two groups  Associations, categorical variables  Linear regressions and correlations  Introduction to Multivariate Analysis, multiple regressions and correlation  Comparing groups: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) method  Combining Regression and ANOVA  Logistic Regression

name: Qualitative Research Methods
description: Module 1– What is Qualitative Social Science Research? Introduction, qualitative modes of inquiry, philosophical foundations and perspectives of interpretive qualitative research

name: Cognitive, Sociocultural and Critical Foundations of the Learning Sciences
description: What are the learning sciences? History and evolution of the research area.; What does it mean to study learning as a science?; The acquisition and participation metaphors for learning – what each of these mean for how we understand, design for and study learning; Knowledge acquisition learning theories - Constructivism and Constructionism; Socio-cultural learning theories - Mind in society and Expansive learning; Cultural processes of learning - Culture in education, identity and learning pathways; Situated learning and communities of practice; Critical learning theories - The transformative role of education, power and sociocultural theories of learning, design of equitable learning environments; Core concepts in learning - cognitive load, scaffolding and metacognition, how each of these influence learning; Problem and project-based learning - key features and how learning happens; Preparation for future learning and productive failure - key features and how learning happens; Collaborative Learning - conditions for effectiveness, supporting learning; Embodied Learning - conditions for effectiveness, supporting learning; The role of experience in learning; The role of motivation, emotions and interest in learning - motivation theories, emotion and self-regulation, fostering interest development

name: Education, Technology and Society
description: Meaning of education and technology; History of technology in education; Evolution of education with technology; How technology shapes educational systems; Technology in/and learning; Technology in educational institutions; Technology and teachers; The AI boom and education - revolution or not; Critical reflections on education in/and technology; Digital divides and inequalities, social justice, biases; Impacts of/on digital education policies; The future of technology in education; New ways of knowing

name: Environmental Economics and Natural Resource Management
