description: Basics of Economics: Agents, Incentives, and Exchanges, Environment: Public Goods, Common-Pooled Resources, Markets Failures: Introduction to Externalities; Consumption and utility, Direct and indirect cost of production; Theory of applied welfare, Property rights (Coase), Standards, taxes, permits and subsidies (Pigou), Collective action (Ostrom); Managing rare-earth minerals, Managing forests vs fisheries; Air pollution, Water scarcity, Land degradation; Non-market valuations, Revealed preferences, Stated preferences;Trade offs, Accounting for externalities, Environment and health, Distributional effects and unintended consequences (Environmental Justice); Technology adoption, Efficient production and Javon’s paradox, Technology for regulatory monitoring; Market-based environmental regulations, Setting prices vs setting quantities, Cap-and-trade programs, Examples of market-based policy in practice; Growth versus development, Limits to economic growth, Green accounting for natural resource management

name: A Critical Journey Through Select Thoughts and Theories
description: The course will be executed through the following segments: These Beginnings • Socratic Dialogues (selections) Plato's Apology • Aristotle's Physiks — Book IV • Select Readings from Sir Francis Bacon's Advancement of Learning • Spinoza's Tractatus Politicus, Chapter-XI (of Democracy) Aesthetics, Sublimity, and Representation • Emmanuel Kant. Selections from the Critique of Judgment • Arthur Scopenhauer, The World as Will and Representation — Volume I, Book Ill • Jean-Francois Lyotard, "Lessons on the Analytic of the Sublime" • Walter Pater, "The Renaissance: studies in Art and Poetry" In a "Dialogue" • Mikhail Bakhtin. "Discourse in the Novel" from The Dialogic Imagination Julia Kristeva. Selected Chapters from Powers of Horror

name: South Asia: History, Culture and Society
description: This course provides a broad survey of fundamental developments in South Asian history, culture and society. The geographic focus of the class will be the Indian subcontinent and will include the peoples and histories of India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal and Sri Lanka. Critical ideas relating to social structures, religious practices, modes of polity and the production of art and literature will be explored from a historical perspective so as to highlight how these multi-dimensional cultural practices have both changed and remained constant over time and space. The course will examine events and ideas from ancient, medieval, early modern, colonial, post-colonial and contemporary South Asia. Themes of cultural adaptation, rejection, assimilation and transformation will provide a critical framework to understand how this culturally rich, complex and diverse region of the world has evolved over many millennia.

name: Tropes of Time and Topography: Select Fiction from South-Asia
description: Analysisoffictional texts from the subcontinent with focus on the temporal, geographical, and ideological tropes: The selected novels based on Srilankan, Bangladeshi, Indian, Pakistani, Buremese, and Nepalese regions; Enquiry into the question of “South-Asia” as a collective entity; The course content will highlight cultural-philosophical and literary elements in the novelistic medium.

name: Digital Cultures and New Media
description: The core focus of the course is to understand the significance of digital cultures and the role of new media in shaping public life and opinion. The discussion will revolve around creative dissent, strife, and the role of new media in the concerns for ‘peace’ and ‘justice’. The course will be conducted through a series of case-studies, field projects, and seminars. Some of the modules proposed to be covered in the course are as follows: (a) The story of the print media (case-studies of The Statesman, The Young India, The Harijan, The Telegraph); (b) The role of television (case-study of Doordarshan, case-study of exit polls and television ad-campaigns, the national debates, and the media trials); (c) Films of peace and strife (Issues of Censorship; case-studies of Judgment at Nuremberg, Rang De Basanti, Mr. and Mrs. Iyer, Firaaq); (d) Digital media: (Hyper-text and Blogging; the role of blogs and freedom of expression; photo-journalism and documentaries); (e) Social networks and micro- blogging (case-studies of Jan Lokpal movement, creating a tribe called IIT through social networks and blogs)

name: A Multilingual Nation
description: Language and statehood; colonial scholarship on language; linguistic reorganization of states; Language : hegemony, culture, economics; language and “dialect” debates; the Eighth Schedule, Linguistic nationalisms in India (Konkani, Bodo, Oriya, Rajasthani, Hindi, Urdu etc); the cultural politics of English; purity vs hybridity debates (“Hinglish,” “Tamlish” etc) and the role of translation in a multilingual society.

name: Scarred Nations : Partition in the Indian subcontinent
description: The ramifications of the 1947 Partition in Bengal, Punjab and Sindh have been far-reaching. Recent scholarship interrogates the temporal marker of 1947 and suggests the persistence of fragmentation of memories and identities in postcolonial India. Topics covered include but are not limited to : Indian nationalism; moments of revivalism; economies of regions; from high-politics to testimonies; narrating the ‘other’; beyond the Hindu-Muslim binaries; divisions of the word, space, music, and histories; Partition in the East; displacement and memory in the West; the cinematic Partition; Partitions of contemporary India.

name: Qualitative and Quantitative Research Methods
description: Approaches to research (qualitative versus quantitative); research questions; conceptual models; textual analysis; ethnographic methods; open-ended interviews; counterfactual causal theory; directed acyclic graphs; confounding; mediation; moderation; measurement; scale development; community-based randomized experiments; quasi-experiments; longitudinal studies (cohort/panel); cross-sectional surveys; factorial studies; threats to validity of causal inference; manuscript writing.

name: India Through the Writer’s Eye
description: This course will explore representations of Indian society and culture through a variety of IndianEnglish and Bengali texts (in translation). The special emphasis will be on an overlapping and intersecting body of texts written in the two languages. It will engage in critical and analytical thinking, and will examine the different elements that comprise fictional writings, It will also examine the various styles, trends and critical approaches to literature.

name: Theories of social epidemiology
description: This course will introduce students to theoretical perspectives that explain the distribution and determinants of disease in society. The focus will be on the application of these theories to understand the impact of social determinants of health. History of public health; development of theories of disease distribution; historical and political influences on development of theoretical perspectives; ancient theories of health', traditional epidemiological models', individual-level health behaviour theories and models; social-ecological model; psychosocial theories; social production of disease (political economy of health); ecosocial theory; importance of theory; application of social epidemiological theories to disease distribution; implications for health policy.

name: Disease, Health & Inequality
description: This course draws on medical anthropology, public health and development literature to examine the relationship between disease, health and inequality. The course will encourage students’ critical engagement in understanding the embodiment of inequality and the larger historical, social and economic forces that shape people’s health experiences globally. In particular, the course will begin with a discussion on the links between science and colonialism and subsequently move on to more contemporary debates on the inequalities of disease, suffering and infections (e.g. HIV/AIDS, Ebola and cancer), social determinants of health and illness, organ trafficking and commodification of human bodies, bioethics of global health practices and a critique of humanitarian aid and market-based medicine in the context of global health. The course will conclude with an examination of medicalized resistance to power and health as a human right.

name: Structures and Hydrology in Ancient India
description: Introduction to emergence of structures in Archaeology: Cave shelters, Making of temporary structures, Emergence of habitational structure and Emergence of Religious structures; Emergence and Development of Structural Stupa Architecture: Origin of Stupa Architecture - Theoretical aspects, Stupa Architecture - Pre-Mauryan and Mauryan periods, North and Central India- Sanchi, Bharhut, Sanghol, Deccan-Pauni, Amravati, Nagarjunkonda, Gandhar-Taxila, Structural monasteries and Chaityas; Emergence and Development of Rock-cut Architecture: Theoretical aspects. Eastern India- Udaygiri, Khandagiri, Western Deccan- Bhaje, Pitalkhora, Kondivate, Kondane, Bedasa, Karle, Nasik, Kanheri, Junnar, Ajanta and Aurangabad, Eastern Deccan-Guntapalle. Central India-Bagh, Udayagiri. Western India-Junagrh, Talaja. Hydrology and hydrologic cycle, Rain gauges, Evaporation, Flood, Water supply, water Conservation, Water quality, Water harvesting

name: Indian and Western Cultural Theory
description: Modernity and its relation with Enlightenment values; Modernity and Modernism; nationalism and colonialism and related theories; Linguistic turn in culture; Cultural turn in translation; Sex and gender; Subjectivity and self

name: Critical perspectives in Sociology and Anthropology
description: Ethnography(es) across time: from colonial to postcolonial critics; “fluid ethnicities” – ethnicity now and its future; the spatial turn in anthropology; Anthropology of the sensorial, senses of belonging and memory; Anthropology at the margins; Anthropocene. Sociological debates on the structure of inequality around social institutions (family, religion, society, state) and representations (gender, race, and ethnicity).

name: History of India, 1930-1964
description: The nature of the organized national movement of the Indian National Congress and other political organizations; Negotiations between the British government and nationalist leaders and other interest groups; Growth of the left in the thirties and forties; Nature and scope of the final phase of the Gandhian mass movement; Growth of communal forces in the country and the consolidation of regional and cultural identities; Circumstances leading to partition and its myriad consequences; Intellectual debates concerning the making of a constitution for the new state; Survey of the early years in the life of the new nation state; The philosophy of political and economic policies pursued by the government; Issues of pluralism and national integration; Foreign policy initiatives of the Government of India; Challenges encountered by the new nation state, policies followed and their long term impact on the people of the country.

name: Literature, Theory and Social Context
description: What is defined as literature?; What role does canon, class, taste and cultural hegemony play in this?; Who is an Author?; What do Indian traditions and Western philosophy have to say about this pleasure and the threat it might pose to a “moral” society?; What are the social conditions that produce specific kind of literature? Deconstructive analysis and literature

name: Public Policy and its Impact Evaluation
description: 1. Introduction to Public Policy concepts: a. Markets and efficiency b. When and why do markets fail? c. Alternatives, when markets fail. d. Role of public policy 2. Basic concepts for quantitative research a. Mean, Standard Deviation and correlation b. Sampling distribution and t-test c. Regressions: estimation, interpretation of coefficients and standard errors. 3. Statistical Quantitative analytical tool will be used for understanding data, producing descriptive tables and figures, and for running regressions and interpreting them 4. Health/Nutrition policy a. Health and Nutrition, their measurements, and the role of public policy b. POLICY: India’s Integrated Child Development Scheme (ICDS) c. Impact Evaluation of the ICDS, using the following method: • METHOD: Use of Controls and Fixed Effects in Regressions 5. Employment policy a. Unemployment, labor markets in developing countries, and the role of public policy b. POLICY: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA) c. Impact Evaluation of MGNREGA in India, using the following method: • METHOD: Difference-in-differences 6. Environmental policy a. Economics of Environmental Policies. Negative Externalities. b. POLICY: India’s Environmental Policy c. Impact Evaluation of the Environmental Clearances in India, using the following method: • METHOD: Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD) 7. Gender policy a. Women empowerment, agency and voice. b. POLICY: Political reservation for women in India c. Impact Evaluation of the 73rd Amendment to the Indian Constitution reserving one-third of Panchayat seats for women • METHOD: Instrumental Variable (IV)

name: Critical Perspectives in Anthropology
description: Module 1–Theory and History of Anthropology History of Anthropology in India and Europe; Social Evolutionism; Structural-Functionalism; Structuralism; Practice Theory; Semiotics

name: Critical Perspectives in Sociology
description: The development of Sociological thinking Developing a Sociological Outlook. How can Sociology help us in our lives? Culture The problem of modernity and hegemony Social Structures in India Perspectives and Processes of Social Transformation. Introduction to Caste. Introduction to Tribes. Gender and Religious Minorites. Social change in India Affirmative Action. Social Movements. Assimilation v/s integration. Sociology of Science and Technology Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The Social Study of Science. Globalisation and Media Imperialism

name: Reading Philosophy: Classical, Contemporary, and Global Perspectives
description: Module-I: Thinking and Acting: What is Philosophy. Analytic/Continental Philosophy, World Philosophy, Comparative Philosophy. Reading and Identifying Arguments; Working with dense philosophical prose; Self and World; Thinking; Acting; Judging ● Module-II Knowledge and Reality: Metaphysics and Ontology; Epistemology. Western (Descartes, Kant ; Hume; Locke) Indian and Buddhist ( Nagarjuna, Nyaya, Vedanta); Islamic (Avicenna); Japanese (Dogen), Chinese (Chuang Zhu). ● Module-III: Ethics and Justice: Plato’s Republic; Aristotle Nicomachean Ethics; Bentham on Suffering; Confucian Ethics, Buddhist and Non-Western Ethics; Practical Ethics; Indigenous Ethics; Value Theory; Justice and Injustice; Freedom.

name: Academic Communication: Explanation and Paraphrasing
description: Writing-to-Communicate: Components of analytical reading and writing; Writing-to-Communicate versus writing-to-learn; Conventional transition cues; Predicting argument structure and flow; Defining and expressing ideas: Structure of a concept definition; SEE-I method; Identifying and interpreting jargon; Reference of discipline-specific jargon; Reading and self-assessing comprehension: Analytical reading; Metacognitive strategies for successful reading and interpretation; Self-checking question building; Preparation for explanation: Target audience assessment; Goal and purpose setting; Gauging scope of details; Techniques for a clear explanation; Summarizing and paraphrasing: Thesis statement; Appraisal of text, Goal-driven breaking down and reformulation of text; Tools for academic success: Reading notes, annotated bibliography, and research log; Systematic Literature Research.

name: Mixed Methods in Research
description: Nature and foundations of mixed methods research: need for mixed methods, advantages and challenges of using mixed methods, historical foundations: when and why, philosophical foundations: philosophy and worldviews applied to mixed methods, theoretical foundations; core mixed methods designs: key concepts that inform mixed methods designs, the convergent design, the explanatory sequential design, the exploratory sequential design; introducing a mixed methods study: stating the research problem, developing the purpose statement, writing research questions and hypotheses; collecting data in mixed methods research: procedures for collecting qualitative and quantitative data, data collection within the mixed methods designs; analyzing and interpreting data in mixed methods research: procedures in qualitative and quantitative data analysis and interpretation, including appropriate software/tools for analysis, integrated data analysis and interpretation within the mixed methods designs; evaluating mixed methods research: qualitative and quantitative evaluation criteria, mixed methods evaluation criteria

name: Academic Communication: Argumentation and Reasoning
description: This course is designed for scholars in Social Sciences whose academic work crucially requires reasoning with data or previous literature. 1. Academic reasoning: Abstract reasoning for academic achievement; Basic structure of logical reasoning; Commonalities and differences between plagiarism and well-thought-out writing. 2. Message and goal of argumentation: Narrowing down the focus; Thesis statement; Selection by exclusion; Connecting reading to writing; Systematic Literature Research; Reading notes, annotated bibliography, and research log; Critical reading and extrapolating research ideas from reading materials. 3. Target audience assessment: Target audience’s current opinion on the topic, common ground, and point of opposition; Target journals and conferences; Identification of scholars relevant to the topic of students’ choice. 4. Rhetorics, strategies, and heuristics: Genre-specific argumentations and rhetorics; Logical fallacies; Metacognitive work; Common heuristics and strategies for academic persuasion; Probing, listing, freewriting, and outlining. 5. Stylistic cues of argumentation: Different effects from keeping an objective perspective versus expressing one’s own voice; Explanation versus argument; Displaying originality while explaining; Structuring claims surrounded by facts, data, and other scholars’ work 6. Data selection and alignment: Types of evidence; Source analysis and appraisal; Evidence-based writing; Goal-driven breaking down and reformulation of message and argumentation; Meta-analytic approaches from primary sources.

name: Ethnoarchaeology
description: Definition, historiography, scope, and methodology; role of analogy, Nature and interrelationship of archaeological and ethnographic records, Relevance of oral tradition in reconstructing the past, Role of ethnography in interpreting archaeological records and past societies; Forager and collector models and their adaptability to Palaeolithic and Mesolithic societies, Insights for reconstructing past material culture: settlement patterns, subsistence, and technology, Middle range theory; Shifting cultivation practices, Monuments and megalithic traditions, Ceramic traditions and cultural implications; Case studies of artefact traditions and their cultural significance; Andaman Islanders, Birhors, Chenchus, Yanadis, Veddas, Kanjars, Pardhis, and Van Vagris; The Bushmen of the Kalahari Desert, The Eskimos of Alaska, Ethnoarchaeological perspectives in Southeast Asia; Visit to a nearby Knowledge Keepers Village

name: Mixed Methods in Research
description: Need for mixed methods; Advantages and challenges of using mixed methods; Historical foundations: when and why; Philosophical foundations: philosophy and worldviews applied to mixed methods; Theoretical foundations; Key concepts that inform mixed methods designs; Introduction to the three core mixed methods designs; The convergent design; The explanatory sequential design; The exploratory sequential design; Complex applications of core mixed methods designs; Introducing a mixed methods study ; Sampling in mixed methods research; Data collection and analysis in mixed; Inference process mixed methods research; Integrated interpretation within the mixed methods designs

name: Environmental Economics and Natural Resource Management***
description: Basics of economics: agents, incentives, and exchanges, environment: public goods, common-pooled resources, markets failures: introduction to externalities, environmental footprint of consumption, consumption and utility, direct and indirect cost of production, environmental welfare theory, theory of applied welfare, economics of renewable vs non-renewable resources, managing rare-earth minerals, managing forests vs fisheries, decoding concurrent environmental challenges, air pollution, water scarcity, land degradation, non-market valuation, cost-benefit analysis, trade offs, accounting for externalities, environment and health, distributional effects and unintended consequences (environmental justice), environmental policies, sustainability and economic growth, growth versus development, limits to economic growth, green accounting for natural resource management

name: The Discovery of Mathematics
description: Early history of mathematics - Origin of numbers, zero, counting, computation, in various civilizations; A chronological treatment of about 20 mathematical personalities and covering their life stories, highlighting famous and/or elegant results, contributions to fields of mathematics and their applications (if any); Famous conjectures and famous paradoxes; Periodic discussions on framing of a problem mathematically, the need for rigor, and various approaches to proofs

name: Engineering and Democracy: An Indian Imperative
description: Facets of Engineering: What is the purpose of engineering?; multifaceted academic discourse on science, technology and engineering; engineering as a captive discourse of market forces; military-industrial complex; the history of the discipline of engineering in the West and India. Paradox of Democracy: What is the purpose of democracy?; requirements for a flourishing democracy; technocrats vs. democrats; the vulnerabilities of democracy. Portrait of a virtuous engineer: common grounds between Democracy and Engineering for technical progress and social justice; human use of engineering and democracy; the challenges of the Indian context. Intellectual independence: Reorganization of knowledge in Information Age; opportunity for newly independent countries like India; capturing the intellectual vibrancy of Indian freedom struggle; inspiration from the makers of modern India, orienting engineering for Social Minimum.

name: Technological progress and Human Values
description: Progress and Entropy: Views of Norbert Wiener; (a). Technological Changes in Europe and in India between 1600 and 1874, (b) Reading of Hind Swaraj: Evaluation of values in Europe and in India; The origin and evolution of engineering education: The American influence after Morrill Act; Computer and Information Ethics: With emphasis on Virtue Ethics; Possibility of Technological Progress within Moral Boundaries: Role of Democracy.

name: Ancient Indian Technology
description: This course will cover a whole range of issues from Archaeology to Ethnography of use of stone, ceramic and glass in pre-modern India. This course is going to alert students to the contextual nature of technologies and how different societies respond to different needs within the environmental, material and cultural constraints. This course will also involve a number of experimental studies and visit to the surviving traditional industries. History of Ancient Indian technology; Surviving traditional technologies in India; Raw materials used in Palaeolithic Technology: Rocks; Palaeolithic Technology: Evolution of stone tools through time; Stone working: Stone objects (grinding stone, beads etc.); Shell, Bone and Antler Working; Ceramic Production: Pottery and Terracotta; Glass Making and Working; Furnaces/Kilns; The carriers of our heritage

name: Building Biotechnology : Science, Ethics, Law & Business
description: Science: Inspiring properties of biological systems, Biotechnology vs. Pharmaceutical Drug Development, Tools and Techniques (Bioinformatics, Proteomics, Microarrays, Functional Genomics, Manufacturing, Nanotechnology), Green (agricultural), White (industrial) and Red (medical) biotechnology applications. Law: Intellectual property, licensing and regulation. Business: Business models, Company characteristics, R&D Stages, Project Selection, and Outsourcing. Ethics: Research involving animals and human subjects, Animal cloning, Privacy issues.

name: Human Evolution
description: ‘We’, ‘Us’ or ‘Modern Humans’ are a consequence of our evolutionary past, traversing both biological and cultural interconnected developments. The course aims to inculcate in students an appreciation and understanding of how we became who we are today and recognize the current diversity in humankind through factual knowledge. This course will introduce students to some of the fundamental theories, debates, concepts, methods and nomenclatures that contribute to the study of human evolution. Students will be able to reconstruct our bio-cultural history through multidisciplinary and scientific perspectives including evolutionary biology, palaeoanthropology, prehistoric archaeology, and genetics. Topic 1: Brief introduction to subjects, theories, methods and concepts which are employed in the study of human evolution. • Scope of subjects such as physical anthropology, palaeoanthropology and prehistoric archaeology. • History of evolutionary thought – Cuvier, Hutton, Lyell, Linnaeus, Lamarck, Darwin, Mendel, Huxley. Concepts of adaptation, natural selection, speciation, mutation, heredity and variation. Micro and macro evolution. • Linnaean system of classification. • Geological time scale, prehistoric timeline, importance of context and material culture in Archaeology, relative and absolute dating techniques and methods. • Miocene, Pliocene and Pleistocene epoch environmental settings. • Human osteology and morphometrics, process of fossilization and taphonomy. Topic 2: Assessment of the human fossil record: tracing the major stages of human evolution during the Miocene- Pliocene – Early Pleistocene epochs (between 23 and 5 million years ago to 1.2 million years ago). • Primate evolution. • Hominoidea superfamily; divergences between- Hominidae and Hylobatidae families, Homininae and Ponginae sub-family, Hominini and Gorillini tribe, Hominina and Panina sub-tribe. • The earliest hominins- Sahelanthropus, Orrorin and Ardipithecus. • Genus Australopithecus, Kenyanthropus and Paranthropus. • Key fossil finds (discovery, prehistoric sites, context and dating); their origins; description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural and adaptive characteristics. Topic 3: Assessment of the human fossil record: tracing the major stages of evolution of our own genus during the Pleistocene (2.5 million years ago to 12,000 years before present) epoch. • The earliest Homo and the earliest archaeological record • From Homo ergaster to Modern Homo sapiens. • Recent Homo species discoveries from South Africa and South East Asia • Key fossil finds (discovery, prehistoric sites, context and dating); their origins; description and comparative assessment of their biological, behavioural, adaptive and cultural characteristics. Topic 4: Special lectures on • Out of Africa theory and the extant human diversity (one lecture by Sharmistha Majumdar) • Using DNA to trace the most recent common ancestor (one lecture by Umashankar Singh) The course will be taught through lectures based on published and online sources along with documentary viewing followed by discussions, human osteology laboratory sessions, museum/field visits and workshops for experimental replication of stone, bone, shell and ceramic objects.

name: Independent Project
description: The course will involve open ended projects on topics selected by students other than their thesis; It requires at least 100 hours of work to earn 3 credits by the student in a given semester. The disciplines will inform the students about the requirement of report submission and presentations. Students are encouraged to choose topics from outside their field of expertise; Students can seek guidance from a faculty member in selecting the topic and developing the scope of work.

name: Independent Research Seminar
description: The course will involve comprehensive literature surveys and open seminars on topics selected by students other than their thesis; It is expected that two to four of such seminars will be delivered in a semester on the same or different topics; It will also require submission of at least two reports during the semester, which will illustrate the work done by the student towards these credits and also include a summary of any other seminars attended by him/her in the Institute or outside during that period. Students may be encouraged to choose some of those topics from outside their field of expertise; Students can seek guidance from a faculty member in selecting the topic and developing the framework of the presentation.

name: Mathematics I
description: Review of limits, continuity, differentiability; Mean value theorem, Taylor’s Theorem, Maxima and Minima; Riemann integrals, Fundamental theorem of Calculus, Improper integrals, applications to area, volume; Convergence of sequences and series, Newton’s method, Picard’s method; Multi-variable functions, Partial Derivatives, gradient and directional derivatives, chain rule, maxima and minima, Lagrange multipliers; Double and Triple integration, Jacobians and change of variables formula; Parametrization of curves and surfaces, vector fields, Line and surface integrals; Divergence and curl, Theorems of Green, Gauss, and Stokes.

name: Mathematics II
description: Linear Algebra: Vectors in R n ; Vector subspaces of R n ; Basis of vector subspace; Systems of Linear equations; Matrices and Gauss elimination; Determinants and rank of a matrix; Abstract vector spaces, Linear transformations, Matrix of a linear transformation, Change of basis and similarity, Rank-nullity theorem; Inner product spaces, Gram-Schmidt process, Orthonormal bases; Projections and least-squares approximation; Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, Characteristic polynomials, Eigenvalues of special matrices; Multiplicity, Diagonalization, Spectral theorem, Quadratic forms. Differential Equations: Exact equations, Integrating factors and Bernoulli's equation; Orthogonal trajectories; Lipschitz condition, Picard’s theorem; Wronskians; Dimensionality of space of solutions, Abel-Liouville formula; Linear ODE’s with constant coefficients; Cauchy-Euler equations; Method of undetermined coefficients; Method of variation of parameters; Laplace transforms, Shifting theorems, Convolution theorem.

name: Calculus of Single Variable and Linear Algebra
description: Linear Algebra: Systems of linear equations, matrices and Gauss elimination, determinant of a matrix, Invertible matrices, transpose of a matrix, special matrices (diagonal, triangular, symmetric etc); Vector space, linear independence, basis and dimension, linear transformations, matrix of a linear transformation, change of basis and similarity, rank of a matrix, Rank-Nullity theorem, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, characteristic polynomials, multiplicity, diagonalization; Inner product spaces, Gram- Schmidt process, orthonormal bases, projections and least-squares approximation, Spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices, Singular value decomposition (overview).

name: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: First order ODE: geometric meaning, direction fields, separable and exact equations, integrating factors, linear equations, orthogonal trajectories; Existence and uniqueness of solutions, Second order linear ODE: general principles, equations with constant coefficients, Euler-Cauchy equations; Linear independence of solutions and the Wronskian, Nonhomogeneous ODEs: method of undetermined coefficients, method of variation of parameters, Higher order linear ODE: general principles; Series solutions: Legendre’s equation and the Legendre polynomials, Frobenius’ method, the Bessel’s functions; Laplace transforms: inverse transform, linearity, transform of derivatives and integrals, differential equations, unit step functions, Dirac’s delta function, shifting theorems, differentiation and integration of transforms, convolution, integral equations.

name: Mathematics III
description: Complex Analysis: Definition and properties of analytics functions; Cauchy-Riemann equations, Harmonic functions; Power series and their properties; Elementary functions; Cauchy’s theorem and its applications; Taylor series and Laurent expansions; Residues and the Cauchy residue formula; Evaluation of improper integrals; Conformal mappings. Differential Equations: Review of power series and series solutions of ODE’s; Legendre’s equation and Legendre polynomials; Regular and irregular singular points, method of Frobenius; Bessel’s equation and Bessel’s functions; Sturm-Liouville problems; Fourier series; D’Alembert solution to the Wave equation; Classification of linear second order PDE in two variables; Vibration of a circular membrane; Fourier Integrals, Heat equation in the half space.

name: Mathematics IV
description: Probability and Statistics Random Experiments; Events; Probability; Random variables; Probability Distributions: Discrete and Continuous Distributions, Mean and Variance of Distributions, Distributions of Several Random variables; Random sampling; Estimation of parameters; Confidence Intervals; Testing of Hypotheses; Goodness of fit - 2 test; Quality control and Acceptance Sampling; Confidence intervals for regression parameters. Numerical Methods Interpolation: Divided differences, Lagrange and Hermite Interpolation; Weierstrass and Taylor’s theorem (statements); Numerical Quadrature (Trapezoidal Simpson’s and Gauss); Numerical Linear Algebra: Matrix norms, Condition number, Direct and Iterative Methods for Linear Systems, LU and QR Decompositions, Eigenvalue Problems, Inclusion of Matrix Eigenvalues, Eigenvalues by Iteration (Power Method); Numerical Solutions to ODE: Euler’s, Multistep, Runge-Kutta methods, BVP- Finite Difference Method, Introduction to Finite Element Method; Numerical solutions to PDE: Elliptic PDE.

name: Numerical Methods
description: Mathematical modelling, Approximations and round-off errors, truncation errors and the Taylor series; Roots of equations: graphical methods, bisection method, simple fixed-point iteration, Newton-Raphson method, Secant method; Systems of nonlinear equations, linear algebraic equations: Gauss elimination, Gauss-Jordan, LU and QR Decomposition, matrix norms and condition numbers, special matrices and Gauss-Seidel method; Interpolation: Newton’s divided difference, Lagrange interpolating polynomials; Numerical Integration: Trapezoidal, Simpson’s, Gauss; Numerical differentiation; Finite Difference methods for ODEs: Euler’s method, Runge- Kutta Method, stiff solvers, multistep methods, boundary value problems (shooting method and finite difference method); Eigenvalue problems: inclusion of matrix eigenvalues, eigenvalues by Iteration (Power Method); Finite Difference methods for PDEs: elliptic equations, parabolic equations.

name: Introduction to Partial Differential Equations
description: Classification of first order partial differential equations (PDEs): method of characteristics, Application to traffic flow, Classification of 2nd order PDEs, different boundary conditions and uniqueness of solutions Fourier series, Fourier integrals and Fourier transforms Separation of variables: heat and wave equations, Use of Fourier series, Fourier transforms and applications to heat equations in R and half space. Method of characteristics, D’ Alembert’s solution of the wave equation, Rectangular membrane, Laplacian in polar coordinates and circular membrane, Laplace’s equation in cylindrical and spherical coordinates Introduction to numerical methods to PDEs: finite difference method

name: Calculus of Several Variables
description: Review: Vector Algebra, Equations of lines and planes Vector functions - continuity and differentiability, Arc length, Unit tangent vector, Curvature and normal vectors of a curve Functions of several variables: Continuity, Partial derivatives and Differentiability, Gradient, Directional Derivatives, Tangent plane, and Normal line Necessary and Sufficient conditions for Maxima and Minima, Saddle points, Lagrange multipliers Double integral, Fubini’s theorem, Volumes and Areas, Polar coordinates, Triple integrals, Applications, Cylindrical and spherical coordinates, Line integrals, Vector fields Green’s theorem, Surface area, Surface integrals, Divergence and Curl of a vector field, Stokes’ theorem, The divergence theorem, and applications.

name: Introduction to Complex Analysis
description: Review of complex numbers, complex plane, polar form, powers and roots, regions in the complex plane, topological properties, functions of a complex variable and their mapping properties, limits, Riemann sphere and stereographic projection, continuity, derivatives, analytic functions, Cauchy- Riemann equations, Laplace’s equation, Elementary functions: exponential function, logarithmic function, general power, trigonometric and hyperbolic functions; Complex integration, line integrals, statement of Cauchy’s integral theorem, statement of Cauchy’s integral formula, Derivatives of analytic functions, statement of Morera’s theorem, Liouville’s theorem, statement of Maximum modulus principle, Power series, Taylor series, Taylor’s theorem, Laurent series, singularities, and zeros, behavior at infinity, residues, statement of residue theorem, evaluation of real integrals, Conformal mappings, and applications (e.g. control theory, fluid dynamics, wave propagation, fracture mechanics etc.).

name: Basic Algebra
description: Groups: Review of groups, subgroups, homomorphisms, finite and discrete groups of motions, group actions, class equation, Sylow theorems, classification of groups of small order, the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups, generators and relations, SL(R), SU(2), simplicity of alternating groups and PSL(2), Nilpotent and Solvable Groups, Normal and Subnormal Series. Rings: Rings, ideals, quotient rings, Euclidean domains, principal ideal domains, unique factorization domains, primes in Z[i] and Fermat's 2-square theorem, ideal classes in imaginary quadratic fields. Representations of Groups: Definitions, Irreducible representations, Unitary Representations, Characters.

name: Complex Analysis
description: Spherical representation of extended complex plane, Analytic Functions, Harmonic Conjugates, Elementary Functions, Cauchy Theorem and Integral Formula, Homotopic version, Power Series, Analytic Continuation and Taylor’s theorem, Zeros of Analytic functions, Hurwitz Theorem, Maximum Modulus Theorem and Open Mapping Theorem, Laurent’s Theorem, Classification of singularities, Residue theorem and applications, Evaluation of real integrals, Argument Principle, Theorems of Rouche and Gauss-Lucas, Winding numbers, Mobius Transformations, Schwarz-Christoffel Transformation, Fractals, Algorithms to generate Sierpinski Gasket.

name: Linear Algebra
description: 1. Review: System of linear equations

name: Nonlinear Functional Analysis
description: Calculus in Banach spaces, Inverse & implicit function theorem, Fixed point theorems of Brouwer, Schauder&Tychonoff; Fixed point theorems for nonexpansive& set-valued maps; Degree theory: TherBrouwer degree, The Leray-Schauder degree, Borsuk's theorem, Genus, Index and applications to differential equations; Bifurcation theory: The Lyapunov-Schmidt method, Morse's lemma, A perturbation method, Krasnoselskii's theorem, Rabinowitz theorem. Variational methods: minimization of functionals, the Palais-Smale condition, the deformation lemma, multiplicity of critical points, Mountain pass theorem, Lyusternik- Schnirelmann theorem.

name: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: Vector fields, Graphical representation of solutions, Lipschitz functions, Integral inequalities, Uniqueness of solutions, Boundary value problems, Green’s functions, Distribution of zeros of solutions, Functional analytical preliminaries, Existence of solutions by Picard’s method, Existence by Perron’s method, Fixed point method, Uniqueness and continuous dependence, Continuity and differentiability with respect to initial conditions and parameters, Continuation of solutions, Linear equations, general theory, Solutions of linear equations with constant coefficients, Equations with periodic coefficients, Floquet theory, Classification of stationary points and phase portraits, Oscillation and boundedness of solutions, Lyapunov theory of stability, Poincare-Bendixson theorem and applications.

name: Real Analysis of One Variable
description: 1. Functions, finite and Infinite sets; Real numbers as an ordered, complete, Archimedean field. 2. Euclidean topology on real numbers; Open sets, closed sets, compact sets, Nested intervals theorem, Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem; Heine-Borel theorem. 3. Convergence of sequences in real field, series of numbers, ratio and root test of convergence, Bolzano-Weierstrass and Cauchy criterion. 4. Continuous functions, properties, Intermediate Value Theorem, uniform continuity. 5. Derivatives for real functions, extreme values, Rolle’s theorem, mean value theorem, Taylor’s theorem. 6. Riemann integrals, Fundamental theorem of calculus. Sequence of functions, Pointwise and uniform convergence, interchange of limit and integration/differentiation, Power Series.

name: Topology
description: Topological Spaces: open sets, closed sets, neighborhoods, bases, sub bases, limit points, closures, interiors, continuous functions, homeomorphisms, nets and filters.Examples of topological spaces: subspace topology, product topology, metric topology, order topology, quotient topology.Connectedness and Compactness: Connected spaces, Connected subspaces of the real line, Components and local connectedness, Compact spaces, Heine- Borel Theorem, Local-compactness. Separation Axioms: Hausdorff spaces, Regularity, Complete Regularity, Normality, Urysohn Lemma, Tychonoff embedding and UrysohnMetrization Theorem, Tietze Extension Theorem, Tychnoff Theorem, One-point Compactification. Complete metric spaces and function spaces, Characterization of compact metric spaces, equicontinuity, Ascoli-Arzela Theorem, Baire Category Theorem. Applications: space filling curve, nowhere differentiable continuous function, Stone-CechCompactification.

name: Introduction to Optimization
description: Linear Programming: Geometry of Linear Programming, Simplex Methods, Revised Simplex Methods, Sensitivity Analysis, Two-Phase Method, Duality and Applications (transportation and assignment problems) Constrained and Unconstrained Optimization: Convexity, Separating Planes and Farkas’ Lemma, Necessary and Sufficient Conditions, Lagrange and Karush-Kuhn-Tucker Formulations Algorithms: One-Dimensional Search Methods (Bisection, Secant, Golden Section Search), Analysis and convergence of Gradient Methods, Newton’s Method: Analysis of Newton’s Method, Levenberg-Marquardt Modification, Newton’s Method for Nonlinear Least-Squares, Conjugate Direction Methods for quadratic and non-quadratic problems, Quasi-Newton Methods: DFP and BFGS Algorithm

name: AI and Mathematics
description: Basics of Machine Learning: Supervised and unsupervised learning; algorithms such as least squares, support vector machines, and logistic regression; dimensionality reduction techniques; Statistical and Information-Theoretic methods: Probability and statistics (review of topics from basic probability theory, distributions, Markov chains, maximum likelihood estimation, supervised PAC learning); information theory (entropy, cross-entropy, Kullback-Leibler divergence); mutual information for feature selection; tail bounds; Deep Learning Theory: Single-layer and multilayer perceptron; activation functions; backpropagation and the chain rule; universal approximation theorem; basics of variational quantum algorithms and quantum neural networks; convolutional neural network.

name: Applied Linear Algebra, Probability and Statistics
description: Linear Algebra I-A. Syllabus Linear Vector Spaces Definition, Basic properties, Examples of vector spaces. All about Matrices I Review of basics (eigenvalue/eigenvectors), Characteristic equation, Geometric and Algebraic multiplicity of Eigenvalues, Conditions for diagonalizability of a matrix and examples of non-diagonalizable matrices, Cayley-Hamilton theorem, Exponentiating a matrix All about Matrices II Matrices as operators on linear vector spaces, Important classes of matrices: Hermitian, Unitary, and Positive Definite/Semidefinite matrices (Specific Examples of important classes for engg: Toeplitz, Circulant, Rotation Matrix etc.) Rank and Nullity. Inner Products Definition of Inner products, orthonormal basis sets, Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization, Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, Dual spaces (if time permits) Numerical Linear Algebra: Matrix Reduction/Elimination/Decomposition Gaussian Elimination, Cholesky Decomposition for positive definite matrices, Least Squares Approximation, Singular Value Decomposition, Condition Number, Sparse Matrices.

name: Applied Vector Calculus and Differential Equations
description: Vector Calculus I-A. Syllabus Review: Some Preliminaries Vectors, Vectors in 3-d, Scalar Product, Triple Product, Einstein summation convention for manipulation of vectors. Differential Operator Gradient, Divergence, and Curl operators and their interpretation Integration Line Integrals, Surface Integrals, and Volume Integrals, Numerical Integration: Newton-Coates Vector Integral Theorems Gauss’ theorem, Green’s Theorem, and Stokes’ theorem

name: Nonlinear Analysis with Applications
description: Elements of Nonlinear Analysis: Review of linear analysis, Different forms of continuity of nonlinear operators in Banach spaces, Concepts of Gateaux and Frechet differentiability, Subdifferential of convex functions and duality mapping, Inverse and implicit function theorems. Theory of Monotone Operators and Fixed Point Theorems: Definition with examples of monotone operators, Surjectivity theorems, Well-known fixed point theorems, Solvability of operator equations, Hammerstein operator equations and applications to boundary value problems and integral equations.Variational Analysis with Optimization and Control: Bilinear and semilinear forms, Convex functionals and minimization, Controllability in finite and infinite dimensional spaces.

name: Commutative Algebra
description: Rings and Ring Homomorphism, Nil radical and Jacobson radical, Modules, Direct Sum and product, Tensor product, Exact sequence, Rings and Modules of fractions, Noetherian and Artinian Rings, Primary Decomposition, Integral extension, Krull dimension of ring, Noether Normalization lemma, Hilbert's Nullstellensatz, Completions, Graded Rings, Artin-Rees lemma, Associated graded rings.

name: Differential Topology
description: The course aims at developing the fundamentals of Differential Topology. The following is an outline of the topics that will be covered: ● Manifolds, Partition of Unity, Differentiable manifolds, Orientation of a manifold, Smooth Maps: Immersion and Submersion, Germs of smooth maps, Tangent vectors and Derivations ● Jacobian criterion, Sard's theorem, Tangent Bundle, Vector Fields, Exterior Algebra, Differential Forms, Integration on Manifolds, complexes of groups and their cohomology, de Rham Cohomology ● If time permits, we will look into more advanced topics such as de Rham isomorphism theorem.

name: Graph Theory and Applications
description: Graphs, Basic Definitions; Matching; Covering and Packing, Erdos-Posa Theorem; Connectivity, Menger’s Theorem, Mader’s Theorem; Planar graphs, Five Color Theorem for Planar Graphs, Kuratowski’s Theorem; Colouring, Konig’s Theorem, Vizing’s Theorem, The Perfect Graph Theorem; Flows, Max Flow Min Cut Theorem; Extremal graph theory, Turan’s Theorem, Regularity Lemma, Erdos-Stone Theorem; Ramsey Theory for Graphs; Minors, Trees and WQO, Tree Decompositions, An Introduction to the Graph Minor Theorem.

name: Measure Theory
description: Semi-algebra, Algebra, Monotone class, Sigma-algebra, Monotone class theorem. Measure spaces, Outline of extension of measures from algebras to the generated sigma-algebras,Measurable sets; Lebesgue Measure and its properties. Measurable functions and their properties; Integration and Convergence theorems. Introduction to Lp-spaces, Riesz-Fischer theorem; Riesz Representation theorem for L2 spaces. Absolute continuity of measures, Radon-Nikodym theorem. Dual of Lp-spaces, Product measure spaces, Fubini's theorem. Fundamental Theorem of Calculus for Lebesgue Integrals.

name: Number Theory
description: Divisibility, Bezout's Identity, Linear Diophantine Equations, Prime Numbers, Congruences, Congruences with a Prime-power Modulus, Chinese Remainder Theorem, The Groups of Units U n , Quadratic Reciprocity, Finite Fields. Arithmetical functions and Dirichlet multiplication, big oh notation, Euler's summation formula, average order of some arithmetical functions, summation by parts, Chebyshev's functions, the Prime Number Theorem, Dirichlet characters, Gauss sums, Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions, Introduction to the theory of the Riemann zeta function, zero-free regions for zeta(s).

name: Partial Differential Equations
description: First order quasi-linear equations: method of characteristics, Monge cone, Nonlinear equations, Cauchy-Kowalewski’s theorem, Higher order equations and characteristics, Classification of second order equations, Riemann’s method and applications, Wave equation and D’ Alembert’s method, Method of decent and Duhamel’s principle, Solutions of equations in bounded domains and uniqueness of solutions. Laplace equations: mean value property, maximum principle, Poisson's formula, BVPs for Laplace’s and Poisson’s equations, Green’s functions and properties, Existence theorem by Perron’s method, Heat equation, Uniqueness of solutions via energy method, Uniqueness of solutions of IVPs for heat conduction equation, Stability theory, Finite difference solutions of partial differential equations.

name: Stochastic Differential Equations
description: Overview of measure-theoretic probability; Stochastic processes, filtrations, stopping times, martingales; Brownian motion, construction and properties, Kolmogorov's extension and continuity theorems; Stochastic integrals, construction and properties, Ito versus Stratonovich, Ito’s formula, Levy's characterisation of Brownian motion, Girsanov’s theorem; Stochastic differential equations, existence and uniqueness of solutions, strong and weak solutions, Markov property, infinitesimal generator, probabilistic representation of solutions to certain linear partial differential equations; the filtering problem, Kalman-Bucy filter.

name: Functional Analysis
description: 1. Normed linear spaces and Banach spaces. Bounded linear operators. Dual of a normed linear space. Hahn-Banach theorem, uniform boundedness principle, open mapping theorem, closed graph theorem. Computing the dual of some well known Banach spaces.

name: Algebraic Topology
description: Quotient topology, Identification spaces; The fundamental group: Homotopy of maps, multiplication of paths, the fundamental group, induced homomorphisms, the fundamental group of the circle, covering spaces, lifting theorems, the universal covering space, Seifert-Van Kampen theorem, applications; Simplicial Complexes, Simplicial and Singular homology - Definitions, Properties and Applications.

name: Algebra-II
description: Examples of Fields, Algebraic and Transcendental Elements, The degree of a field extension, finding the Irreducible Polynomials, Ruler and Compass construction, Adjoining Roots, Finite Fields, Primitive Elements, Function Fields, The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, Symmetric Functions, The Discriminant, Splitting Fields, Isomorphisms of Field Extensions, Fixed Fields, Galois Extensions, The Main Theorem, Cubic Equations, Quartic Equations, Roots of Unity, Kummer Extensions, Quintic Equations; Modules, Free Modules, Identities, Diagonalizing of Integer Matrices, Generators and Relations, Projective modules, Injective modules, Flat modules, Noetherian Rings, Structure theorem for finitely generated modules over a PID, Application to Linear Operators, Polynomial Rings in Several variables.

name: Real Analysis of Several Variables
description: Review of Functions on Euclidean Space: Norm and Inner product, functions and continuity Differentiation: Partial derivatives and continuity, Differentiability, the derivatives, The Chain rule, Gradients and directional derivatives, Inverse and Implicit functions theorem. Higher derivatives and Extrema: Taylor’s theorem, Maxima and Minima, Second derivative test, Constrained Extrema and Lagrange Multipliers. Integration: Basic definitions, Measure zero and content zero, Integrable functions, Fubini’s theorem, Partition of unity, Change of variable. Integration on Chains: Algebraic preliminaries, Fields and forms, Geometric preliminaries, The fundamental theorem of Calculus (Stokes’ theorem).

name: Measure Theory & Probability
description: Basic theory of measure and integration: Sigma algebras, measures, basic properties of measures, construction of measures (sketch), examples (Lebesgue measure on the real line). Measurable functions and integration. Simple functions, properties of measurable functions, (Lebesgue) integration, limit theorems for integration (Fatou’s Lemma, Monotone Convergence Theorem, Dominated Convergence Theorem), comparison with Riemann integrals. Absolute continuity, Radon-Nikodym theorem (statement), functions of bounded variation. Convergence of sequences of measurable functions. Product measures and the Fubini-Tonelli theorems (statements only). Basic theory of probability: Motivating examples from the discrete setting. Probability spaces, events, probability measures, basic properties. Random variables. Distributions functions and properties, densities and mass functions, examples. Expectation. Mean, variance, moments, moment generating functions and properties. Independence and conditioning. Borel-Cantelli Lemmas, conditional expectations and probabilities given sigma algebras, properties. Convergence of random variables. Weak law of large numbers, Strong law of large numbers (statement only), Central limit theorem (statement only).

name: Special Functions
description: Bernoulli, Euler and Stirling numbers, the Gamma function, hyper geometric functions, orthogonal polynomials, differential equations associated with various special functions, Bessel functions, elliptic functions, theta functions, introduction to the theory of Riemann zeta function C(s) - Riemann's mem oir, the functional equations for C(s), asymptotic expansions of special functions, applications of special functions.

name: Applied Statistics
description: Need for statistics; Probability: continuous and discrete probability distributions, joint probability distribution, conditional probability, Bayes theorem; Data collection and analysis: sampling, descriptive statistics, outlier detection, measures of central tendency, central limit theorem, point estimation of parameters, statistical intervals; Hypothesis testing: common assumptions, parametric tests, non-parametric tests, analysis of covariance; Correlation and regression: maximum likelihood estimation, univariate linear regression and correlation, multivariate regression, total least squares and partial least squares approaches; Drawbacks in hypothesis testing using frequentist statistics: introduction to prior and posterior distributions, Bayesian inference.

name: Theory of Partitions
description: Fundamental theorems in q-series and theta functions – q-binomial theorem, Jacobi triple product identity, Euler’s pentagonal number theorem, Ramanujan’s 1Ψ1 summation formula, Quintuple product identity and its special cases. Applications of the elementary series-product identities to partitions - combinatorial techniques in partition theory – through generating functions, and through bijective proofs, Gaussian polynomials and their properties. Congruences for p(n) and the associated partition Statistics - congruences for the partition function p(n) modulo 5, 7, and 11, ranks, cranks – their properties and their generalizations, Restricted partition functions - connections with q-series, modular forms and mock modular forms Rogers-Ramanujan identities - analytic and combinatorial versions, applications in various areas. The Hardy-Ramanujan-Rademacher formula for p(n) – proof and applications Miscellaneous topics in partitions – Macmahon’s partition analysis, plane partitions, recent advances in partition theory.

name: Algebraic Number Theory
description: Motivation via Fermat’s last theorem, Algebraic numbers and integers, Number Fields and Number Rings, algebraic properties of number rings, embeddings in C, additive structure of a number ring, Dedekind Domains, unique factorisation and prime decomposition in Number rings, Galois theory applied to Number Fields, the ideal class group, the unit group

name: Perturbation Methods
description: Asymptotic Approximations: Asymptotic expansions; Regular perturbation problems (including the domain perturbation problems, e.g., and singular perturbation problems; Asymptotic solution of algebraic, transcendental, and differential equations. Matched Asymptotic Expansion: Outer and inner solutions, matching, composite expansion; Boundary layers; Interior layers; Corner layers; Partial differential equations – elliptic problem, parabolic boundary layer. Multiple Scales: Two and three-time scales; Forced motion near resonance; Weakly coupled oscillators; Slowly varying coefficients; Boundary layers; Linear and nonlinear waves and their applications, application to porous media flow. The Wentzel-Kramers-Brillouin (WKB) theory: Turning points – multiple turning points; Wave propagation and energy methods; Wave propagation and slender-body approximations; Ray methods – WKB expansion, surfaces, and wavefronts, Eikonal equation, transport equation, ray equation; Discrete WKB method, application to potential barrier problems. Applications: There will be significant emphasis on highlighting applications of these topics in fluid mechanics, electrodynamics, quantum mechanics. Some of these examples can also be discussed at the discretion of the instructor, if time permits.

name: Introduction to Riemannian Geometry
description: Introduction to manifolds: Locally Euclidean spaces, Topological manifolds, Differential manifolds, differentiable functions and mappings; Tangent spaces: Tangent space, Vector fields, Lie groups, immersions and embeddings, action of Lie groups on Manifolds, One-Parameter and Local One-Parameter Groups Acting on a Manifold, One-Parameter Subgroups of Lie Groups, the Lie Algebra of Vector Fields on a Manifold, (if time permits, the existence theorem for ordinary differential equations); Differential forms: Differential forms on manifolds, Tensors, Integration on manifolds, Stokes theorem, Orientation and volume forms; Riemannian Manifolds: Riemannian metrics, Levi-Cevita connection, differentiation on Riemannian manifolds, minimizing properties of Geodesics, Curvature, Gauss-Bonnet theorem, Tangent Bundle and Exponential Mapping; Applications: Computer imaging, Statistics and data analysis, General relativity

name: Numerical Analysis
description: Interpolation: Interpolation Formulas of Lagrange and Newton, Error in Polynomial Interpolation, Hermite Interpolation, Interpolation by Spline Functions, Cubic Spline and B-Splines ; System of Linear Equations: Gaussian Elimination, Gauss-Jordan Algorithm, The Cholesky Decomposition, Q-R Decomposition, Least Square Approximations, Iterative Methods and Convergence Theorems; Nonlinear Equations in R^n: Derivatives and Other Basic Concepts, Convex Functionals, Contractions, Inverse and Implicit Function Theorems, Newton’s Methods and Its Variations and Minimization Methods ; Differential Equations - Initial and Boundary Value Problems: One Step Methods with Convergence, Multi Step Methods with Convergence, Simple and Multiple Shooting Methods, Introduction of Numerical Solutions to PDEs.

name: Matrix Lie Groups
description: Definition of a matrix Lie group and its examples; Topological properties; connectedness, compactness, and simply connectedness of certain matrix Lie groups; Lie algebra of a matrix Lie group, exponential mapping, complexification of a Lie algebra; The Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff formula, the connection between Lie group homomorphism and Lie algebra homomorphism; Basic representation theory, direct sum and tensor product of representations, Dual representation, Complete reducibility, Hopf algebra of matrix elements of finite dimensional representations; The representation of the Lie algebra su(2); Semisimple Lie algebra, Roots and Root spaces, Weyl Group, Root system; Highest weight Representation of a semisimple Lie algebra, Construction of representations through Verma module; Classification of simple Lie algebras, a glimpse into Kac-Moody algebras. Applications of matrix Lie groups, for example, SU(2), SU(3), SO(3), SO(3,1), to physics.

name: Matrix Analysis
description: Review: Spectral theorem, singular value decomposition, Moore-Penrose inverse, least squares solutions. Variational principles of spectrum: The Minmax principle for spectrum of Hermitian matrices, Rayleigh–Ritz theorem, Courant–Fischer theorem, Weyl’s monotonicity theorem, Cauchy interlacing theorem, inertia and congruence, Sylvester’s law of inertia. Matrix norms: Definition and examples of matrix norms, Gelfand formula, location of eigenvalues, Greschogorin theorem. Applications to sensitivity of linear systems. Positive semidefinite and Positive definite matrices: Properties and characterizations, the Loewner partial order relation, operator monotone and operator convex functions, Hadamard’s inequality, Fischer’s inequality. Applications to dynamical systems. Perron–Frobenius theory: Matrices with positive and non-negative entries. Applications to Page rank algorithm and steady state of a stationary finite Markov chain.

name: Integral Geometry in Imaging Sciences
description: Introduction to integral transforms: A basic model for tomography, Tomography, Beer’s law, and X-ray tomography.  The Radon transform: The Radon transform, Inversion of the Radon transform, the Central slice theorem, the Radon Inversion Formula, Backprojection, Filtered Backprojection, an alternate formula for the Radon inverse, the Hilbert transform, mapping properties of the Hilbert transform, Approximate inverses for the Radon transform, the range of the Radon transform, data with bounded support, Continuity of the Radon transform and it’s inverse, estimates for the inverse transform, the higher dimensional Radon transform.  X-ray transform: Definition and elementary properties of X-ray transform, Inversion formulas, the interplay between X-ray transform, Fourier transform, and convolution, Uniqueness, the ranges, stability estimates.  The Doppler transform and its generalizations: Definition and some properties of Doppler transform, kernel description and injectivity modulo the natural kernel, invertibility, and discussion of the range of the Doppler transform.  Incomplete data and support theorem: Support theorems for the Radon and X-ray transform. The limited angle problem, the exterior problem, the interior problem, and the restricted source problem.  Current state of the art: A brief survey of generalization of these transforms to tensor fields and some ongoing research problems.

name: Nonlinear Functional Analysis
description: Calculus in Banach spaces, Inverse and Implicit function theorem.

name: Fourier Analysis on LCA Groups
description: Topological groups: Examples, Some topological properties; Locally compact groups, Haar measure, Modular Function; The dual group, Fourier Transform, The C*-algebra of a Locally compact abelian group(LCA-group); The Pontryagin Duality, The Plancherel Theorem; The structure theorems of LCA groups.

name: Numerical Analysis for Partial Differential Equations
description: Heat equation, Convection-Diffusion Equation, Navier-Stokes equation, classification of PDEs; Basics of finite difference, finite difference approximation of Laplacian in two-dimension, stability and convergence of the finite difference approximation of the Poisson problem, higher-order discretisation; Weak derivatives and distributions, Lebesque spaces and Sobolev spaces, Sobolev spaces with non-integer and negative exponents, Gaussian theorem, Sobolev imbedding theorems; Riesz theorem, Lax-Milgram lemma, weak formulation, Ritz and Galerkin method, finite element spaces, finite element on simplices and quadrilaterals, interpolation in Sobolev spaces, general convergence theorem, error estimates; Mesh handling, numerical integration, sparse matrix storage, assembling of matrices and vectors, inclusion of boundary conditions, solution of algebraic system.

name: Advanced Numerical Analysis for Partial Differential Equations
description: Basics of variational formulation, review of finite element method for elliptic problems; Existence & Uniqueness of saddle point problems, continuous and discrete inf-sup stability condition, examples of finite element spaces violating and satisfying the discrete inf-sup condition, application to Stokes problem; Hyperbolic & Parabolic Problems, notion of stability, time-discretisation using theta-schemes and strong stability preserving Runge-Kutta schemes, space-time FEM, method of lumped mass, FEM formulation for wave equation; Estimator strategies including residual-based, hierarchical, and duality-based, marking strategies, adaptive-mesh refinement; Basic concepts of neurons and neural networks, supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms, deep neural networks with application to differential equations, physics-informed neural networks.

name: Commutative Algebra II
description: Integral extensions: Lying over, going up and going down theorems of Cohen-Seidenberg, Noether normalisation and its application to weak and strong nullstellensatz, Serre’s normality criterion, finiteness of rings of integers; Dedekind domains: Valuation rings, fractional ideals, Dedekind domains, divisor class groups of rings of integers; Completions of local rings: I-adic topologies and completions, inverse limits, graded rings and modules, Artin-Rees Lemma, Krull intersection theorem, flatness of completions; Dimension theory: Dimension of finitely generated ring extensions, dimension inequality, dimension and integral extensions, flat extensions and dimension, Hilbert-Serre theorem, dimension theorem for local rings and graded rings, applications to face rings of simplicial complexes; Cohen-Macaulay local rings: Depth and dimension, depth lemma, Cohen-Macaulay modules and rings, catenary property of Cohen-Macaulay rings, examples of Cohen-Macaulay rings in invariant theory, determinantal rings and face rings of simplicial complexes.

name: Modular Forms
description: Elliptic functions, The action of the modular group, the space of modular forms, Eisenstein series, cusp forms, congruence subgroups, Hecke operators, Hecke eigenforms, newforms, theta functions, applications in number theory

name: Statics and Dynamics
description: Static equilibrium, free body diagrams; Structures, method of joints, method of sections; Centroid, area moment of inertia, parallel axis theorem; Kinematics and (Newtonian) dynamics of a particle and system of particles; Two- and three-dimensional rigid-body kinematics and dynamics; motion relative to a translating and rotating frame of reference; Work-energy principle, impulse-momentum principle; angular momentum, gyrodynamics, Euler equations, Euler- angles; Analytical mechanics, Euler-Lagrange’s equations; Introduction to multi body dynamics through MSC Adams (or an equivalent multi-body-dynamics software);Measurement techniques, error analysis, calibration, structured and unstructured experiments

name: Fluid Dynamics
description: Introduction, history, and applications of fluid dynamics: Properties: continuum approximation, Knudsen number, density, specific gravity, pressure, vapor pressure, compressibility, speed of sound, viscosity, surface tension; Fluid kinematics: Lagrangian vs Eulerian descriptions, flow patterns and visualization, linear and volumetric strains, shear strain, and vorticity; Reynolds transport theorem, integral and differential forms of conservation of mass, momentum (linear and angular), and energy; Navier-Stokes equations and exact solutions; Bernoulli’s equations, applications and its relation to conservation of energy; Dimensional analysis and modeling: Dimensional analysis and similarity, method of repeating variables and the Buckingham Pi theorem, experimental testing and modeling; Reynolds number and qualitative description of different flow regimes: laminar vs turbulent flow, creeping flow, inviscid flow, irrotational flow, and boundary layers; Internal flow: Boundary layer development, laminar and turbulent flow in pipes, major and minor losses, piping networks and pump rating; External flows: concept of drag and lift, flow separation, friction and pressure drag, drag coefficients; Introduction to compressible flows: Definition of compressible flow, Mach number and flow regimes, Quasi-1D isentropic flows, area-velocity relation, flow in nozzles and diffusers, choking, qualitative description of shock and expansion waves; Solution of flow problems using CFD softwares. Laboratory: The laboratory component will complement the theory component of the course. The students will learn to do the following measurements and visualizations: pressure measurement; velocity and flow rate measurement; flow visualization The acquired skills and techniques will in turn be used to critically examine and understand the concepts covered in the theory component of the course

name: Vibrations
description: Introduction to vibration theory, applications and relevance. Governing equations, equilibrium points and linearization about an equilibrium point. Free analysis of undamped and damped systems with viscous, Coulomb and hysteretic damping. Forced response due to arbitrary excitations (Duhamel/convolution integral) and time-periodic excitations, modal analysis, decoupling of EOMs of multi-degree of freedom systems, proportionally and classically damped systems. Vibration isolation systems, tuned mass absorbers/dampers; Introduction to vibration testing and sensors

name: Principles of Manufacturing Processes
description: Introduction to Manufacturing - Historical perspective; Importance of manufacturing; Classification of manufacturing processes; Introduction to Engineering Materials- Fundamentals of the mechanical behavior of materials; Structure and manufacturing properties of metals, polymers, ceramics, glass and composites; Principles of heat treating; annealing, normalizing, hardening and tempering; Casting Processes-Basics of casting; Analysis of melting, pouring and solidification phenomena; Design of pattern, core, feeder, and gating system; Deformation Processes- Mechanics of Forming Processes; Basic concepts of plastic deformation; Hot & cold working; Common bulk deformation processes; Common sheet metal forming processes; Joining Processes- Fundamentals & classification of Joining processes, Principles of solid phase and fusion welding, Principles of solid/liquid state joining (brazing and soldering, adhesive bonding); Subtractive Manufacturing- Mechanism and mechanics chip formation; Tools - geometry, materials, Common machining operations (turning, milling, drilling, etc); Material removal rates and surface finish; Economics of machining; Abrasive machining operations; Additive Manufacturing- Principles of rapid prototyping and digital manufacturing; Layer wise manufacturing, liquid-based processes, powder-based processes, deposition-based processes, economic considerations; Manufacturing of Polymers- Principles of extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression, and transfer molding; Non-Traditional Manufacturing Processes- Energy beam (lasers, electron beam, ion beam) based processes, Electric discharge Machining, Electrochemical machining, ultrasonic machining and abrasive jet machining.

name: Mechanics of Deformable Bodies
description: Stress at a point, equilibrium equations, 3-D state of stress, principal stresses and maximum shear stress, stress invariants; Strain at a point, strain-displacement relations in Cartesian coordinates, compatibility equations; stress-strain relations; Yield criteria: von Mises (maximum distortion energy) and Tresca (maximum shear stress); Stresses beyond yielding (for elastic perfectly plastic materials) in axial members, bending and torsion. Un-symmetrical bending, shear flow and shear center; Symmetric bending of curved bars; Torsion of non-circular shafts; Formulation of elasticity problem, boundary conditions, plane stress and plane strain problems; 2-D problems in polar cylindrical coordinates, equilibrium equations and strain-displacement relations, thick cylinders, composite tubes, rotating discs; Airy’s stress function, solution by polynomials, end effects – St. venant principle; Stress distribution around a circular hole, stress concentration; Fracture mechanics concepts; Fatigue failure.

name: Synthesis and Analysis of Mechanisms
description: Introduction to Mechanisms. Classification of Links and Joints. Kinematic Drawing of Mechanisms. Mobility. Grashof condition for Fourbar linkages. Kinematic (Position, Velocity and Acceleration) Analysis and Synthesis of Mechanical Linkages. Cam Follower Mechanisms. CAM Design. Gears and Gear Trains. Belts, Chains and Sprockets. Static and Dynamic Analysis of Mechanisms. Lab Work: A few structured experiments on four bar linkages, QR mechanism, CAMs and Gears (plus new experiments) and computer modelling in support of the theory and perform group mechanism design projects targeted at Mechanism Design Contests.

name: Manufacturing Processes and Systems
description: Fundamentals of Machine Tools: General-purpose, semi-automatic and Automatic machine tools, Set- ups nd operations such as lathe, drilling, milling, grinding, broaching; Machining processes for production: Gear cutting (Hobbing and Shaping), Thread cutting; Finishing operations: honing, lapping, electroplating, painting etc. Metal forming machines and processes. Introduction to Jigs and Fixture Design. Principles of location and clamping. Basics of Computer Numerical Control Technology. Computer Integrated Manufacturing Engineering (CIME). Non-conventional Machining Processes: Electric discharge Machining (EDM), Electrochemical Machining, Laser and Abrasive Flow Machining, etc.Dimensional Metrology: Limits, Fits and dimensional tolerancing; Design of limit gauges, Taylor’s principle, Gage tolerancing; Geometrical tolerancing of form, orientation, position, location, run-out; Basic definitions and measurement principles, MMC/RFS conditions. Comparators and Metrological Instruments: Principles of optical, pneumatic, electric/electronic instruments; Inspection of gears and screw threads; Surface finish and its measurement, Coordinate Dimensional metrology. Non-contact vision based inspection systems. Introduction to assembly engineering.

name: Industrial Engineering and Operations Research
description: Introduction to Industrial Engineering, Work-time study and productivity; Capacity planning; Location and layout models for factories and warehouses; Manufacturing planning concepts based on forecasting, push and pull models of planning, and aggregate planning; Basic inventory models; Introduction to concepts of operations research and optimization; Linear Programming: problem formulation, simplex method, concept of primal-dual, duality and sensitivity analysis, Interior point methods Network flow model & methods including transportation and assignment models, min-cost flow, shortest path problem; Integer programming models, branch and bound method; Introduction to applied probability models for decision making; Random variables and their distributions; Independence and conditional probabilities/expectations; Expectations, variances and probabilistic notions of performance.

name: Mechanics of Materials
description: Strain at a point, strain-displacement relations in Cartesian coordinates, compatibility equations, stress-strain relations; Stress at a point, equilibrium equations, 3-D state of stress, principal stresses and maximum shear stress, stress invariants; Yield criteria: von Mises (maximum distortion energy) and Tresca (maximum shear stress); Unsymmetrical bending, shear flow and shear center; Torsion of non-circular shafts; Formulation of elasticity problem, boundary conditions, plane stress and plane strain problems; 2-D problems in cylindrical coordinates, equilibrium equations and strain-displacement relations, thick cylinders, Airy’s stress function, and solution by polynomials; Stress distribution around a circular hole, stress concentration.

name: Heat and Mass Transfer
description: Basic concepts of heat transfer: modes and laws of heat transfer, conduction, Fouriers law, heat diffusion equation and its solutions, concept of thermal resistance, composite problems, conduction with heat generation, heat transfer through extended surfaces, transient heat conduction, lumped capacitance, solutions of transient conduction through semi-infinite body, convection heat transfer, laminar flow over a flat plate, thermal boundary layer, heat transfer coefficient, internal flow convection, natural convection, boiling and condensation, heat exchangers, LMTD, NTU method, thermal radiation, emissive power, view factor analysis and blackbody exchange, gray body exchange, multi-mode heat transfer and solar, mass transfer, diffusion, interphase mass transfer, local and overall mass transfer coefficients, correlations.

name: Synthesis and Analysis of Mechanisms
description: Introduction: Kinematic chains, Mechanisms, and Structures; Links and Joints; Mobility; Kinematic Diagrams and Inversion; Examples; Kinematics analysis: Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration Analysis of Planar Mechanisms; Graphical linkage synthesis: Synthesis for Motion, Function and Path Generation; Analytical linkage synthesis: Synthesis for Motion, Function and Path Generation; Dynamics analysis: Static and Dynamic Force Analysis of Mechanisms; Balancing: Static and Dynamic Balances of Linkages; Power Transmission Devices: Introduction to Cams, Gears, Gear Trains, Belts, Chains, and Sprockets.

name: Mechanical Systems Design
